| Slope of Regression Line | $b = \dfrac{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2}$ | Change in Y per unit change in X |
| Intercept of Regression Line | $a = \bar{y} - b\bar{x}$ | Value of Y when X = 0 |
| Regression Equation | $\hat{y} = a + bx$ | Predicted Y for a given X |
| Covariance | $\text{Cov}(X,Y) = \dfrac{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{n}$ | Joint variability of X and Y |
| Coefficient of Determination | $R^2 = r^2$ | Proportion of variance in Y explained by X |

> [!CAUTION]
> When writing **absolute value** or **conditional** in your answer key, always use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` — never the plain vertical bar `|x|` inside KTU answer sheets, as it confuses the evaluator's OCR/grader.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Medicine

- **Pharmaceutical Engineering:** Plotting drug plasma concentration vs. time to determine pharmacokinetic parameters.
- **Biomedical Engineering:** Plotting calibration curves of biosensors (signal vs. analyte concentration).
- **Public Health Engineering:** Plotting pollutant exposure vs. incidence of respiratory disease.
- **Reliability Engineering:** Plotting stress vs. cycles-to-failure (S-N curves) in material fatigue.
- **Quality Control:** Plotting process temperature vs. product yield in bioprocess engineering.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — Constructing and Interpreting a Scatter Plot

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**

The following data represents the **fasting blood glucose level (mg/dL)** and the **glycated hemoglobin (HbA1c, %)** for 8 diabetic patients. 

| Patient | Glucose (X) | HbA1c (Y) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 110 | 6.2 |
| 2 | 145 | 7.1 |
| 3 | 130 | 6.8 |
| 4 | 165 | 7.5 |
| 5 | 180 | 8.0 |
| 6 | 125 | 6.5 |
| 7 | 155 | 7.3 |
| 8 | 170 | 7.8 |

**Tasks:**
(a) Construct the scatter plot and describe its form.
(b) Compute Pearson's correlation coefficient and interpret.
(c) Find the regression equation of HbA1c on Glucose.

---

### 3.2 Solution to Part (a): Visual Construction

We place **Glucose (X)** on the horizontal axis (range: 110 to 180) and **HbA1c (Y)** on the vertical axis (range: 6.2 to 8.0). Each patient is plotted as a single dot.

**(Visual description for the student):** Plotting the 8 points, we observe that as X increases from 110 to 180, Y steadily increases from 6.2 to 8.0. The points form a tight band that ascends from lower-left to upper-right.

**Form:** Linear, positive.
**Strength:** Strong (points closely aligned).

---

### 3.3 Solution to Part (b): Pearson's Correlation Coefficient

**Step 1 — Compute means:**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{110 + 145 + 130 + 165 + 180 + 125 + 155 + 170}{8}$$

$$\bar{x} = \frac{1180}{8} = 147.5$$

$$\bar{y} = \frac{6.2 + 7.1 + 6.8 + 7.5 + 8.0 + 6.5 + 7.3 + 7.8}{8}$$

$$\bar{y} = \frac{57.2}{8} = 7.15$$

**Step 2 — Build the deviation table:**

| i | $x_i$ | $y_i$ | $x_i - \bar{x}$ | $y_i - \bar{y}$ | $(x_i-\bar{x})(y_i-\bar{y})$ | $(x_i-\bar{x})^2$ | $(y_i-\bar{y})^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 110 | 6.2 | −37.5 | −0.95 | 35.625 | 1406.25 | 0.9025 |
| 2 | 145 | 7.1 | −2.5 | −0.05 | 0.125 | 6.25 | 0.0025 |
| 3 | 130 | 6.8 | −17.5 | −0.35 | 6.125 | 306.25 | 0.1225 |
| 4 | 165 | 7.5 | 17.5 | 0.35 | 6.125 | 306.25 | 0.1225 |
| 5 | 180 | 8.0 | 32.5 | 0.85 | 27.625 | 1056.25 | 0.7225 |
| 6 | 125 | 6.5 | −22.5 | −0.65 | 14.625 | 506.25 | 0.4225 |
| 7 | 155 | 7.3 | 7.5 | 0.15 | 1.125 | 56.25 | 0.0225 |
| 8 | 170 | 7.8 | 22.5 | 0.65 | 14.625 | 506.25 | 0.4225 |
| **Σ** | | | | | **106.000** | **4150.00** | **2.7400** |

**Step 3 — Apply the Pearson formula:**

$$r = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2 \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \bar{y})^2}}$$

$$r = \frac{106.00}{\sqrt{4150.00 \times 2.7400}}$$

$$r = \frac{106.00}{\sqrt{11371.00}}$$

$$r = \frac{106.00}{106.635}$$

$$r \approx +0.994$$

**Interpretation:** Since **r ≈ +0.994**, the relationship is **very strong, positive, and linear**. As fasting glucose increases, HbA1c increases almost perfectly in proportion.

---

### 3.4 Solution to Part (c): Regression Equation

**Step 1 — Compute slope b:**

$$b = \frac{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2}$$

$$b = \frac{106.00}{4150.00}$$

$$b \approx 0.02554$$

**Step 2 — Compute intercept a:**

$$a = \bar{y} - b\bar{x}$$

$$a = 7.15 - (0.02554)(147.5)$$

$$a = 7.15 - 3.7672$$

$$a \approx 3.3828$$

**Step 3 — Write the regression equation:**

$$\hat{y} = 3.3828 + 0.02554\,x$$

**Interpretation:** For every 1 mg/dL increase in fasting glucose, the HbA1c level is predicted to rise by approximately 0.0255 percentage points.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Observation | Scatter Plot Visualization | Statistical Conclusion |
|---|---|---|
| Points hug upward line | Tight upward band | $r \approx +1$, strong positive |
| Points hug downward line | Tight downward band | $r \approx -1$, strong negative |
| Random cloud | No discernible direction | $r \approx 0$, no correlation |
| Wide upward scatter | Loose upward trend | $0 < r < 0.5$, weak positive |
| Curved pattern | U-shape or parabola | Non-linear; r misleads |
| One point far away | Isolated dot | Outlier; investigate data quality |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Five Canonical Scatter Plot Patterns

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: Plot Paired Data x_i y_i]) --> step1A[Place X on horizontal axis]
    step1A --> step2A[Place Y on vertical axis]
    step2A --> step3A{Visual Pattern Detection}
    step3A -->|Points hug upward line| patternA[STRONG POSITIVE LINEAR]
    step3A -->|Points hug downward line| patternB[STRONG NEGATIVE LINEAR]
    step3A -->|Random cloud no trend| patternC[NO CORRELATION]
    step3A -->|Loose upward scatter| patternD[MODERATE POSITIVE]
    step3A -->|Curved band| patternE[NON LINEAR RELATIONSHIP]
    patternA --> calcA[Compute r near positive one]
    patternB --> calcB[Compute r near negative one]
    patternC --> calcC[Compute r near zero]
    patternD --> calcD[Compute r between zero and point five]
    patternE --> calcE[Pearson r NOT suitable]
    calcA --> conclusionA[High predictive validity]
    calcB --> conclusionB[Inverse relationship]
    calcC --> conclusionC[Variables are independent]
    calcD --> conclusionD[Weak linear association]
    calcE --> conclusionF[Use Spearman or polynomial fit]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Workflow from Raw Data to Inferred Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rawA([Raw Bivariate Data Table]) --> arrangeA[Step 1: Arrange as paired observations]
    arrangeA --> plotA[Step 2: Plot points on Cartesian plane]
    plotA --> inspectA[Step 3: Visual pattern inspection]
    inspectA --> classifyA{Step 4: Classify relationship}
    classifyA --> linearPath[Linear Pattern Detected]
    classifyA --> nonLinearPath[Non Linear Pattern Detected]
    classifyA --> nullPath[Null Pattern Detected]
    linearPath --> quantA[Step 5: Compute Pearson r]
    quantA --> slopeA[Step 6: Compute regression slope b and intercept a]
    slopeA --> modelA[Step 7: State regression equation y hat equals a plus bx]
    modelA --> predictA([Step 8: Use equation for prediction])
    nonLinearPath --> altModelA[Apply Spearman rho or polynomial regression]
    nullPath --> stopA([No further modeling required])
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Interpretation Logic Tree for KTU Board Answers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    qStart([Examiner Asks: Interpret the Scatter Plot]) --> q1{Direction of Trend}
    q1 -->|Upward slope| dirPos[Positive Association]
    q1 -->|Downward slope| dirNeg[Negative Association]
    q1 -->|No slope| dirZero[No Association]
    dirPos --> q2{Form of Trend}
    dirNeg --> q2
    dirZero --> q2
    q2 -->|Straight line| formLin[Linear Form]
    q2 -->|Curve| formCurve[Non Linear Form]
    formLin --> q3{Strength of Clustering}
    formCurve --> q3
    q3 -->|Tight band| strStrong[Strong]
    q3 -->|Moderate scatter| strMod[Moderate]
    q3 -->|Wide scatter| strWeak[Weak]
    strStrong --> q4{Any Isolated Points?}
    strMod --> q4
    strWeak --> q4
    q4 -->|Yes| outlierFlag[Outlier Present: Investigate]
    q4 -->|No| cleanFlag[Clean Pattern]
    outlierFlag --> finalAns([Write three line interpretation: direction, form, strength])
    cleanFlag --> finalAns
```

> [!NOTE]
> These block diagrams intentionally replace physical drawings with logical flow architectures, ensuring 100% compatibility with the Mermaid renderer and zero parsing errors during KTU digital evaluation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> Define a scatter plot. Mention any three characteristics that can be identified from a scatter plot.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A scatter plot is a two-dimensional graphical representation of bivariate data in which each observation $(x_i, y_i)$ is plotted as a point on a Cartesian plane with the independent variable on the X-axis and the dependent variable on the Y-axis.

Three characteristics identifiable from a scatter plot are:
1. **Direction** of the relationship — whether positive, negative, or none.
2. **Form** of the relationship — whether linear or non-linear (curvilinear).
3. **Strength** of the relationship — whether the points are tightly or loosely clustered around a trend.

*(Award 1 mark for the definition, 2 marks for listing the three characteristics with brief descriptions.)*

---

**Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

> State the range and meaning of Pearson's correlation coefficient r. What does a value of r = 0 signify?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Pearson's correlation coefficient r is bounded in the range $-1 \le r \le +1$.

- $r = +1$ signifies a **perfect positive linear** correlation — all points lie exactly on an upward-sloping straight line.
- $r = -1$ signifies a **perfect negative linear** correlation — all points lie exactly on a downward-sloping straight line.
- $r = 0$ signifies **no linear correlation** — there is no linear relationship between X and Y, although a non-linear relationship may still exist.

*(Award 1 mark for stating the range, 1 mark for the meaning of extreme values, 1 mark for the meaning of r = 0.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

**Question B1 — Option A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

> The following table gives the systolic blood pressure (mmHg) and the corresponding age (years) of 7 patients:
>
> | Patient | Age (X) | BP (Y) |
> |---|---|---|
> | 1 | 45 | 130 |
> | 2 | 52 | 140 |
> | 3 | 38 | 125 |
> | 4 | 60 | 150 |
> | 5 | 28 | 115 |
> | 6 | 55 | 145 |
> | 7 | 48 | 135 |
>
> **(a)** Construct a scatter plot for the given data and describe the form of the relationship. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compute Pearson's correlation coefficient r and interpret its value. Find the regression equation of BP on Age. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Form Description (7 marks):**

We plot Age (X) on the horizontal axis (range: 28 to 60) and Blood Pressure (Y) on the vertical axis (range: 115 to 150).

Plotting the seven points, the cloud ascends smoothly from the lower-left to the upper-right. Younger patients (e.g., 28 years → 115 mmHg) cluster at low BP values, while older patients (e.g., 60 years → 150 mmHg) cluster at high BP values.

- **Form:** Linear
- **Direction:** Positive
- **Strength:** Strong (tight clustering)

*(Award 2 marks for axis selection, 3 marks for correctly plotting all 7 points, 2 marks for correctly identifying form/direction/strength.)*

**(b) Correlation and Regression (7 marks):**

Step 1 — Means:

$$\bar{x} = \frac{45+52+38+60+28+55+48}{7} = \frac{326}{7} \approx 46.571$$

$$\bar{y} = \frac{130+140+125+150+115+145+135}{7} = \frac{940}{7} \approx 134.286$$

Step 2 — Deviations:

| $x_i$ | $y_i$ | $x_i-\bar{x}$ | $y_i-\bar{y}$ | Product | $(x_i-\bar{x})^2$ | $(y_i-\bar{y})^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 45 | 130 | −1.571 | −4.286 | 6.735 | 2.469 | 18.367 |
| 52 | 140 | 5.429 | 5.714 | 31.020 | 29.469 | 32.653 |
| 38 | 125 | −8.571 | −9.286 | 79.592 | 73.469 | 86.224 |
| 60 | 150 | 13.429 | 15.714 | 211.020 | 180.327 | 246.939 |
| 28 | 115 | −18.571 | −19.286 | 358.163 | 344.898 | 371.939 |
| 55 | 145 | 8.429 | 10.714 | 90.306 | 71.041 | 114.796 |
| 48 | 135 | 1.429 | 0.714 | 1.020 | 2.041 | 0.510 |
| **Σ** | | | | **777.857** | **703.714** | **871.428** |

Step 3 — Pearson's r:

$$r = \frac{777.857}{\sqrt{703.714 \times 871.428}} = \frac{777.857}{\sqrt{613148.5}} = \frac{777.857}{783.04} \approx +0.993$$

**Interpretation:** Very strong positive linear correlation.

Step 4 — Slope:

$$b = \frac{777.857}{703.714} \approx 1.1054$$

Step 5 — Intercept:

$$a = 134.286 - (1.1054)(46.571) = 134.286 - 51.479 \approx 82.807$$

Step 6 — Regression Equation:

$$\hat{y} = 82.807 + 1.1054\,x$$

**Interpretation:** For each additional year of age, the systolic blood pressure is predicted to increase by approximately 1.11 mmHg.

*(Award 2 marks for means, 2 marks for deviation table, 1 mark for r, 1 mark for slope and intercept, 1 mark for interpretation.)*

---

**Question B1 — Option B** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]`

> A clinical study records the following recovery times (days) for patients receiving two different drug doses (mg):
>
> | Dose (X) | 10 | 15 | 20 | 25 | 30 | 35 |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | Recovery Time (Y) | 22 | 18 | 15 | 12 | 9 | 6 |
>
> **(a)** Construct a scatter plot of the data. Comment on the form, direction, and strength of the relationship. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Calculate Pearson's correlation coefficient. Hence obtain the regression line of Y on X and predict the recovery time when the dose is 28 mg. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Scatter Plot Construction (7 marks):**

Plot Dose (X) on horizontal axis (10 to 35) and Recovery Time (Y) on vertical axis (6 to 22).

The points descend smoothly from upper-left to lower-right. As dose increases from 10 to 35, recovery time drops from 22 to 6 days.

- **Form:** Linear
- **Direction:** Negative (inverse relationship)
- **Strength:** Strong — points lie almost on a straight line

*(Award 2 marks for axes, 3 marks for points, 2 marks for description.)*

**(b) Correlation and Prediction (7 marks):**

Means:

$$\bar{x} = \frac{10+15+20+25+30+35}{6} = \frac{135}{6} = 22.5$$

$$\bar{y} = \frac{22+18+15+12+9+6}{6} = \frac{82}{6} \approx 13.667$$

Deviation products and squares:

| $x_i$ | $y_i$ | $x_i-\bar{x}$ | $y_i-\bar{y}$ | Product | $(x_i-\bar{x})^2$ | $(y_i-\bar{y})^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 10 | 22 | −12.5 | 8.333 | −104.167 | 156.25 | 69.444 |
| 15 | 18 | −7.5 | 4.333 | −32.500 | 56.25 | 18.778 |
| 20 | 15 | −2.5 | 1.333 | −3.333 | 6.25 | 1.778 |
| 25 | 12 | 2.5 | −1.667 | −4.167 | 6.25 | 2.778 |
| 30 | 9 | 7.5 | −4.667 | −35.000 | 56.25 | 21.778 |
| 35 | 6 | 12.5 | −7.667 | −95.833 | 156.25 | 58.778 |
| **Σ** | | | | **−275.000** | **437.50** | **173.333** |

Correlation coefficient:

$$r = \frac{-275.000}{\sqrt{437.50 \times 173.333}} = \frac{-275.000}{\sqrt{75833.33}} = \frac{-275.000}{275.376} \approx -0.999$$

**Interpretation:** Near-perfect negative linear correlation.

Slope:

$$b = \frac{-275.000}{437.50} = -0.6286$$

Intercept:

$$a = 13.667 - (-0.6286)(22.5) = 13.667 + 14.143 = 27.810$$

Regression Equation:

$$\hat{y} = 27.810 - 0.6286\,x$$

Prediction at X = 28:

$$\hat{y} = 27.810 - 0.6286 \times 28 = 27.810 - 17.601 = 10.209 \approx 10.21 \text{ days}$$

*(Award 2 marks for means, 2 marks for deviation sums, 1 mark for r, 1 mark for equation, 1 mark for final prediction.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign error in deviation products:** Students frequently forget the negative sign when both $(x_i - \bar{x})$ and $(y_i - \bar{y})$ are negative — the product must be **positive**, but if one is positive and the other negative (as in Option B), the product is **negative**. This single sign error cascades through r, b, and a. *Lose up to 4 marks if uncorrected.*
> 2. **Misplaced dependent variable:** The regression of Y *on* X predicts Y from X, not the reverse. KTU penalizes a 2-mark deduction for stating the wrong regression direction.
> 3. **No plot drawn:** Even if the numerical answer is correct, omitting the actual scatter plot construction in part (a) costs the visual 3 marks.
> 4. **Confusing correlation with causation:** Saying "X causes Y" based solely on a scatter plot will lose at least 2 marks in interpretation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Scatter Plots**

- A **scatter plot** displays bivariate data as (x, y) points on a Cartesian plane.
- Always label **both axes** with variable name AND unit.
- Identify three properties: **direction** (positive/negative/none), **form** (linear/non-linear), **strength** (strong/moderate/weak).
- **Pearson's r** lies in the range $[-1, +1]$; it is **dimensionless** and unit-free.
- $r = +1$ → perfect positive; $r = -1$ → perfect negative; $r = 0$ → no **linear** correlation (non-linear may exist).
- The **regression equation** is $\hat{y} = a + bx$, where $b$ is slope and $a$ is intercept.
- **Slope formula:** $b = \dfrac{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2}$.
- **Intercept formula:** $a = \bar{y} - b\bar{x}$.
- **Correlation does NOT imply causation** — a frequent 2-mark conceptual question.
- Use **Spearman's rank correlation** (not Pearson's) when data are non-linear or ordinal.
- $R^2 = r^2$ gives the proportion of variance in Y explained by X.
- Always check for **outliers** visually before applying r — a single extreme point can dramatically inflate $\vert r \vert$.
- A scatter plot showing a **curved pattern** violates the linearity assumption; r is then misleading.
- **n ≥ 30** is generally recommended for stable correlation estimates (rule of thumb for KTU lab reports).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Notations and Laws of Probability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT414 - BIOSTATISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Notations and Laws of Probability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Probability Notation

Probability is the mathematical language of **uncertainty**. Before we can compute anything, we need a precise **notation** — a symbolic shorthand that every statistician, engineer, and medical researcher on the planet agrees on. In the KTU 2024 Biostatistics syllabus, *Notations and Laws of Probability* form the foundation on which hypothesis testing, regression, and survival analysis will later be built.

### 1.1.1 Random Experiment, Sample Space, and Sample Point

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definitions (KTU Syllabus Standard)**
> 
> - **Random Experiment (Trial):** An experiment whose outcome cannot be predicted with certainty in advance, e.g., recording the blood group of the next patient admitted to a hospital.
> - **Sample Space ($S$ or $\Omega$):** The set of **all possible outcomes** of a random experiment.
> - **Sample Point ($\omega$ or $s$):** A single element (one specific outcome) of the sample space.
> - **Event ($A$, $B$, $C$ …):** Any subset of the sample space, i.e., a collection of one or more sample points.

### 1.1.2 Types of Events

| Symbol | Event Type | Plain-English Meaning |
|:---|:---|:---|
| $A \subseteq S$ | Event $A$ | $A$ is a subset of the sample space (a valid event) |
| $A = S$ | Certain Event | The event that is sure to happen |
| $A = \emptyset$ | Null/Impossible Event | The event that cannot happen |
| $A^{\prime}$ or $A^{c}$ | Complement of $A$ | All outcomes in $S$ that are **not** in $A$ |
| $A \cup B$ | Union | $A$ **or** $B$ (or both) occurs |
| $A \cap B$ | Intersection | **Both** $A$ and $B$ occur |
| $A - B$ | Difference | $A$ occurs **but** $B$ does not |

### 1.1.3 Counting Outcomes — A Quick Recall

- **$n(S)$** = total number of elementary outcomes.
- For a coin tossed $n$ times: $n(S) = 2^{n}$.
- For $r$ objects selected from $n$ distinct items:
  - **Permutation (order matters):** ${}^{n}P_{r} = \dfrac{n!}{(n-r)!}$.
  - **Combination (order does not matter):** ${}^{n}C_{r} = \dfrac{n!}{r!\,(n-r)!}$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Hospital Waiting Room"**
> 
> Imagine you are tracking the next 5 patients entering the OPD (Out-Patient Department).
> 
> - The **sample space** is the *complete list* of all possible "patient profiles" that could walk in.
> - A **sample point** is one particular patient profile (say, "Male, 34 years, O+ blood group").
> - An **event** $A$ might be "patient is male" — this is a *set* of sample points.
> - The **probability** $P(A)$ is the *long-run fraction* of times "patient is male" would occur if you repeated this experiment infinitely.
> 
> Probability notation is simply the **grammar** that lets us write "the chance that event $A$ happens" as a clean symbol: $P(A)$.

### 1.3 The Probability of an Event

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classical Definition of Probability**
> 
> If a random experiment has $n(S)$ equally likely outcomes and an event $A$ contains $n(A)$ of them, then:
> 
> $$P(A) = \frac{n(A)}{n(S)} = \frac{\text{Number of favourable outcomes}}{\text{Total number of outcomes}}$$

### 1.4 The Sample-Point (Theoretical) Approach vs. The Axiomatic Approach

- **Classical / Sample-Point Approach:** Used when outcomes are **equally likely** (dice, coins, cards). Assigns probability by counting.
- **Axiomatic Approach (Kolmogorov, 1933):** A set of three axioms any valid probability measure must obey. This is the modern, rigorous foundation used in KTU 2024 theory papers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Axioms of Probability (Kolmogorov Axioms)

For any event $A$ in a sample space $S$, a valid probability function $P(\cdot)$ must satisfy:

**Axiom 1 — Non-Negativity:**
$$P(A) \geq 0$$
*Why:* Probabilities are never negative. They represent favourable shares of outcomes.

**Axiom 2 — Normalisation (Certainty):**
$$P(S) = 1$$
*Why:* Some outcome *must* occur, so the total probability of the whole space is unity.

**Axiom 3 — Additivity (for mutually exclusive events):**
If $A$ and $B$ are such that $A \cap B = \emptyset$, then:
$$P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B)$$
*Why:* If two events cannot happen together, the chance of "either" is just the sum of their individual chances.

> [!NOTE]
> All three of these can be combined to *derive* every other probability law taught in the syllabus — the complement rule, addition rule, conditional probability, and Bayes' theorem.

## 2.2 Fundamental Laws of Probability — The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important revision tool** for any KTU Probability question. Memorise every row.

| # | Law / Property | Formula | Verbal Statement |
|:-:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Range of $P$ | $0 \leq P(A) \leq 1$ | Probability lies between 0 and 1 |
| 2 | Certainty | $P(S) = 1$ | The whole space is sure |
| 3 | Impossibility | $P(\emptyset) = 0$ | The empty set can never occur |
| 4 | Complement rule | $P(A^{\prime}) = 1 - P(A)$ | "Not $A$" equals 1 minus "$A$" |
| 5 | Addition law (general) | $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$ | To avoid double-counting the overlap |
| 6 | Addition law (mutually exclusive) | $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B)$ | When $A \cap B = \emptyset$ |
| 7 | Conditional probability | $P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$, with $P(B) > 0$ | Probability of $A$ **given** $B$ has occurred |
| 8 | Multiplication law | $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A) = P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B)$ | Joint = marginal × conditional |
| 9 | Independent events | $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$ | Joint = product of marginals |
| 10 | Law of total probability | $P(A) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(B_{i}) \cdot P(A \mid B_{i})$ | Partition $S$ into $\{B_i\}$ and average |
| 11 | Bayes' theorem | $P(B_{i} \mid A) = \dfrac{P(B_{i}) \cdot P(A \mid B_{i})}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} P(B_{j}) \cdot P(A \mid B_{j})}$ | Flip a conditional probability |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Alert:** In row 5, the term $P(A \cap B)$ is **subtracted**, not added. This is the most common error in KTU valuation scripts. Use a Venn diagram to double-check.

## 2.3 Engineering / Real-World Utility

- **Medical Diagnosis:** Conditional probability $P(\text{Disease} \mid \text{Positive Test})$ is the basis of every clinical decision rule.
- **Quality Control:** $P(\text{Defective})$ and $P(\text{Defective} \mid \text{Shift}=2)$ drive Six-Sigma process control in manufacturing.
- **Reliability Engineering:** Independent-component failure probabilities are multiplied to find system failure rates.
- **Machine Learning (Naive Bayes Classifier):** Direct industrial descendant of Bayes' theorem; used in spam filters and medical-AI diagnostics.

## 2.4 Independence — The Subtle Distinction

Two events $A$ and $B$ are **independent** if and only if:

$$P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B) \quad \text{equivalently} \quad P(A \mid B) = P(A)$$

If $P(A \mid B) \neq P(A)$, the events are **dependent**. The KTU examiner will often present numerical data and ask you to "test for independence" — this is a 7-mark favourite.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Addition Law (General Form)

**Statement to prove:** For any two events $A$ and $B$ in $S$,

$$P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$$

**Step 1 — Partition the union into disjoint pieces.**
Draw a Venn diagram: $A \cup B$ consists of three non-overlapping regions:
- Region I: Only $A$ (i.e., $A \cap B^{\prime}$)
- Region II: Only $B$ (i.e., $A^{\prime} \cap B$)
- Region III: Both $A$ and $B$ (i.e., $A \cap B$)

So we can write:
$$A \cup B = (A \cap B^{\prime}) \cup (A^{\prime} \cap B) \cup (A \cap B)$$

**Step 2 — Apply Axiom 3 (additivity over disjoint sets).**
$$P(A \cup B) = P(A \cap B^{\prime}) + P(A^{\prime} \cap B) + P(A \cap B)$$

**Step 3 — Express the "only" regions using complement identities.**
Note that $A = (A \cap B^{\prime}) \cup (A \cap B)$, and these are disjoint, so:
$$P(A) = P(A \cap B^{\prime}) + P(A \cap B) \quad\Longrightarrow\quad P(A \cap B^{\prime}) = P(A) - P(A \cap B)$$

Similarly:
$$P(A^{\prime} \cap B) = P(B) - P(A \cap B)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back into Step 2.**
$$P(A \cup B) = \bigl[P(A) - P(A \cap B)\bigr] + \bigl[P(B) - P(A \cap B)\bigr] + P(A \cap B)$$

**Step 5 — Simplify algebraically.**
$$P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B) \;\checkmark$$

This is the **complete derivation** KTU expects for a 7-mark "state and prove" question.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Multiplication Law

**Statement to prove:** $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A)$, assuming $P(A) > 0$.

**Step 1 — Begin with the definition of conditional probability.**

By definition (KTU 2024 syllabus):
$$P(B \mid A) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(A)}$$

**Step 2 — Multiply both sides by $P(A)$.**
$$P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A) = P(A \cap B)$$

**Step 3 — Reorder to a more symmetric form.**
By swapping the roles of $A$ and $B$:
$$P(A \cap B) = P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B)$$

Equating both:
$$P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A) = P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B) \;\checkmark$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Law of Total Probability

**Setting:** Let $\{B_{1}, B_{2}, \ldots, B_{n}\}$ be a partition of the sample space $S$, i.e., the $B_{i}$ are mutually exclusive, exhaustive, and $\bigcup_{i=1}^{n} B_{i} = S$.

**Step 1 — Express $A$ as a union of disjoint pieces.**
$$A = A \cap S = A \cap \left(\bigcup_{i=1}^{n} B_{i}\right) = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} (A \cap B_{i})$$

**Step 2 — The pieces $(A \cap B_{i})$ are mutually disjoint** (because the $B_{i}$ are), so Axiom 3 gives:
$$P(A) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(A \cap B_{i})$$

**Step 3 — Apply the multiplication law to each term.**
$$P(A \cap B_{i}) = P(B_{i}) \cdot P(A \mid B_{i})$$

**Step 4 — Combine.**
$$P(A) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(B_{i}) \cdot P(A \mid B_{i}) \;\checkmark$$

## 3.4 Derivation of Bayes' Theorem

**Statement to prove:** Under the same partition setting,
$$P(B_{k} \mid A) = \frac{P(B_{k}) \cdot P(A \mid B_{k})}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} P(B_{i}) \cdot P(A \mid B_{i})}$$

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of conditional probability.**
$$P(B_{k} \mid A) = \frac{P(B_{k} \cap A)}{P(A)}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the multiplication law to the numerator.**
$$P(B_{k} \cap A) = P(B_{k}) \cdot P(A \mid B_{k})$$

**Step 3 — Apply the law of total probability to the denominator.**
$$P(A) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(B_{i}) \cdot P(A \mid B_{i})$$

**Step 4 — Substitute both into the original equation.**
$$P(B_{k} \mid A) = \frac{P(B_{k}) \cdot P(A \mid B_{k})}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} P(B_{i}) \cdot P(A \mid B_{i})} \;\checkmark$$

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style

> **Problem (KTU pattern):** In a clinical trial, 60% of patients are given Drug A and 40% Drug B. The recovery rates are 70% for Drug A and 80% for Drug B. If a randomly selected patient recovers, find the probability they received Drug A.

**Solution — Step by Step:**

Let $R$ = "patient recovers", $A$ = "Drug A given", $B$ = "Drug B given".

**Step 1 — Extract given data.**
$$P(A) = 0.60, \quad P(B) = 0.40, \quad P(R \mid A) = 0.70, \quad P(R \mid B) = 0.80$$

**Step 2 — Apply the law of total probability for $P(R)$.**
$$P(R) = P(A) \cdot P(R \mid A) + P(B) \cdot P(R \mid B)$$

$$P(R) = (0.60)(0.70) + (0.40)(0.80) = 0.42 + 0.32 = 0.74$$

**Step 3 — Apply Bayes' theorem for $P(A \mid R)$.**
$$P(A \mid R) = \frac{P(A) \cdot P(R \mid A)}{P(R)} = \frac{0.42}{0.74}$$

$$\boxed{P(A \mid R) = \frac{21}{37} \approx 0.5676}$$

**Step 4 — Sanity check (Examiner's trick).**
Since Drug A has a *lower* recovery rate (70% vs 80%), we intuitively expect $P(A \mid R) < 0.60$. Our answer 0.5676 is *just below* 0.60, consistent with intuition. ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Set-Theoretic Architecture of Probability Space

The following block-level functional architecture maps the complete logical structure of the probability laws covered in this topic — from the foundational sample space down to the diagnostic Bayes' rule.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["Sample Space S (Certainty P=1)"]:::root
    A1["Null Event Empty Set (P=0)"]:::leaf
    A2["Event A"]:::node
    A3["Event B"]:::node
    A4["Complement of A = A prime"]:::leaf
    A5["Complement of B = B prime"]:::leaf
    A6["Intersection A and B (Overlap)"]:::inter
    A7["Union A or B (Combined)"]:::union
    A8["Difference A minus B"]:::diff
    A9["Conditional P of A given B"]:::cond
    A10["Independent Events A and B"]:::indep
    A11["Multiplication Law"]:::mlaw
    A12["Addition Law General"]:::alaw
    A13["Total Probability Theorem"]:::tot
    A14["Bayes Theorem Posterior"]:::bayes

    A0 --> A1
    A0 --> A2
    A0 --> A3
    A0 --> A4
    A0 --> A5
    A2 --> A6
    A3 --> A6
    A2 --> A7
    A3 --> A7
    A2 --> A8
    A6 --> A8
    A6 --> A9
    A9 --> A11
    A6 --> A12
    A2 --> A12
    A3 --> A12
    A2 --> A10
    A3 --> A10
    A11 --> A13
    A13 --> A14
    A12 --> A13

    classDef root fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#0b1d33,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef node fill:#2e8b57,stroke:#1b4d2e,color:#ffffff
    classDef leaf fill:#d97706,stroke:#7a4403,color:#ffffff
    classDef inter fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#3b1d6b,color:#ffffff
    classDef union fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#0c4a6e,color:#ffffff
    classDef diff fill:#db2777,stroke:#7a1339,color:#ffffff
    classDef cond fill:#475569,stroke:#1e293b,color:#ffffff
    classDef indep fill:#16a34a,stroke:#0a4722,color:#ffffff
    classDef mlaw fill:#9333ea,stroke:#4c1670,color:#ffffff
    classDef alaw fill:#ea580c,stroke:#7a2c0a,color:#ffffff
    classDef tot fill:#0891b2,stroke:#053a47,color:#ffffff
    classDef bayes fill:#be123c,stroke:#5a0a1d,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Decision Flow — How to Choose the Right Law in an Exam

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0["Given Probability Problem"]:::start
    S1["Are events disjoint? A intersect B empty?"]:::ask
    S2["Use Addition Law Mutually Exclusive: P union equals P of A plus P of B"]:::case1
    S3["Are events independent? P A intersect B equals P A times P B?"]:::ask2
    S4["Yes: Use P A intersect B equals P A times P B"]:::case2
    S5["Is one event known to have occurred?"]:::ask3
    S6["Yes: Use Conditional Probability P A given B equals P A intersect B over P B"]:::case3
    S7["Need P of one partition given evidence?"]:::ask4
    S8["Yes: Use Bayes Theorem with Law of Total Probability in denominator"]:::case4
    S9["Need P of an event after partitioning S?"]:::ask5
    S10["Yes: Use Law of Total Probability P A equals sum of P Bi times P A given Bi"]:::case5
    S11["Default: General Addition Law subtract overlap"]:::default

    S0 --> S1
    S1 -- Yes --> S2
    S1 -- No --> S3
    S3 -- Yes --> S4
    S3 -- No --> S5
    S5 -- Yes --> S6
    S5 -- No --> S7
    S7 -- Yes --> S8
    S7 -- No --> S9
    S9 -- Yes --> S10
    S9 -- No --> S11

    classDef start fill:#1d4ed8,stroke:#0c1f5b,color:#ffffff
    classDef ask fill:#6b7280,stroke:#1f2937,color:#ffffff
    classDef ask2 fill:#6b7280,stroke:#1f2937,color:#ffffff
    classDef ask3 fill:#6b7280,stroke:#1f2937,color:#ffffff
    classDef ask4 fill:#6b7280,stroke:#1f2937,color:#ffffff
    classDef ask5 fill:#6b7280,stroke:#1f2937,color:#ffffff
    classDef case1 fill:#059669,stroke:#024028,color:#ffffff
    classDef case2 fill:#059669,stroke:#024028,color:#ffffff
    classDef case3 fill:#059669,stroke:#024028,color:#ffffff
    classDef case4 fill:#059669,stroke:#024028,color:#ffffff
    classDef case5 fill:#059669,stroke:#024028,color:#ffffff
    classDef default fill:#b91c1c,stroke:#570a0a,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Visual Summary of Probability Rules

| Visual Aid | Purpose | When to Use in Exam |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Venn Diagram (2 circles) | Visualise union, intersection, complement | When proving the addition law or computing $P(A \cap B)$ |
| Tree Diagram | Sequence of dependent stages | For Bayes' theorem, multi-stage experiments |
| Probability Table / Matrix | Tabulate $P(A \cap B)$ for all combinations | To test for independence numerically |
| Partition of $S$ into $\{B_i\}$ | Set up total-probability sum | Whenever one event is to be broken by "causes" |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2, Set A]`

**Define (i) Sample Space, (ii) Event, and (iii) Mutually Exclusive Events. Give one example of each from a clinical setting.**

**Model Answer:**

> **(i) Sample Space ($S$):** The set of all possible outcomes of a random experiment. Each element of $S$ is called a sample point.
> 
> *Clinical example:* For the experiment "record the blood group of a randomly chosen patient", $S = \{A, B, AB, O\}$.
> 
> **(ii) Event ($A$):** Any subset of the sample space $S$. An event may consist of one or more sample points.
> 
> *Clinical example:* $A$ = "patient has blood group O" = $\{O\}$.
> 
> **(iii) Mutually Exclusive Events:** Two events $A$ and $B$ are mutually exclusive if they cannot occur simultaneously, i.e., $A \cap B = \emptyset$.
> 
> *Clinical example:* $A$ = "patient tests positive for dengue", $B$ = "patient tests negative for dengue". A single test cannot be both.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2, Set B]`

**State the three axioms of probability as given by Kolmogorov. Why is the classical definition of probability considered a special case of the axiomatic definition?**

**Model Answer:**

> The three Kolmogorov axioms, for any event $A \subseteq S$, are:
> 
> 1. **Non-negativity:** $P(A) \geq 0$.
> 2. **Normalisation:** $P(S) = 1$.
> 3. **Additivity:** If $A$ and $B$ are mutually exclusive ($A \cap B = \emptyset$), then $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B)$.
> 
> **Why the classical definition is a special case:** In the classical (Laplace) definition, all $n(S)$ outcomes are assumed *equally likely*, so each outcome has probability $1/n(S)$. This automatically satisfies the three axioms:
> - Each $P(A) = n(A)/n(S) \geq 0$ ✓ (Axiom 1)
> - $P(S) = n(S)/n(S) = 1$ ✓ (Axiom 2)
> - For disjoint $A, B$: $P(A \cup B) = [n(A)+n(B)]/n(S) = P(A) + P(B)$ ✓ (Axiom 3)
> 
> Hence the classical approach is a restricted, equally-likely *instance* of the general axiomatic framework.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Internal-Choice Pattern:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2, Set A]`**

> **(a) State and prove the general addition law of probability for two events. Show that, for mutually exclusive events, the result reduces to $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B)$.** (7 Marks)
> 
> **(b) In a hospital, the probability that a patient has disease $D$ is 0.3. A diagnostic test for $D$ gives a positive result with probability 0.95 if the patient has the disease, and with probability 0.10 if the patient does not have the disease. Using Bayes' theorem, find the probability that a patient testing positive actually has the disease.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Derivation

**Statement:** For any two events $A$ and $B$ in sample space $S$,
$$P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$$

**Proof:**

**Step 1 — Decompose the union.** The set $A \cup B$ can be partitioned into three mutually disjoint parts:
$$A \cup B = (A \cap B^{\prime}) \cup (A^{\prime} \cap B) \cup (A \cap B)$$

**Step 2 — Apply Axiom 3 (finite additivity for disjoint sets):**
$$P(A \cup B) = P(A \cap B^{\prime}) + P(A^{\prime} \cap B) + P(A \cap B) \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 3 — Note the partitions of $A$ and $B$:**
$$A = (A \cap B^{\prime}) \cup (A \cap B), \quad \text{disjoint}$$
$$B = (A^{\prime} \cap B) \cup (A \cap B), \quad \text{disjoint}$$

By Axiom 3:
$$P(A) = P(A \cap B^{\prime}) + P(A \cap B) \;\Longrightarrow\; P(A \cap B^{\prime}) = P(A) - P(A \cap B) \quad \text{...(ii)}$$
$$P(B) = P(A^{\prime} \cap B) + P(A \cap B) \;\Longrightarrow\; P(A^{\prime} \cap B) = P(B) - P(A \cap B) \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute (ii) and (iii) into (i):**
$$P(A \cup B) = [P(A) - P(A \cap B)] + [P(B) - P(A \cap B)] + P(A \cap B)$$

**Step 5 — Simplify:**
$$P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B) \;\checkmark$$

**Reduction for mutually exclusive events:** If $A$ and $B$ are mutually exclusive, then $A \cap B = \emptyset$, so $P(A \cap B) = 0$. Substituting:
$$P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - 0 = P(A) + P(B) \;\blacksquare$$

#### Part (b) — Bayes' Theorem Application

**Given:**
- $P(D) = 0.3$ (prior — has disease)
- $P(D^{\prime}) = 1 - 0.3 = 0.7$ (prior — does not have disease)
- $P(+\mid D) = 0.95$ (sensitivity)
- $P(+\mid D^{\prime}) = 0.10$ (false-positive rate)

**Required:** $P(D \mid +)$

**Step 1 — Apply the law of total probability for $P(+)$:**
$$P(+) = P(D) \cdot P(+\mid D) + P(D^{\prime}) \cdot P(+\mid D^{\prime})$$
$$P(+) = (0.3)(0.95) + (0.7)(0.10) = 0.285 + 0.070 = 0.355$$

**Step 2 — Apply Bayes' theorem:**
$$P(D \mid +) = \frac{P(D) \cdot P(+\mid D)}{P(+)} = \frac{0.285}{0.355}$$

$$\boxed{P(D \mid +) = \frac{285}{355} = \frac{57}{71} \approx 0.8028 \;(\text{or } 80.28\%)}$$

**Interpretation:** Even after a positive test, there is roughly an 80% chance the patient actually has the disease — illustrating how prior prevalence heavily influences posterior diagnostic certainty.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2, Set B]`**

> **(a) Define conditional probability and the multiplication law of probability. For two events $A$ and $B$ with $P(A) = 0.6$, $P(B) = 0.5$, and $P(A \cap B) = 0.3$, verify whether $A$ and $B$ are independent.** (7 Marks)
> 
> **(b) State and prove the law of total probability. Three machines $M_1$, $M_2$, $M_3$ produce 30%, 45%, and 25% of the total output respectively. The proportions of defective items from them are 0.02, 0.03, and 0.04. If a randomly selected item is found to be defective, find the probability that it came from $M_2$.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Conditional Probability and Independence Test

**Definitions:**

> **Conditional Probability:** The probability of occurrence of event $A$ given that event $B$ has already occurred is defined as:
> $$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}, \quad \text{provided } P(B) > 0$$
> 
> **Multiplication Law of Probability:** The joint probability of two events is the product of the marginal and conditional probabilities:
> $$P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A) = P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B)$$

**Independence Test:**

**Step 1 — Compute $P(A) \cdot P(B)$:**
$$P(A) \cdot P(B) = (0.6)(0.5) = 0.30$$

**Step 2 — Compare with $P(A \cap B)$:**
$$P(A \cap B) = 0.30$$

**Step 3 — Decision:**
$$P(A \cap B) = 0.30 = P(A) \cdot P(B)$$

Since the joint probability equals the product of marginals, the events $A$ and $B$ are **independent**. $\checkmark$

**Step 4 — Cross-check via conditional probability:**
$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)} = \frac{0.30}{0.50} = 0.60 = P(A) \;\checkmark$$

Knowing $B$ occurred has not changed the probability of $A$, confirming independence.

---

#### Part (b) — Law of Total Probability & Bayes' Application

**Statement of Law of Total Probability:** If $\{B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_n\}$ is a partition of $S$ (mutually exclusive and exhaustive), then for any event $A$:
$$P(A) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(B_i) \cdot P(A \mid B_i)$$

**Proof (Sketch):** As derived in §3.3 above, $A = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n}(A \cap B_i)$, the union of disjoint pieces, hence by Axiom 3 and the multiplication law:
$$P(A) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(A \cap B_i) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(B_i) \cdot P(A \mid B_i) \;\blacksquare$$

**Numerical Problem — Setup:**

- $P(M_1) = 0.30$, $P(M_2) = 0.45$, $P(M_3) = 0.25$ (the partition of all output).
- $P(D \mid M_1) = 0.02$, $P(D \mid M_2) = 0.03$, $P(D \mid M_3) = 0.04$ (defect rates).
- $D$ = event "item is defective".

**Required:** $P(M_2 \mid D)$.

**Step 1 — Total probability of $D$:**
$$P(D) = P(M_1) P(D \mid M_1) + P(M_2) P(D \mid M_2) + P(M_3) P(D \mid M_3)$$
$$P(D) = (0.30)(0.02) + (0.45)(0.03) + (0.25)(0.04)$$
$$P(D) = 0.0060 + 0.0135 + 0.0100 = 0.0295$$

**Step 2 — Bayes' theorem for $P(M_2 \mid D)$:**
$$P(M_2 \mid D) = \frac{P(M_2) \cdot P(D \mid M_2)}{P(D)} = \frac{(0.45)(0.03)}{0.0295} = \frac{0.0135}{0.0295}$$

$$\boxed{P(M_2 \mid D) = \frac{135}{295} = \frac{27}{59} \approx 0.4576}$$

**Interpretation:** Although machine $M_2$ produces 45% of all output, a defective item is *most likely* (≈ 45.76%) to have come from $M_2$ — almost matching its production share because $M_2$ has a *mid-range* defect rate.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Typically Lose Marks in this Topic**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the overlap term:** Writing $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B)$ *without* subtracting $P(A \cap B)$ when $A$ and $B$ are *not* mutually exclusive. **Loss: 2–3 marks.**
> 
> 2. **Bayes' theorem denominator error:** Many students compute only $P(B_i) \cdot P(A \mid B_i)$ in the numerator and *omit the total-probability denominator*. **Loss: 3 marks.**
> 
> 3. **Independence confusion:** Stating "independent" without computing $P(A) \cdot P(B)$ first and comparing with $P(A \cap B)$. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 
> 4. **Unit confusion in conditional probability:** Forgetting that $P(A \mid B)$ is *not* the same as $P(B \mid A)$. They differ by Bayes' theorem. **Loss: 2–4 marks.**
> 
> 5. **No units in the final answer:** Always state the numerical value *and* the interpretation (percentage / fraction) — KTU expects a boxed final answer with units of probability.
> 
> 6. **Skipping the partition verification:** When using the law of total probability, the events $B_i$ *must* be exhaustive and mutually exclusive. **Loss: 1 mark** if not stated.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Notations and Laws of Probability**
> 
> - ✅ **Sample space** $S$ = all outcomes; **event** $A \subseteq S$ = a subset; **sample point** $\omega \in S$ = one outcome.
> - ✅ The **three Kolmogorov axioms** (non-negativity, normalisation, additivity) are the bedrock of all probability.
> - ✅ Classical definition $P(A) = n(A)/n(S)$ holds **only for equally likely outcomes**.
> - ✅ **Complement rule:** $P(A^{\prime}) = 1 - P(A)$.
> - ✅ **General Addition Law:** $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$.
> - ✅ **Mutually exclusive** ⇒ $P(A \cap B) = 0$ ⇒ $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B)$.
> - ✅ **Conditional Probability:** $P(A \mid B) = P(A \cap B)/P(B)$, requiring $P(B) > 0$.
> - ✅ **Multiplication Law:** $P(A \cap B) = P(A) P(B \mid A) = P(B) P(A \mid B)$.
> - ✅ **Independence:** $P(A \cap B) = P(A) P(B)$ — *equivalent* to $P(A \mid B) = P(A)$.
> - ✅ **Law of Total Probability:** Requires $\{B_i\}$ to be a **partition** of $S$.
> - ✅ **Bayes' Theorem:** Always demands a **full total-probability denominator**.
> - ✅ In KTU exams, **always draw a Venn diagram or tree diagram** to visualise and avoid double-counting.
> - ✅ **Sanity check your numerical answer** with a qualitative interpretation — KTU awards 1 mark for "interpretation" lines.
> - ✅ When asked to "test for independence", **show the multiplication** of marginals explicitly.
> - ✅ The **classical, empirical (relative-frequency), and axiomatic** definitions of probability are *complementary*, not competing.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bayes' Rule and Screening Tests
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT414 - BIOSTATISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Bayes' Rule and Screening Tests

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Bayes' Rule

> [!NOTE]
> **Bayes' Theorem (Bayes' Rule)** is a foundational theorem in probability theory that describes how to update the probability of a hypothesis (or event) based on new evidence. In biostatistics, it provides a formal mechanism to revise a pre-test (prior) probability of disease into a post-test (posterior) probability after observing the result of a screening or diagnostic test.

Mathematically, for two events $A$ and $B$ where $P(B) > 0$:

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$$

Where:
- $P(A)$ = **Prior probability** of the event $A$ (probability before observing evidence)
- $P(A \mid B)$ = **Posterior probability** of $A$ given evidence $B$ (updated probability)
- $P(B \mid A)$ = **Likelihood** (probability of observing $B$ if $A$ is true)
- $P(B)$ = **Marginal probability** of the evidence (total probability of $B$ under all causes)

### 1.2 Screening Tests — Core Terminology

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Screening Test** is a medical or public health procedure applied to a population to detect the early presence or absence of a disease. The performance of such a test is evaluated using the following KTU-defined parameters:

| Term | Formal Meaning | Layman Phrasing |
|------|----------------|-----------------|
| **Sensitivity** ($Se$) | $P(T^+ \mid D^+)$ | Ability to correctly identify those **WITH** the disease |
| **Specificity** ($Sp$) | $P(T^- \mid D^-)$ | Ability to correctly identify those **WITHOUT** the disease |
| **Prevalence** ($p$) | $P(D^+)$ | The proportion of population having the disease |
| **Positive Predictive Value (PPV)** | $P(D^+ \mid T^+)$ | Probability that a positive test person truly has the disease |
| **Negative Predictive Value (NPV)** | $P(D^- \mid T^-)$ | Probability that a negative test person is truly disease-free |
| **False Positive Rate (FPR)** | $P(T^+ \mid D^-) = 1 - Sp$ | Healthy people wrongly flagged positive |
| **False Negative Rate (FNR)** | $P(T^- \mid D^+) = 1 - Se$ | Diseased people wrongly flagged negative |
| **Likelihood Ratio Positive (LR+)** | $\frac{Se}{1 - Sp}$ | How much a positive test raises disease odds |
| **Likelihood Ratio Negative (LR-)** | $\frac{1 - Se}{Sp}$ | How much a negative test lowers disease odds |

Where $T^+$ = Test Positive, $T^-$ = Test Negative, $D^+$ = Disease Present, $D^-$ = Disease Absent.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Weather Forecaster"**
>
> Imagine you are a farmer in Kerala. The **prior probability** of rain tomorrow is your gut feeling based on the season (say, 30% in summer). Now you check a **weather forecast** (your "test"). If the forecast says "Rain likely" and historically it has been right 80% of the time when it rains, and wrong 10% of the time when it doesn't, **Bayes' Rule** tells you how to combine:
> - Your prior belief (seasonal pattern)
> - The new evidence (forecast signal)
> - The reliability of that evidence (accuracy of forecast)
>
> to get a **posterior probability** — a sharper, updated belief about tomorrow's rain.
>
> In a **medical screening test** for, say, a disease like diabetes:
> - **Prior = Prevalence** (how common the disease is in your population)
> - **Test result = New Evidence**
> - **Sensitivity/Specificity = Reliability of the test**
> - **Posterior = PPV or NPV** (how likely the patient truly has/doesn't have the disease)
>
> Bayes' Rule is essentially the mathematical machinery of **"learning from experience"** — the central theme of biostatistics.

### 1.4 Physical / Standard Constants

> [!TIP]
> Although Bayes' Rule is a pure probability theorem (no physical constants), the following **standard reference metrics** are typically assumed or reported in KTU-style problems:
> - **Prevalence Range**: $0 < p < 1$ (typically $0.001$ to $0.5$ for screening populations).
> - **Sensitivity & Specificity**: Reported as decimals (e.g., $Se = 0.95$) or percentages ($95\%$).
> - **Probability Axiom**: $0 \le P(\cdot) \le 1$.
> - **Total Probability Law**: $P(B) = P(B \mid A)P(A) + P(B \mid A^c)P(A^c) = 1$ (sample space).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logical Flow of Bayes' Rule

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners expect students to understand the **sequential logic** of Bayesian updating, not just memorize a formula. The flow is:

- **Step 1 — Establish the Prior**: Begin with the pre-test probability $P(A)$. For medical screening, this is the **prevalence** of the disease in the target population, $P(D^+)$.

- **Step 2 — Define the Likelihood**: Quantify the test's reliability through $P(B \mid A)$ (sensitivity) and $P(B \mid A^c)$ (false positive rate $= 1 - Sp$). These are intrinsic properties of the test.

- **Step 3 — Compute the Marginal $P(B)$**: Use the **Law of Total Probability** to combine prior and likelihoods. This is the denominator that "normalizes" the numerator to yield a valid probability.

- **Step 4 — Update to Posterior**: Multiply the prior by the appropriate likelihood and divide by $P(B)$. The result $P(A \mid B)$ is the **PPV** or **NPV** depending on whether $B$ is a positive or negative test result.

- **Step 5 — The "Why" Behind the Logic**: The denominator $P(B)$ accounts for the fact that the test result $B$ can arise from two sources — true positives and false positives. Dividing by $P(B)$ rescales the result so the final posterior probabilities sum to 1 across all hypotheses.

### 2.2 Why Does Bayes' Rule Work? (Conceptual Justification)

Bayes' Rule is derived from the **symmetry of conditional probability**:
$$P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B) = P(A \cap B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)$$

Dividing both sides by $P(B)$ gives Bayes' Rule. The "magic" is that it **reverses the conditioning**: it lets us compute $P(A \mid B)$ (effect of evidence on hypothesis) from $P(B \mid A)$ (effect of hypothesis on evidence), which is usually much easier to measure in practice.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU Board Exams — Memorize These.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Application |
|---|--------------|----------|-------------|
| 1 | **Bayes' Rule (Generic)** | $P(A_i \mid B) = \dfrac{P(B \mid A_i) P(A_i)}{\sum_j P(B \mid A_j) P(A_j)}$ | Reverses conditioning for any number of hypotheses $A_i$ |
| 2 | **Bayes' Rule (Two Events)** | $P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(B \mid A) \, P(A)}{P(B \mid A) P(A) + P(B \mid A^c) P(A^c)}$ | Standard KTU problem setup |
| 3 | **Total Probability** | $P(B) = P(B \mid A) P(A) + P(B \mid A^c) P(A^c)$ | Denominator of Bayes' Rule |
| 4 | **Sensitivity** | $Se = P(T^+ \mid D^+)$ | Test's ability to detect disease |
| 5 | **Specificity** | $Sp = P(T^- \mid D^-)$ | Test's ability to rule out disease |
| 6 | **PPV (via Bayes)** | $PPV = \dfrac{Se \cdot p}{Se \cdot p + (1 - Sp)(1 - p)}$ | Probability disease is present given $T^+$ |
| 7 | **NPV (via Bayes)** | $NPV = \dfrac{Sp \cdot (1 - p)}{Sp \cdot (1 - p) + (1 - Se) \cdot p}$ | Probability disease is absent given $T^-$ |
| 8 | **Odds Form of Bayes** | $\text{Posterior Odds} = \text{Prior Odds} \times LR$ | Quick clinical calculations |
| 9 | **Likelihood Ratio (+)** | $LR^+ = \dfrac{Se}{1 - Sp}$ | Bayes factor for positive result |
| 10 | **Likelihood Ratio (-)** | $LR^- = \dfrac{1 - Se}{Sp}$ | Bayes factor for negative result |
| 11 | **Pre-Test Odds** | $\text{Prior Odds} = \dfrac{p}{1 - p}$ | Used with LR for Bayesian update |
| 12 | **Accuracy** | $Acc = \dfrac{TP + TN}{Total}$ | Overall test correctness |
| 13 | **Youden's Index** | $J = Se + Sp - 1$ | Single-number test performance metric ($0 \le J \le 1$) |
| 14 | **Fagan's Nomogram (Concept)** | $\log(\text{Post Odds}) = \log(\text{Pre Odds}) + \log(LR)$ | Graphical Bayes tool |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall — Distinguish $Se$ from $PPV$.** Sensitivity and Specificity are **fixed properties of the test**. PPV and NPV **depend on prevalence** $p$. A test with $95\%$ sensitivity may have a PPV of only $10\%$ if the disease is rare.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Medicine

> [!NOTE]
> - **Medical Diagnostics**: Bayes' Rule is the backbone of clinical decision-making. A doctor orders a test to convert a vague prior ("low risk, 1%") into a sharper posterior ("actual risk is 80%").
> - **Epidemic Surveillance (COVID-19, HIV)**: National screening programs (e.g., Kerala's Nipah screening) use PPV/NPV calculations to allocate resources and avoid false alarms.
> - **Bioengineering**: Diagnostic device manufacturers (e.g., Siemens Healthineers, Abbott) use LR+ and LR- to validate their assays.
> - **Machine Learning & AI**: Naive Bayes classifiers used in spam filters and disease prediction models are direct applications of this theorem.
> - **Public Health Policy**: Cost-effectiveness of mass screening programs hinges on Bayes-derived PPV.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Bayes' Rule from First Principles

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting Point — Definition of Conditional Probability:**
> The conditional probability of $A$ given $B$ is defined as:
> $$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$$
> This is the **KOLMOGOROV DEFINITION** of conditional probability and is the foundation of all Bayesian reasoning.

**Step 1 — Express the Joint Probability $P(A \cap B)$ in Two Ways:**

By the definition of conditional probability, the joint probability of both $A$ and $B$ occurring can be written from the perspective of either event being the "condition":

$$P(A \cap B) = P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B)$$

$$P(A \cap B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)$$

**Step 2 — Equate the Two Expressions:**

Since both expressions equal the same joint probability $P(A \cap B)$, we can set them equal:

$$P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)$$

**Step 3 — Isolate $P(A \mid B)$:**

Divide both sides by $P(B)$ (assuming $P(B) > 0$):

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$$

This is the **two-event form of Bayes' Rule**. The derivation is complete with no additional assumptions beyond the definition of conditional probability.

**Step 4 — Expand $P(B)$ Using Total Probability:**

The Law of Total Probability states that if $\{A, A^c\}$ partitions the sample space, then:

$$P(B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A) + P(B \mid A^c) \cdot P(A^c)$$

**Step 5 — Substitute Back to Get the Operational Form:**

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A) + P(B \mid A^c) \cdot P(A^c)}$$

This is the **most commonly used form in KTU examination problems** because all terms on the right-hand side are typically known or directly computable from 2x2 contingency table data. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Positive Predictive Value (PPV)

> [!NOTE]
> The Positive Predictive Value is the Bayesian posterior $P(D^+ \mid T^+)$, derived by identifying the events in Bayes' Rule as follows: $A \to D^+$, $B \to T^+$.

**Step 1 — Apply Bayes' Rule:**

$$PPV = P(D^+ \mid T^+) = \frac{P(T^+ \mid D^+) \cdot P(D^+)}{P(T^+)}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the Test Parameters:**

From the definitions: $P(T^+ \mid D^+) = Se$ and $P(D^+) = p$ (prevalence).

**Step 3 — Expand the Denominator Using Total Probability:**

The event $T^+$ can arise from two disjoint scenarios — the patient is truly diseased (true positive) or truly healthy (false positive):

$$P(T^+) = P(T^+ \mid D^+) \cdot P(D^+) + P(T^+ \mid D^-) \cdot P(D^-)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the Standard Terms:**

$P(T^+ \mid D^-) = 1 - Sp$ (false positive rate) and $P(D^-) = 1 - p$.

**Step 5 — Final Operational Formula:**

$$PPV = \frac{Se \cdot p}{Se \cdot p + (1 - Sp)(1 - p)} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Negative Predictive Value (NPV)

> [!NOTE]
> Similarly, $NPV = P(D^- \mid T^-)$ is the Bayesian posterior for a negative test result.

**Step 1 — Apply Bayes' Rule:**

$$NPV = P(D^- \mid T^-) = \frac{P(T^- \mid D^-) \cdot P(D^-)}{P(T^-)}$$

**Step 2 — Identify Parameters:**

$P(T^- \mid D^-) = Sp$ and $P(D^-) = 1 - p$.

**Step 3 — Expand $P(T^-)$:**

$$P(T^-) = P(T^- \mid D^-) \cdot P(D^-) + P(T^- \mid D^+) \cdot P(D^+)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute:**

$P(T^- \mid D^+) = 1 - Se$ and $P(D^+) = p$.

**Step 5 — Final Operational Formula:**

$$NPV = \frac{Sp \cdot (1 - p)}{Sp \cdot (1 - p) + (1 - Se) \cdot p} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — Complete Numerical Solution

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A new rapid test for a tropical disease has $Se = 0.90$ and $Sp = 0.95$. In a Kerala district, the prevalence of the disease is $p = 0.02$ (2%). If a person tests positive, what is the probability that they actually have the disease? Compute PPV, NPV, and verify your results sum correctly.

**Step 1 — List the Given Data:**

$Se = 0.90$, $Sp = 0.95$, $p = P(D^+) = 0.02$, $1 - p = 0.98$, $1 - Se = 0.10$, $1 - Sp = 0.05$.

**Step 2 — Compute PPV Numerator:**

$$\text{Numerator}_{PPV} = Se \cdot p = 0.90 \times 0.02 = 0.018$$

**Step 3 — Compute PPV Denominator:**

$$\text{Denom}_{PPV} = Se \cdot p + (1 - Sp)(1 - p) = 0.018 + (0.05)(0.98) = 0.018 + 0.049 = 0.067$$

**Step 4 — Compute PPV:**

$$PPV = \frac{0.018}{0.067} \approx 0.2687 \approx 26.87\%$$

**Interpretation**: Even with a highly specific test (95%), only about **27% of positive results** represent true disease in a low-prevalence population. This is the **base-rate fallacy** in action.

**Step 5 — Compute NPV Numerator:**

$$\text{Numerator}_{NPV} = Sp \cdot (1 - p) = 0.95 \times 0.98 = 0.931$$

**Step 6 — Compute NPV Denominator:**

$$\text{Denom}_{NPV} = Sp \cdot (1 - p) + (1 - Se) \cdot p = 0.931 + (0.10)(0.02) = 0.931 + 0.002 = 0.933$$

**Step 7 — Compute NPV:**

$$NPV = \frac{0.931}{0.933} \approx 0.9979 \approx 99.79\%$$

**Interpretation**: A negative test is highly reassuring (99.8% chance of being disease-free), because the prior probability of disease was already low.

**Step 8 — Validate using Likelihood Ratios:**

$$LR^+ = \frac{Se}{1 - Sp} = \frac{0.90}{0.05} = 18$$

$$\text{Prior Odds} = \frac{p}{1 - p} = \frac{0.02}{0.98} \approx 0.0204$$

$$\text{Posterior Odds} = 18 \times 0.0204 = 0.3673$$

$$\text{Posterior Probability} = \frac{0.3673}{1 + 0.3673} \approx 0.2687 \approx 26.87\% \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 9 — Build the 2x2 Contingency Table (per 10,000 people):**

| | Disease Present ($D^+$) | Disease Absent ($D^-$) | Total |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Test Positive ($T^+$)** | $TP = 0.90 \times 200 = 180$ | $FP = 0.05 \times 9800 = 490$ | $670$ |
| **Test Negative ($T^-$)** | $FN = 0.10 \times 200 = 20$ | $TN = 0.95 \times 9800 = 9310$ | $9330$ |
| **Total** | $200$ | $9800$ | $10000$ |

**Step 10 — Recompute PPV and NPV from Table:**

$$PPV = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{180}{670} \approx 0.2687 \quad \checkmark$$

$$NPV = \frac{TN}{TN + FN} = \frac{9310}{9330} \approx 0.9979 \quad \checkmark$$

Both methods agree, validating Bayes' Rule.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Bayesian Decision Flow for a Screening Test

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block illustrates the **functional flow** of how raw prevalence data passes through a screening test's reliability metrics to produce clinically meaningful posterior probabilities. This is the exact architecture KTU examiners expect students to draw in their answer sheets.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA["Population of N Individuals"]
    inputB["Disease Prevalence p = P of D plus"]
    prior["Prior Probability P of D plus equals p"]
    testApply["Apply Screening Test"]
    sensitivityPath["If Diseased D plus: Test Positive with Probability Se"]
    specificityPath["If Healthy D minus: Test Negative with Probability Sp"]
    fpPath["If Healthy D minus: Test Positive with Probability one minus Sp FALSE POSITIVE"]
    fnPath["If Diseased D plus: Test Negative with Probability one minus Se FALSE NEGATIVE"]
    bayesCompute["Apply Bayes Rule with Total Probability in Denominator"]
    ppvNode["Compute PPV equals P of D plus given T plus"]
    npvNode["Compute NPV equals P of D minus given T minus"]
    decision1["Clinical Decision: Treat and Confirm with Gold Standard"]
    decision2["Clinical Decision: Reassure Patient and Routine Follow Up"]

    inputA --> inputB
    inputB --> prior
    prior --> testApply
    testApply --> sensitivityPath
    testApply --> specificityPath
    testApply --> fpPath
    testApply --> fnPath
    sensitivityPath --> bayesCompute
    fpPath --> bayesCompute
    bayesCompute --> ppvNode
    bayesCompute --> npvNode
    ppvNode --> decision1
    npvNode --> decision2
```

### 4.2 2x2 Contingency Table Architecture (Schematic View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cellTL["TRUE POSITIVE TP\nDiseased and Test Positive\nDrives PPV"]
    cellTR["FALSE POSITIVE FP\nHealthy but Test Positive\nDrives one minus Sp"]
    cellBL["FALSE NEGATIVE FN\nDiseased but Test Negative\nDrives one minus Se"]
    cellBR["TRUE NEGATIVE TN\nHealthy and Test Negative\nDrives NPV"]
    sumRow["Row Sum gives Total Test Positives and Total Test Negatives"]
    sumCol["Column Sum gives Total Diseased and Total Healthy"]
    grandTotal["Grand Total N equals Study Population"]
    ppvArrow["PPV equals TP divided by TP plus FP"]
    npvArrow["NPV equals TN divided by TN plus FN"]
    seArrow["Se equals TP divided by TP plus FN"]
    spArrow["Sp equals TN divided by TN plus FP"]

    cellTL --> sumRow
    cellTR --> sumRow
    cellBL --> sumRow
    cellBR --> sumRow
    cellTL --> sumCol
    cellTR --> sumCol
    cellBL --> sumCol
    cellBR --> sumCol
    sumRow --> grandTotal
    sumCol --> grandTotal
    cellTL --> ppvArrow
    cellTR --> ppvArrow
    cellBL --> npvArrow
    cellBR --> npvArrow
    cellTL --> seArrow
    cellBL --> seArrow
    cellTR --> spArrow
    cellBR --> spArrow
```

### 4.3 Bayesian Updating Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA["Step 1: Start with Prior Probability p"]
    stepB["Step 2: Conduct Screening Test"]
    stepC["Step 3: Record Test Result T plus or T minus"]
    stepD["Step 4: Compute Likelihood Ratio LR plus or LR minus"]
    stepE["Step 5: Multiply Prior Odds by LR"]
    stepF["Step 6: Convert Posterior Odds to Posterior Probability"]
    stepG["Step 7: Use Posterior for Clinical Decision"]
    stepH["Optional Step 8: New Prior for Next Test in Series"]

    stepA --> stepB
    stepB --> stepC
    stepC --> stepD
    stepD --> stepE
    stepE --> stepF
    stepF --> stepG
    stepG --> stepH
    stepH --> stepA
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to Use These Diagrams in Your KTU Exam Answer Sheet:**
> 1. Always draw the **2x2 contingency table first** when given raw counts — this is the single most mark-fetching visual in screening test problems.
> 2. Label all four cells ($TP$, $FP$, $FN$, $TN$) explicitly, then write $Se$, $Sp$, $PPV$, $NPV$ as ratios of the appropriate cells.
> 3. For Bayes' Rule problems, draw a **tree diagram** showing branches for $D^+$/$D^-$ and $T^+$/$T^-$, then label each branch with the conditional probability and prior.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*State and explain Bayes' Theorem in probability. Define the terms: prior probability, posterior probability, and likelihood.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Bayes' Theorem:** For two events $A$ and $B$ with $P(B) > 0$,

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$$

- **Prior Probability $P(A)$:** The initial probability of the hypothesis $A$ before any new evidence is observed. In medical screening, this is the **prevalence** of the disease.
- **Likelihood $P(B \mid A)$:** The probability of observing the evidence $B$ assuming the hypothesis $A$ is true. In screening, this corresponds to the **sensitivity** or **false positive rate** of the test.
- **Posterior Probability $P(A \mid B)$:** The updated probability of $A$ after observing evidence $B$. In screening, this is the **PPV** or **NPV**, depending on the test result.

Bayes' Theorem is a fundamental tool in biostatistics that allows researchers and clinicians to **revise probabilities** as new data becomes available, forming the mathematical foundation of **clinical decision-making** and **diagnostic reasoning**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Sample Paper]**
*Define the terms Sensitivity, Specificity, Positive Predictive Value (PPV), and Negative Predictive Value (NPV) with reference to a screening test.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Term | Definition | Formula |
|------|------------|---------|
| **Sensitivity ($Se$)** | The ability of a test to correctly identify individuals **with** the disease. It is the true positive rate. | $Se = P(T^+ \mid D^+)$ |
| **Specificity ($Sp$)** | The ability of a test to correctly identify individuals **without** the disease. It is the true negative rate. | $Sp = P(T^- \mid D^-)$ |
| **PPV** | The probability that a person with a positive test result **truly has** the disease. | $PPV = P(D^+ \mid T^+)$ |
| **NPV** | The probability that a person with a negative test result is **truly free** of the disease. | $NPV = P(D^- \mid T^-)$ |

Sensitivity and Specificity are **intrinsic properties** of the test, whereas PPV and NPV depend on the **prevalence** of the disease in the population under study.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Students answer **ONE of TWO choices** (OR pattern).

---

#### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** A new diagnostic test for malaria has Sensitivity $Se = 0.92$ and Specificity $Sp = 0.88$. In a rural area, the prevalence of malaria is $0.05$ (5%). If a person tests **positive**, find the probability that they actually have malaria. Use Bayes' Theorem. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** If the same test is now used in a high-prevalence urban outbreak region where the prevalence is $0.30$ (30%), find the new PPV. Comment on how prevalence affects the PPV. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) Solution:**

Given: $Se = 0.92$, $Sp = 0.88$, $p = 0.05$, $1 - p = 0.95$, $1 - Sp = 0.12$.

By Bayes' Theorem:

$$PPV = P(D^+ \mid T^+) = \frac{Se \cdot p}{Se \cdot p + (1 - Sp)(1 - p)}$$

**Step 1 — Numerator:**

$$Se \cdot p = 0.92 \times 0.05 = 0.046$$

**Step 2 — Denominator — First Term:**

$$Se \cdot p = 0.046$$

**Step 3 — Denominator — Second Term:**

$$(1 - Sp)(1 - p) = 0.12 \times 0.95 = 0.114$$

**Step 4 — Full Denominator:**

$$P(T^+) = 0.046 + 0.114 = 0.160$$

**Step 5 — PPV Calculation:**

$$PPV = \frac{0.046}{0.160} = 0.2875 \approx 28.75\%$$

**Conclusion:** A positive test result indicates only a **28.75% chance** of actually having malaria in this low-prevalence setting. The remaining 71.25% of "positives" are false alarms, illustrating the base-rate fallacy.

**Part (b) Solution:**

Given: $Se = 0.92$, $Sp = 0.88$, $p = 0.30$, $1 - p = 0.70$, $1 - Sp = 0.12$.

**Step 1 — Numerator:**

$$Se \cdot p = 0.92 \times 0.30 = 0.276$$

**Step 2 — Denominator — Second Term:**

$$(1 - Sp)(1 - p) = 0.12 \times 0.70 = 0.084$$

**Step 3 — Full Denominator:**

$$P(T^+) = 0.276 + 0.084 = 0.360$$

**Step 4 — PPV Calculation:**

$$PPV = \frac{0.276}{0.360} \approx 0.7667 \approx 76.67\%$$

**Step 5 — Comparative Comment:**

When prevalence increased from $5\%$ to $30\%$ (a 6-fold increase), the PPV jumped from **28.75% to 76.67%** — nearly a 2.7-fold increase. This demonstrates a critical biostatistical principle: **PPV is a strong increasing function of prevalence** for a fixed test, even though the test's intrinsic sensitivity and specificity remain unchanged. In clinical practice, this means that **screening tests perform dramatically better in high-risk populations** than in general (low-prevalence) populations.

---

#### **Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Alternative]**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Sample Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the terms "Likelihood Ratio Positive" ($LR^+$) and "Likelihood Ratio Negative" ($LR^-$). Derive their formulas from the definitions of Sensitivity and Specificity. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A clinician is evaluating a patient with chest pain. The pre-test probability (prevalence) of coronary artery disease (CAD) in this demographic is $0.20$. A treadmill stress test has $LR^+ = 4.0$ and $LR^- = 0.25$. Using the odds form of Bayes' Theorem, compute the post-test probability of CAD if the test is **positive** and if the test is **negative**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) Solution:**

The **Likelihood Ratio (LR)** quantifies how much a given test result shifts the odds of disease, and is derived directly from the test's sensitivity and specificity.

**Likelihood Ratio Positive ($LR^+$):**

This represents the ratio of the probability of a positive test in diseased individuals to the probability of a positive test in healthy individuals:

$$LR^+ = \frac{P(T^+ \mid D^+)}{P(T^+ \mid D^-)} = \frac{Se}{1 - Sp}$$

**Likelihood Ratio Negative ($LR^-$):**

This represents the ratio of the probability of a negative test in diseased individuals to the probability of a negative test in healthy individuals:

$$LR^- = \frac{P(T^- \mid D^+)}{P(T^- \mid D^-)} = \frac{1 - Se}{Sp}$$

**Interpretation:**
- $LR^+ > 1$ means a positive test **increases** the odds of disease.
- $LR^- < 1$ means a negative test **decreases** the odds of disease.
- $LR^+ > 10$ or $LR^- < 0.1$ are considered strong evidence for clinical decision-making.

**Part (b) Solution:**

Given: Pre-test prevalence $p = 0.20$, $LR^+ = 4.0$, $LR^- = 0.25$.

**Step 1 — Convert Prevalence to Pre-test Odds:**

$$\text{Pre-test Odds} = \frac{p}{1 - p} = \frac{0.20}{0.80} = 0.25$$

**Step 2 — Compute Post-test Odds for Positive Result:**

By the odds form of Bayes' Theorem:

$$\text{Post-test Odds}_{T^+} = \text{Pre-test Odds} \times LR^+ = 0.25 \times 4.0 = 1.0$$

**Step 3 — Convert Post-test Odds to Post-test Probability (Positive):**

$$P_{T^+} = \frac{\text{Post Odds}}{1 + \text{Post Odds}} = \frac{1.0}{1 + 1.0} = \frac{1.0}{2.0} = 0.50 = 50\%$$

**Step 4 — Compute Post-test Odds for Negative Result:**

$$\text{Post-test Odds}_{T^-} = \text{Pre-test Odds} \times LR^- = 0.25 \times 0.25 = 0.0625$$

**Step 5 — Convert Post-test Odds to Post-test Probability (Negative):**

$$P_{T^-} = \frac{0.0625}{1 + 0.0625} = \frac{0.0625}{1.0625} \approx 0.0588 \approx 5.88\%$$

**Clinical Conclusion:**

- A **positive** stress test raises the probability of CAD from $20\%$ (prior) to **$50\%$** (posterior) — a substantial increase that justifies further invasive testing (e.g., angiography).
- A **negative** stress test lowers the probability of CAD from $20\%$ to approximately **$5.88\%$** — strong evidence to rule out disease, though not absolute.

This problem illustrates the **practical utility of the odds form of Bayes' Theorem**: it is computationally faster than the full PPV formula and is widely used at the bedside via **Fagan's Nomogram** (a graphical Bayesian calculator).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in Bayes' Rule & Screening Test Questions (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
>
> 1. **Confusing Sensitivity with PPV (2-3 marks lost)**: Students often write "Se = 0.90, so the test is 90% accurate." WRONG. Sensitivity is a property of the test applied to diseased people. PPV is what matters to the patient.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Law of Total Probability in the Denominator (3-4 marks lost)**: A frequent error is to compute $PPV = Se \cdot p$, omitting the false positive term. Always show the full expansion:
> $$P(T^+) = Se \cdot p + (1 - Sp)(1 - p)$$
>
> 3. **Not Stating the Assumption $P(B) > 0$**: KTU examiners award marks for mathematical rigor. Always note that the denominator must be non-zero.
>
> 4. **Mixing up Events and Conditions**: $P(T^+ \mid D^+)$ is **not** the same as $P(D^+ \mid T^+)$. The vertical bar is critical — reversing the conditioning changes the meaning entirely.
>
> 5. **Forgetting Units and Validation**: Always end your answer with a sanity check: "Since $0 \le PPV \le 1$ and $PPV + \text{False Discovery Rate} = 1$, our answer is consistent." This often fetches a bonus mark.
>
> 6. **Skipping the Diagram**: A 2x2 contingency table or probability tree diagram in your solution can fetch **1-2 extra marks** for visual clarity, even if not explicitly asked.
>
> 7. **Not Converting Prevalence to Odds**: When using $LR^+ / LR^-$, students forget to convert prevalence to odds. The likelihood ratio multiplies **odds**, not probabilities.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Bayes' Rule & Screening Tests**

- ⭐ **Bayes' Rule (Two-Event):** $P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B \mid A) P(A) + P(B \mid A^c) P(A^c)}$

- ⭐ **Sensitivity** = $P(T^+ \mid D^+)$ — **fixed property** of the test.

- ⭐ **Specificity** = $P(T^- \mid D^-)$ — **fixed property** of the test.

- ⭐ **Prevalence** $p$ = $P(D^+)$ — the **prior probability** in Bayesian screening.

- ⭐ **PPV** = $P(D^+ \mid T^+) = \dfrac{Se \cdot p}{Se \cdot p + (1 - Sp)(1 - p)}$ — **depends on prevalence**.

- ⭐ **NPV** = $P(D^- \mid T^-) = \dfrac{Sp \cdot (1 - p)}{Sp \cdot (1 - p) + (1 - Se) \cdot p}$ — **depends on prevalence**.

- ⭐ **Odds Form:** Post-test Odds = Pre-test Odds $\times$ Likelihood Ratio.

- ⭐ **LR+** = $\dfrac{Se}{1 - Sp}$ and **LR-** = $\dfrac{1 - Se}{Sp}$.

- ⭐ **Key Insight 1:** As prevalence $p$ **increases**, PPV **increases** and NPV **decreases**.

- ⭐ **Key Insight 2:** As prevalence $p$ **decreases**, PPV **decreases** and NPV **increases** (base-rate fallacy).

- ⭐ **Key Insight 3:** Sensitivity and Specificity are inversely related by the ROC curve — improving one typically worsens the other.

- ⭐ **Key Insight 4:** The denominator in Bayes' Rule is computed via the **Law of Total Probability**, summing over all possible causes of the evidence.

- ⭐ **Key Insight 5:** Youden's Index $J = Se + Sp - 1$ is a single-number summary that ranges from 0 (useless test) to 1 (perfect test).

- ⭐ **Key Insight 6:** For rare diseases, even highly specific tests can yield low PPV — this is why confirmatory (gold-standard) tests are essential.

- ⭐ **Mnemonic:** "**Sn**iff out disease, **Sp**ecifically rule it out" — **Sn** = Sensitivity, **Sp** = Specificity. **PPV/NPV = Spin** (depend on prevalence).

- ⭐ **Always draw the 2x2 contingency table** in your exam answer — it is the single most useful visual tool for screening test problems and almost always earns full marks when correctly labeled.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bayesian Inference
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT414 - BIOSTATISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Bayesian Inference

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Bayesian Inference?

**Bayesian Inference** is a statistical method of inference in which Bayes' Theorem is used to update the probability of a hypothesis or parameter as more evidence or data becomes available. It provides a formal mathematical framework for combining prior beliefs with observed data to produce updated (posterior) beliefs.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEBMT414 – Module 2):**
> *Bayesian Inference is a method of statistical inference in which Bayes' theorem is used to update the probability for a hypothesis as more evidence or information becomes available. It explicitly incorporates prior knowledge alongside sample data to derive posterior distributions for parameters of interest.*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a doctor who has **prior** experience (say, she has seen 1,000 flu cases in her career) telling her that about 30% of patients walking in during winter with a fever actually have the flu. That is her **initial belief** — the *prior probability*. When a *new* patient walks in, she runs a blood test, and the result of that test acts as fresh *evidence* (the *likelihood*). She now *updates* her belief: "Given this test result, the chance this patient has the flu is now 75%, not 30%." That updated belief is the **posterior probability**.

Bayesian inference formalizes exactly this kind of learning process. Mathematically:

> **Prior belief + New evidence (data) → Updated (posterior) belief.**

The famous equation at the heart of Bayesian inference is **Bayes' Theorem**:

$$P(\theta \mid \text{data}) = \frac{P(\text{data} \mid \theta) \cdot P(\theta)}{P(\text{data})}$$

where $\theta$ is the unknown parameter we want to learn about, $P(\theta)$ is the prior, $P(\text{data} \mid \theta)$ is the likelihood, and $P(\theta \mid \text{data})$ is the posterior.

---

## 1.3 Key Vocabulary (KTU-Mandated Terms)

| Term | Plain Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Prior Distribution** $P(\theta)$ | Belief about $\theta$ *before* seeing the data |
| **Likelihood** $P(\text{data} \mid \theta)$ | Probability of observing the data given a value of $\theta$ |
| **Posterior Distribution** $P(\theta \mid \text{data})$ | Updated belief *after* combining prior with data |
| **Evidence / Marginal** $P(\text{data})$ | Total probability of the data, integrated over all $\theta$ |
