| **Credible Interval** | Bayesian analogue of a confidence interval |
| **Conjugate Prior** | A prior from the same family as the posterior |
| **Bayes Factor** | Ratio comparing evidence for two competing hypotheses |
| **Posterior Odds** | Ratio of posterior probabilities of two hypotheses |
| **Hyperparameter** | Parameter that governs the shape of the prior distribution |
| **Non-informative Prior** | A vague prior that lets the data dominate the posterior |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:**
> In biostatistics, Bayesian inference is critical for clinical trials, diagnostic testing, epidemiological modeling, genomic studies, and survival analysis. The KTU syllabus specifically expects you to derive the posterior, identify conjugate priors, compute credible intervals, and perform Bayesian hypothesis testing using **Bayes Factors**.

---

## 1.4 Why Bayesian Inference? (Engineering & Bio-Medical Utility)

- **Combines expert knowledge** with hard data — vital in medical research where prior clinical experience is invaluable.
- **Naturally handles small samples** by borrowing strength from informative priors.
- Provides **direct probabilistic statements** about parameters (e.g., "the probability that a drug is effective is 0.92"), which is more intuitive than the frequentist interpretation.
- Flexible: works for **complex hierarchical models** used in epidemiology and pharmacokinetics.
- Forms the mathematical backbone of **machine learning classification methods** such as Naive Bayes classifiers, Bayesian Neural Networks, and Gaussian Processes.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bayesian Workflow — Step-by-Step Logic

The operational sequence a KTU student must follow for any Bayesian problem is:

**Step 1 — Identify the unknown parameter $\theta$.**
Clearly define what you are trying to learn (e.g., the prevalence of a disease, the mean of a treatment effect, the odds ratio for a risk factor).

**Step 2 — Specify the prior distribution $P(\theta)$.**
Choose a prior that reflects pre-experimental knowledge. Common choices in biostatistics:
- **Beta prior** for a proportion $p \in [0,1]$.
- **Gamma prior** for a rate $\lambda > 0$ or a positive variance.
- **Normal prior** for a mean $\mu$ on the real line.
- **Uniform prior** (non-informative) when no prior knowledge is assumed.

**Step 3 — Write down the likelihood $P(\text{data} \mid \theta)$.**
This is the standard sampling model — e.g., Binomial, Poisson, Normal, Exponential — depending on the data type.

**Step 4 — Apply Bayes' Theorem to obtain the posterior $P(\theta \mid \text{data})$.**
The posterior is proportional to *prior × likelihood*. The constant of proportionality is the marginal evidence $P(\text{data})$, which is often a normalising constant that we don't always need to compute explicitly.

**Step 5 — Summarise the posterior.**
Compute the posterior mean, mode, variance, and a credible interval (e.g., 95% Highest Posterior Density interval).

**Step 6 — Make a decision (if needed).**
Use Bayes Factors, posterior odds, or a loss function to choose between competing hypotheses or actions.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is the **must-memorize** formula set for the KTU ESE on Bayesian Inference. Note: vertical bars are rendered using `\mid` to keep the table markdown safe.

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bayes' Theorem | $P(\theta \mid D) = \dfrac{P(D \mid \theta)\,P(\theta)}{P(D)}$ | $D \equiv \text{data}$ |
| 2 | Posterior proportional form | $P(\theta \mid D) \propto P(D \mid \theta)\,P(\theta)$ | Useful when normalising constant is hard |
| 3 | Marginal evidence | $P(D) = \int P(D \mid \theta)\,P(\theta)\,d\theta$ | Integral over parameter space |
| 4 | Beta-Binomial conjugate | If $p \sim \text{Beta}(\alpha,\beta)$ and $X \mid p \sim \text{Bin}(n,p)$, then $p \mid X \sim \text{Beta}(\alpha + x,\; \beta + n - x)$ | Conjugate pair |
| 5 | Posterior mean (Beta-Binomial) | $E[p \mid x] = \dfrac{\alpha + x}{\alpha + \beta + n}$ | Shrinks prior mean and MLE |
| 6 | Normal-Normal conjugate (known $\sigma^2$) | If $\mu \sim N(m_0, v_0^2)$ and $X \mid \mu \sim N(\mu,\sigma^2)$, posterior $\mu \mid \bar{x} \sim N(m_n, v_n^2)$ with $v_n^2 = \left(\dfrac{1}{v_0^2} + \dfrac{n}{\sigma^2}\right)^{-1}$, $m_n = v_n^2\left(\dfrac{m_0}{v_0^2} + \dfrac{n\bar{x}}{\sigma^2}\right)$ | Classical conjugate |
| 7 | Gamma-Poisson conjugate | If $\lambda \sim \text{Gamma}(\alpha,\beta)$ and $X \mid \lambda \sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda)$, then $\lambda \mid \sum x_i \sim \text{Gamma}\!\left(\alpha + \sum x_i,\; \beta + n\right)$ | Used for count/rate data |
| 8 | Posterior odds | $\text{Posterior Odds} = \text{Prior Odds} \times \text{Bayes Factor}$ | $\dfrac{P(H_1 \mid D)}{P(H_2 \mid D)} = \dfrac{P(H_1)}{P(H_2)} \times BF_{12}$ |
| 9 | Bayes Factor | $BF_{12} = \dfrac{P(D \mid H_1)}{P(D \mid H_2)}$ | Ratio of marginal likelihoods |
| 10 | $2 \log_{e}(BF_{10})$ vs $\chi^2$ | $2 \ln BF_{10} \sim \chi^2_1$ (simple point null) | Approximate conversion for KTU problems |
| 11 | Posterior predictive | $P(\tilde{x} \mid D) = \int P(\tilde{x} \mid \theta)\,P(\theta \mid D)\,d\theta$ | For predicting new observations |
| 12 | Equal-tail 95% credible interval | $P(\theta_L \le \theta \le \theta_U \mid D) = 0.95$ with $P(\theta < \theta_L \mid D) = P(\theta > \theta_U \mid D) = 0.025$ | Bayesian analogue of CI |
| 13 | Jeffrey's prior (for $\mu$, $\sigma^2=1$) | $P(\mu) \propto 1$ | Non-informative (uniform) |
| 14 | Jeffrey's prior (for $p$ proportion) | $P(p) \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{p(1-p)}}$ = Beta(1/2, 1/2) | Non-informative on Bernoulli |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering & Bio-Medical Utility

- **Clinical Trials:** Bayesian adaptive designs allow trials to be stopped early if a treatment is clearly superior or futile — used extensively in pharmaceutical R&D.
- **Diagnostic Testing:** Updating disease probability given a positive/negative test (sensitivity, specificity) is a direct application of Bayes' rule.
- **Genomics & Bioinformatics:** Bayesian methods are used to identify differentially expressed genes and to estimate genetic heritability.
- **Epidemiology:** Estimating disease prevalence in a population when only a partial sample is available.
- **Reliability Engineering:** Bayesian reliability prediction uses prior failure data combined with new test data to forecast component lifetimes.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bayes' Theorem from Conditional Probability Axioms

We begin from the definition of conditional probability:

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}, \quad P(B) > 0$$

By symmetry, the joint probability of two events $A$ and $B$ can be written in two ways:

$$P(A \cap B) = P(A \mid B)\,P(B) = P(B \mid A)\,P(A)$$

Equating the two expressions and solving for $P(A \mid B)$:

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A)\,P(A)}{P(B)}$$

Now substitute the parameter $\theta$ for $A$ and the data $D$ for $B$:

$$P(\theta \mid D) = \frac{P(D \mid \theta)\,P(\theta)}{P(D)}$$

To express the marginal evidence $P(D)$ explicitly, we use the law of total probability, summing (or integrating) over all possible values of $\theta$:

$$P(D) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} P(D \mid \theta)\,P(\theta)\,d\theta$$

Hence the continuous form of Bayes' theorem is:

$$P(\theta \mid D) = \frac{P(D \mid \theta)\,P(\theta)}{\int P(D \mid \theta)\,P(\theta)\,d\theta}$$

For discrete parameters, the integral is replaced by a sum over all possible values of $\theta$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Beta-Binomial Conjugate Posterior

This is the **single most important derivation** in KTU Module 2. Master this — examiners love it.

**Setup:**
- We observe $X$ successes out of $n$ Bernoulli trials, so $X \mid p \sim \text{Binomial}(n, p)$.
- We adopt a Beta prior for $p$, with hyperparameters $\alpha > 0$ and $\beta > 0$:
  $$P(p) = \frac{1}{B(\alpha,\beta)}\,p^{\alpha-1}(1-p)^{\beta-1}, \quad 0 \le p \le 1$$
- The Beta function is $B(\alpha,\beta) = \dfrac{\Gamma(\alpha)\Gamma(\beta)}{\Gamma(\alpha+\beta)}$.

**Likelihood function:**

$$P(X = x \mid p) = \binom{n}{x}\,p^{x}(1-p)^{n-x}$$

**Apply Bayes' theorem (proportional form):**

$$P(p \mid x) \propto P(x \mid p)\,P(p)$$

$$P(p \mid x) \propto \left[\binom{n}{x}\,p^{x}(1-p)^{n-x}\right]\cdot\left[\frac{1}{B(\alpha,\beta)}\,p^{\alpha-1}(1-p)^{\beta-1}\right]$$

Drop the constants that don't depend on $p$ (they get absorbed by the normalising constant):

$$P(p \mid x) \propto p^{x}\,(1-p)^{n-x} \cdot p^{\alpha-1}\,(1-p)^{\beta-1}$$

Combine the powers of $p$ and $(1-p)$:

$$P(p \mid x) \propto p^{(\alpha + x) - 1}\,(1-p)^{(\beta + n - x) - 1}$$

This is the **kernel of a Beta distribution** with updated parameters $\alpha' = \alpha + x$ and $\beta' = \beta + n - x$. Therefore, the exact posterior is:

$$p \mid x \;\sim\; \text{Beta}\!\left(\alpha + x,\; \beta + n - x\right)$$

**Posterior mean:**

$$E[p \mid x] = \frac{\alpha + x}{\alpha + \beta + n} = \frac{\alpha + \beta}{\alpha + \beta + n}\cdot\frac{\alpha}{\alpha + \beta} + \frac{n}{\alpha + \beta + n}\cdot\frac{x}{n}$$

This is a **weighted average** of the prior mean $\dfrac{\alpha}{\alpha+\beta}$ and the MLE $\dfrac{x}{n}$, with weights proportional to the prior "sample size" $(\alpha+\beta)$ and the data sample size $n$.

**Posterior variance:**

$$\text{Var}(p \mid x) = \frac{(\alpha+x)(\beta+n-x)}{(\alpha+\beta+n)^2\,(\alpha+\beta+n+1)}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Normal-Normal Conjugate (Known Variance)

**Setup:**
- Data: $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n \mid \mu \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ with known $\sigma^2$.
- Prior: $\mu \sim N(m_0, v_0^2)$.

**Likelihood (combined, sufficient statistic is $\bar{X}$):**

$$P(\bar{x} \mid \mu) \propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{n(\bar{x} - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

**Prior:**

$$P(\mu) \propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{(\mu - m_0)^2}{2v_0^2}\right)$$

**Posterior (up to proportionality):**

$$P(\mu \mid \bar{x}) \propto \exp\!\left[-\frac{1}{2}\!\left(\frac{n(\bar{x}-\mu)^2}{\sigma^2} + \frac{(\mu-m_0)^2}{v_0^2}\right)\right]$$

Collect the $\mu^2$ and $\mu$ terms by completing the square. The coefficient of $\mu^2$ gives the posterior precision:

$$\frac{1}{v_n^2} = \frac{1}{v_0^2} + \frac{n}{\sigma^2}$$

The coefficient of $\mu$ gives the posterior mean:

$$m_n = v_n^2\!\left(\frac{m_0}{v_0^2} + \frac{n\bar{x}}{\sigma^2}\right)$$

Therefore:

$$\mu \mid \bar{x} \;\sim\; N\!\left(m_n,\; v_n^2\right)$$

The posterior mean is again a precision-weighted average of prior mean and data mean — a beautiful illustration of how Bayesian learning works.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Bayes Factor and Posterior Odds

We have two competing hypotheses $H_1$ and $H_2$ (e.g., a new drug is effective vs. ineffective). Their priors are $P(H_1)$ and $P(H_2)$ with $P(H_1) + P(H_2) = 1$.

Using Bayes' theorem for each hypothesis:

$$P(H_1 \mid D) = \frac{P(D \mid H_1)\,P(H_1)}{P(D)}, \quad P(H_2 \mid D) = \frac{P(D \mid H_2)\,P(H_2)}{P(D)}$$

Form the **posterior odds**:

$$\frac{P(H_1 \mid D)}{P(H_2 \mid D)} = \frac{P(D \mid H_1)}{P(D \mid H_2)} \cdot \frac{P(H_1)}{P(H_2)}$$

Define the **Bayes Factor**:

$$BF_{12} = \frac{P(D \mid H_1)}{P(D \mid H_2)} = \frac{\int P(D \mid \theta_1, H_1)\,P(\theta_1 \mid H_1)\,d\theta_1}{\int P(D \mid \theta_2, H_2)\,P(\theta_2 \mid H_2)\,d\theta_2}$$

So the **master identity** is:

$$\text{Posterior Odds} = \text{Bayes Factor} \times \text{Prior Odds}$$

Interpretation table (Jeffreys' scale, **must memorize**):

| $2 \log_{e}(BF_{10})$ | Evidence for $H_1$ |
|---|---|
| $0$ to $2$ | Barely worth mentioning |
| $2$ to $6$ | Positive |
| $6$ to $10$ | Strong |
| $> 10$ | Very strong |

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Decision Guide for Choosing Priors

| Data Type | Unknown Parameter | Recommended Conjugate Prior | Posterior Family |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bernoulli / Binomial | Proportion $p$ | $\text{Beta}(\alpha, \beta)$ | $\text{Beta}(\alpha + x,\; \beta + n-x)$ |
| Poisson counts | Rate $\lambda$ | $\text{Gamma}(\alpha, \beta)$ | $\text{Gamma}(\alpha + \sum x_i,\; \beta + n)$ |
| Exponential | Rate $\lambda$ | $\text{Gamma}(\alpha, \beta)$ | $\text{Gamma}(\alpha + n,\; \beta + \sum x_i)$ |
| Normal (known $\sigma^2$) | Mean $\mu$ | $N(m_0, v_0^2)$ | $N(m_n, v_n^2)$ |
| Normal (known $\mu$) | Variance $\sigma^2$ | Inverse-Gamma$(\alpha, \beta)$ | Inverse-Gamma$(\alpha + n/2,\; \beta + \sum (x_i - \mu)^2/2)$ |
| Multinomial | Probability vector | Dirichlet$(\boldsymbol{\alpha})$ | Dirichlet$(\boldsymbol{\alpha} + \mathbf{x})$ |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Bayesian Inference Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the full **functional flow** of a Bayesian analysis — how a researcher (or KTU student solving a numerical) moves from raw data to a decision. It is rendered as a process flow rather than a physical schematic, since Bayesian Inference is a computational/statistical workflow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Observed Data D] --> B[Step 1: Define Parameter of Interest theta]
    B --> C[Step 2: Specify Prior Distribution P theta]
    C --> D[Step 3: Formulate Likelihood P D given theta]
    D --> E[Step 4: Apply Bayes Theorem]
    E --> F[Step 5: Derive Posterior P theta given D]
    F --> G[Step 6a: Summarise Posterior]
    F --> H[Step 6b: Posterior Prediction]
    F --> I[Step 6c: Hypothesis Testing via Bayes Factor]
    G --> J[Output: Posterior Mean, Variance, Credible Interval]
    H --> K[Output: Predictive Distribution for New Observation]
    I --> L[Output: Posterior Odds and Decision]
    J --> M[Final Decision or Report]
    K --> M
    L --> M
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The data $D$ enters from the top-left.
- Every box corresponds to one **mandatory step** of a KTU-style Bayesian numerical problem.
- The three branches after *Posterior* (summarise / predict / test) are the three ways you can "use" the posterior — at least one of these appears in every KTU question.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Relationship Between Prior, Likelihood, and Posterior

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Prior P theta] -->|Multiply| M1[Numerator = Prior x Likelihood]
    L[Likelihood P D given theta] -->|Multiply| M1
    M1 --> N[Normalising Constant P D]
    N --> R[Posterior P theta given D]
    D2[Observed Data D] -->|Update trigger| R
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The posterior is the *normalised product* of prior and likelihood.
- The normalising constant $P(D)$ is just a number (independent of $\theta$) chosen so that the posterior integrates to 1.
- In many conjugate calculations, the form of $P(\theta \mid D)$ is recognised immediately from the kernel, so $P(D)$ need not be computed.

---

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Bayesian Hypothesis Test

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Two Hypotheses H1 and H2] --> P1[Specify Priors P H1 and P H2]
    P1 --> LF[Compute Marginal Likelihoods P D given H1 and P D given H2]
    LF --> BF[Compute Bayes Factor BF12 = P D given H1 over P D given H2]
    BF --> PO[Compute Posterior Odds = Prior Odds x BF12]
    PO --> D1{2 ln BF10 greater than 10}
    D1 -->|Yes| V1[Decision: Very strong evidence for H1]
    D1 -->|No| D2{2 ln BF10 between 2 and 6}
    D2 -->|Yes| V2[Decision: Positive evidence for H1]
    D2 -->|No| D3{2 ln BF10 between 0 and 2}
    D3 -->|Yes| V3[Decision: Barely worth mentioning]
    D3 -->|No| V4[Decision: Evidence favours H2 or inconclusive]
```

This decision tree is **directly tied to Jeffreys' scale** and frequently appears in KTU 14-mark numericals on hypothesis testing.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** Define Bayesian Inference. State Bayes' theorem and explain the role of the prior, likelihood, and posterior. **[3 Marks]**
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
Bayesian inference is a method of statistical inference that uses **Bayes' theorem** to update the probability of a hypothesis or parameter $\theta$ as more data $D$ is observed. Bayes' theorem is:

$$P(\theta \mid D) = \frac{P(D \mid \theta)\,P(\theta)}{P(D)}$$

- **Prior** $P(\theta)$ — belief about $\theta$ before seeing the data.
- **Likelihood** $P(D \mid \theta)$ — probability of the data given a specific $\theta$.
- **Posterior** $P(\theta \mid D)$ — updated belief after combining the prior with the observed data through the likelihood.
- $P(D)$ is the normalising constant (marginal evidence).

The posterior is the final, data-updated distribution used for inference and decision-making.

---

### **Q2.** What is a conjugate prior? Give one example relevant to biostatistics. **[3 Marks]**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
A **conjugate prior** is a prior distribution that, when combined with a particular likelihood, yields a posterior distribution belonging to the **same family** as the prior. This mathematical convenience allows closed-form posterior computation.

**Example:** For data arising from a Binomial($n,p$) distribution, the Beta($\alpha,\beta$) distribution is a conjugate prior for $p$. The resulting posterior is again a Beta distribution:

$$p \mid x \;\sim\; \text{Beta}(\alpha + x,\; \beta + n - x)$$

This Beta-Binomial conjugacy is widely used in biostatistics for estimating disease prevalence, sensitivity, and specificity.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** A diagnostic test for a disease has sensitivity 0.95 and specificity 0.90. If the prevalence of the disease in the population is 0.02, and a randomly selected person tests positive, find the probability that the person actually has the disease. Comment on the result. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** State and prove Bayes' theorem for continuous parameters. Define prior, likelihood, and posterior. Explain with a suitable example how the posterior is used for point estimation. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Solution to Q A (a)**

**Define events:**
- $D$ = person has the disease
- $\bar{D}$ = person does not have the disease
- $T^+$ = test is positive

**Given:**
- $P(T^+ \mid D) = 0.95$ (sensitivity)
- $P(T^- \mid \bar{D}) = 0.90 \Rightarrow P(T^+ \mid \bar{D}) = 0.10$
- $P(D) = 0.02,\quad P(\bar{D}) = 0.98$

**Apply Bayes' theorem:**

$$P(D \mid T^+) = \frac{P(T^+ \mid D)\,P(D)}{P(T^+)}$$

Compute the denominator using the law of total probability:

$$P(T^+) = P(T^+ \mid D)\,P(D) + P(T^+ \mid \bar{D})\,P(\bar{D})$$

$$P(T^+) = (0.95)(0.02) + (0.10)(0.98)$$

$$P(T^+) = 0.0190 + 0.0980 = 0.1170$$

Therefore:

$$P(D \mid T^+) = \frac{(0.95)(0.02)}{0.1170} = \frac{0.0190}{0.1170} \approx 0.1624$$

**Comment:** Even with a strongly positive test result, the probability the person actually has the disease is only about **16.24%**, because the disease is rare (low prevalence). This is a classic illustration of the **base-rate fallacy** in clinical diagnostics.

---

#### **Solution to Q A (b)**

**Statement of Bayes' Theorem (continuous case):**
Let $\theta$ be a continuous random parameter with prior density $P(\theta)$ and likelihood $P(D \mid \theta)$. Then the posterior density of $\theta$ given data $D$ is:

$$P(\theta \mid D) = \frac{P(D \mid \theta)\,P(\theta)}{\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} P(D \mid \theta)\,P(\theta)\,d\theta}$$

**Proof (from conditional probability axioms):**
By the definition of conditional probability:

$$P(\theta \mid D) = \frac{P(\theta \cap D)}{P(D)} \quad \text{and} \quad P(D \mid \theta) = \frac{P(D \cap \theta)}{P(\theta)}$$

Since $P(\theta \cap D) = P(D \cap \theta)$, we have $P(\theta \cap D) = P(D \mid \theta)\,P(\theta)$. Substituting:

$$P(\theta \mid D) = \frac{P(D \mid \theta)\,P(\theta)}{P(D)}$$

The marginal $P(D)$ is obtained by integrating the joint $P(D \mid \theta)\,P(\theta)$ over all $\theta$:

$$P(D) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} P(D \mid \theta)\,P(\theta)\,d\theta$$

Hence the result.

**Definitions:**
- **Prior $P(\theta)$:** Probability density assigned to $\theta$ before observing the data; encodes existing knowledge.
- **Likelihood $P(D \mid \theta)$:** Probability of the data, parameterised by $\theta$.
- **Posterior $P(\theta \mid D)$:** Updated density after observing $D$.

**Example for point estimation:**
Suppose $X_1,\dots,X_n \mid \mu \sim N(\mu,\sigma^2)$ with known $\sigma^2$, and prior $\mu \sim N(m_0, v_0^2)$. The posterior mean is:

$$E[\mu \mid D] = m_n = v_n^2\left(\frac{m_0}{v_0^2} + \frac{n\bar{x}}{\sigma^2}\right)$$

This posterior mean serves as a **Bayesian point estimate** of $\mu$. It is a precision-weighted average of prior mean $m_0$ and sample mean $\bar{x}$, automatically balancing prior knowledge and observed data.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — **Alternative Choice**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Suppose that the number of cases of a rare disease in a city follows a Poisson distribution with mean $\lambda$ per year. Five years of data show: 2, 4, 1, 3, 5 cases. Assuming a Gamma($\alpha=2,\beta=1$) prior for $\lambda$, derive the posterior distribution, compute the posterior mean and a 95% credible interval. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain the concept of a Bayes Factor. How is it used in Bayesian hypothesis testing? Discuss the Jeffreys' scale for interpreting Bayes Factors with a numerical example. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Solution to Q B (a)**

**Step 1 — Identify the data and sufficient statistic.**
- $n = 5$ observations, $\sum x_i = 2 + 4 + 1 + 3 + 5 = 15$.
- Likelihood (joint Poisson):

$$P(\text{data} \mid \lambda) = \prod_{i=1}^{5} \frac{e^{-\lambda}\lambda^{x_i}}{x_i!} = \frac{e^{-5\lambda}\lambda^{\sum x_i}}{\prod x_i!}$$

**Step 2 — Write the prior.**
The Gamma($\alpha=2,\beta=1$) prior is (using the rate parameterisation):

$$P(\lambda) = \frac{\beta^{\alpha}}{\Gamma(\alpha)}\,\lambda^{\alpha-1}e^{-\beta\lambda} = \frac{1^2}{\Gamma(2)}\,\lambda^{1}e^{-\lambda} = \lambda\,e^{-\lambda}, \quad \lambda > 0$$

**Step 3 — Multiply prior and likelihood (proportional form):**

$$P(\lambda \mid \text{data}) \propto P(\text{data} \mid \lambda)\,P(\lambda)$$

$$P(\lambda \mid \text{data}) \propto \left[e^{-5\lambda}\lambda^{15}\right]\cdot\left[\lambda\,e^{-\lambda}\right]$$

$$P(\lambda \mid \text{data}) \propto \lambda^{16}\,e^{-6\lambda}$$

**Step 4 — Recognise the kernel.**
The kernel of a Gamma($\alpha',\beta'$) density is $\lambda^{\alpha'-1}e^{-\beta'\lambda}$. Matching:

$$\alpha' - 1 = 16 \Rightarrow \alpha' = 17$$
$$\beta' = 6$$

So the posterior is:

$$\lambda \mid \text{data} \;\sim\; \text{Gamma}(17,\; 6)$$

**Step 5 — Posterior mean:**

$$E[\lambda \mid \text{data}] = \frac{\alpha'}{\beta'} = \frac{17}{6} \approx 2.833 \text{ cases per year}$$

**Step 6 — Posterior variance:**

$$\text{Var}(\lambda \mid \text{data}) = \frac{\alpha'}{\beta'^2} = \frac{17}{36} \approx 0.472$$

**Step 7 — 95% Credible Interval (using Gamma quantiles).**
Using standard Gamma tables (or software) for $\text{Gamma}(17, 6)$:
- 2.5% quantile: $\lambda_L \approx 1.55$
- 97.5% quantile: $\lambda_U \approx 4.27$

So the **95% equal-tail credible interval** for $\lambda$ is approximately **(1.55, 4.27) cases per year**.

> *Bayesian interpretation:* There is a 95% posterior probability that the true mean annual incidence $\lambda$ lies between 1.55 and 4.27 — a direct probabilistic statement, unlike the frequentist confidence interval.

---

#### **Solution to Q B (b)**

**Definition of Bayes Factor:**
The **Bayes Factor** $BF_{12}$ is the ratio of the marginal likelihoods of the data under two competing hypotheses $H_1$ and $H_2$:

$$BF_{12} = \frac{P(D \mid H_1)}{P(D \mid H_2)} = \frac{\int P(D \mid \theta_1, H_1)\,P(\theta_1 \mid H_1)\,d\theta_1}{\int P(D \mid \theta_2, H_2)\,P(\theta_2 \mid H_2)\,d\theta_2}$$

**Use in hypothesis testing:**
The relationship between posterior odds, prior odds, and Bayes Factor is:

$$\underbrace{\frac{P(H_1 \mid D)}{P(H_2 \mid D)}}_{\text{Posterior Odds}} \;=\; \underbrace{BF_{12}}_{\text{Data Evidence}} \;\times\; \underbrace{\frac{P(H_1)}{P(H_2)}}_{\text{Prior Odds}}$$

If $BF_{12} > 1$, the data favour $H_1$; if $BF_{12} < 1$, the data favour $H_2$.

**Jeffreys' Scale (1961):**

| $2 \ln BF_{10}$ | Strength of Evidence for $H_1$ |
|---|---|
| $< 0$ | Favours $H_2$ |
| $0$ to $2$ | Barely worth mentioning |
| $2$ to $6$ | Positive evidence |
| $6$ to $10$ | Strong evidence |
| $> 10$ | Very strong evidence |

**Numerical Example:**
Consider a clinical trial of a new drug:
- $H_1$: drug is effective (mean effect $\mu \sim N(0, 4)$)
- $H_2$: drug is ineffective ($\mu = 0$ exactly)

Suppose the trial observes $\bar{x} = 1.5$ with $n = 10$, $\sigma = 2$, so the standard error of $\bar{x}$ is $\sigma/\sqrt{n} = 0.632$.

Compute $P(D \mid H_1)$: integrate likelihood over the prior.

$$P(D \mid H_1) = \int P(D \mid \mu)\,P(\mu \mid H_1)\,d\mu$$

Since the prior on $\mu$ is $N(0,4)$ and the likelihood is $N(\mu, \sigma^2/n) = N(\mu, 0.4)$, the convolution gives a normal density at $\bar{x}=1.5$ with variance $4 + 0.4 = 4.4$:

$$P(D \mid H_1) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi(4.4)}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{1.5^2}{2(4.4)}\right) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi(4.4)}}\exp(-0.2557) \approx 0.1835$$

Compute $P(D \mid H_2) = P(D \mid \mu=0)$:

$$P(D \mid H_2) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi(0.4)}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{1.5^2}{2(0.4)}\right) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi(0.4)}}\exp(-2.8125) \approx 0.1261$$

Bayes Factor:

$$BF_{10} = \frac{0.1835}{0.1261} \approx 1.455$$

$$2 \ln BF_{10} = 2 \ln(1.455) \approx 2(0.3747) \approx 0.75$$

**Interpretation:** Since $0 \le 2 \ln BF_{10} < 2$, the evidence for $H_1$ (drug effective) is **"barely worth mentioning"** — the trial is inconclusive. A larger sample size would be needed to gather decisive evidence.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks in Bayesian Inference Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the normalising constant $P(D)$ in the proportional form:** Many students write $P(\theta \mid D) = P(D \mid \theta)\,P(\theta)$ without the $\propto$ symbol. KTU examiners explicitly deduct 1 mark for this.
> 2. **Confusing credible intervals with confidence intervals:** A 95% credible interval has the **direct** probability interpretation; a 95% confidence interval does **not**. Mixing them is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
> 3. **Posterior odds vs. Bayes Factor:** A common error is reporting the prior odds as the Bayes Factor. Always remember: **Posterior Odds = Prior Odds × Bayes Factor**.
> 4. **Conjugate prior parameterisation mismatch:** Gamma priors have two conventions — *shape-rate* and *shape-scale*. Always state which one you are using in a KTU answer.
> 5. **Skipping the comment step:** In diagnostic-test problems, after computing $P(D \mid T^+)$, the examiner expects a brief comment on the result (e.g., mention of the base-rate fallacy). Forgetting this costs 1 mark.
> 6. **Numerical slip in Beta-Binomial updates:** Confusing $\alpha + x$ with $\alpha + n$ — the correct posterior is $\text{Beta}(\alpha + x,\; \beta + n - x)$, not $\text{Beta}(\alpha + n,\; \beta + n)$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Bayes' theorem (continuous):** $P(\theta \mid D) = \dfrac{P(D \mid \theta)\,P(\theta)}{\int P(D \mid \theta)\,P(\theta)\,d\theta}$
- **Bayes' theorem (discrete):** Replace integral with sum.
- **Three ingredients:** Prior (belief before data) + Likelihood (data given parameter) → Posterior (belief after data).
- **Conjugate prior pairs to memorise:**
  - Beta ↔ Binomial
  - Gamma ↔ Poisson / Exponential
  - Normal ↔ Normal (known variance)
  - Dirichlet ↔ Multinomial
  - Inverse-Gamma ↔ Normal (known mean)
- **Posterior mean for Beta-Binomial:** $\dfrac{\alpha + x}{\alpha + \beta + n}$ — always a weighted average of prior mean and MLE.
- **Posterior mean for Normal-Normal:** precision-weighted average of $m_0$ and $\bar{x}$.
- **Posterior odds formula:** $\text{Posterior Odds} = \text{Prior Odds} \times \text{Bayes Factor}$.
- **Bayes Factor:** $BF_{12} = \dfrac{P(D \mid H_1)}{P(D \mid H_2)}$.
- **Jeffreys' scale thresholds:** $0, 2, 6, 10$ for $2 \ln BF_{10}$.
- **Credible interval** = direct Bayesian probability statement; **confidence interval** = long-run frequentist property.
- **Posterior predictive distribution:** $P(\tilde{x} \mid D) = \int P(\tilde{x} \mid \theta)\,P(\theta \mid D)\,d\theta$.
- **Non-informative priors to know:** Uniform on $\mu$ (Jeffrey's prior with $\sigma^2$ known), Beta(1/2, 1/2) for a Bernoulli proportion.
- **Total probability law** must always be used to compute $P(D)$ when required (e.g., diagnostic-test problems).
- **Always state the parameter of interest** at the start of the answer — KTU examiners deduct marks for vague framing.
- **Always interpret** the final number in one sentence (e.g., "Hence the posterior probability of disease given a positive test is approximately 16.24%, illustrating the base-rate fallacy.").

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ROC Curves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBMT414 - BIOSTATISTICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** ROC Curves # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) Curve** is a fundamental graphical diagnostic tool in biostatistics and clinical epidemiology that plots the **True Positive Rate (Sensitivity)** on the y-axis against the **False Positive Rate (1 − Specificity)** on the x-axis, across all possible threshold (cut-off) values of a binary classifier or diagnostic test. The curve was originally developed during World War II for radar signal detection and was later adopted into medical statistics for evaluating the discriminatory power of diagnostic tests. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** An ROC curve is a two-dimensional plot that illustrates the diagnostic ability of a binary classifier system as its discrimination threshold is varied. It is constructed by plotting **Sensitivity (True Positive Rate)** versus **1 − Specificity (False Positive Rate)** for every possible cut-off value of the diagnostic test. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a doctor using a **blood sugar test** to detect diabetes. The doctor must decide where to draw the "diabetic / non-diabetic" line (the cut-off threshold): - If the threshold is set **very low** (e.g., 70 mg/dL), the doctor will catch almost every diabetic patient (high **Sensitivity**) but will also wrongly flag many healthy people as diabetic (low **Specificity**). - If the threshold is set **very high** (e.g., 250 mg/dL), the doctor will rarely flag healthy people (high **Specificity**) but will miss many actual diabetic patients (low **Sensitivity**). The ROC curve simply **draws the entire trade-off** between these two errors for every possible threshold value. The **Area Under this Curve (AUC)** tells us how well the test discriminates between diseased and non-diseased individuals overall — a single number that summarizes the test's diagnostic accuracy. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** A perfect test has an AUC of **1.0**. A useless test (no better than flipping a coin) has an AUC of **0.5**, which is the diagonal reference line (the "line of no-discrimination"). ## 1.3 The 2×2 Confusion Matrix Foundation Every point on the ROC curve is derived from the **2×2 Confusion Matrix** (also called the **Contingency Table** or **Cross-Classification Table**): | Actual \ Predicted | Test Positive (Diseased) | Test Negative (Healthy) | Total | |---|---|---|---| | **Actually Diseased** | True Positive (**TP**) | False Negative (**FN**) | TP + FN | | **Actually Healthy** | False Positive (**FP**) | True Negative (**TN**) | FP + TN | | **Total** | TP + FP | FN + TN | N | ## 1.4 Core Diagnostic Metrics (with Standard Notation) | Metric | Formula | Meaning | |---|---|---| | **Sensitivity (True Positive Rate, TPR)** | $\dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Probability that a diseased person tests positive. Also called **Recall** or **Hit Rate**. | | **Specificity (True Negative Rate, TNR)** | $\dfrac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | Probability that a healthy person tests negative. | | **1 − Specificity (False Positive Rate, FPR)** | $\dfrac{FP}{TN + FP}$ | Probability that a healthy person is wrongly flagged as diseased. | | **Positive Predictive Value (PPV)** | $\dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Probability that a person testing positive is actually diseased. | | **Negative Predictive Value (NPV)** | $\dfrac{TN}{FN + TN}$ | Probability that a person testing negative is actually healthy. | | **Youden's J Statistic** | $J = \text{Sensitivity} + \text{Specificity} - 1$ | Single index identifying the optimal threshold. | | **Accuracy** | $\dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Overall proportion of correct predictions. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** The x-axis is always **FPR = 1 − Specificity**, NOT directly "Specificity". This is a very common student mistake that costs marks. The curve rises from (0,0) at the lower-left to (1,1) at the upper-right. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic The construction of an ROC curve follows a **deterministic, ordered procedure**: 1. **Step 1 — Collect Test Results:** Obtain continuous (or ordinal) diagnostic measurements from two groups: a group of confirmed diseased individuals (D+) and a group of confirmed non-diseased individuals (D−). 2. **Step 2 — Rank All Observations:** Sort the combined test results in **descending order** (highest to lowest). 3. **Step 3 — Apply Sequential Thresholds:** Treat each observed value as a potential cut-off. For every candidate cut-off, classify all observations as "test positive" (≥ threshold) or "test negative" (< threshold). 4. **Step 4 — Compute the 2×2 Matrix:** For each threshold, build the 2×2 contingency table and calculate the **Sensitivity (TPR)** and **1 − Specificity (FPR)**. 5. **Step 5 — Plot the Points:** Plot the (FPR, TPR) coordinate for each threshold on a Cartesian plane. 6. **Step 6 — Connect the Points:** Join consecutive plotted points with line segments to form the ROC curve. 7. **Step 7 — Reference Line:** Add the **45° diagonal line** from (0,0) to (1,1). This represents the "no-discrimination" reference (random classifier with AUC = **0.5**). 8. **Step 8 — Compute AUC:** Use the **trapezoidal rule** (or non-parametric Mann-Whitney U statistic) to estimate the area under the ROC curve. ## 2.2 Geometric Interpretation of the Curve | Curve Position | Interpretation | Diagnostic Value | |---|---|---| | Curve hugs the **top-left corner** | Excellent discrimination | Test has high Sensitivity AND high Specificity simultaneously | | Curve lies along the **diagonal** (y = x) | No discrimination | Test is no better than random chance | | Curve falls **below the diagonal** | Test performs worse than chance | Labels may be reversed; investigate | | Curve forms a **right-angle at (0,1)** | Perfect test | AUC = 1.0; 100% Sensitivity AND 100% Specificity | ## 2.3 The AUC (Area Under the Curve) — The "Headline Number" The **AUC** is the single most important summary statistic of an ROC curve. It has a powerful **probabilistic interpretation**: $$P(\text{test score of a randomly chosen diseased person} > \text{test score of a randomly chosen non-diseased person}) = \text{AUC}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Probabilistic Meaning of AUC:** If you randomly pick one diseased person and one non-diseased person, the AUC is the probability that the diagnostic test will assign a **higher score** to the diseased person than to the non-diseased person. ## 2.4 The Mann-Whitney U Statistic Connection For a non-parametric (empirical) ROC curve, the AUC is mathematically equivalent to the **Wilcoxon-Mann-Whitney statistic**: $$\text{AUC} = \frac{U}{n_1 \cdot n_2}$$ where $U$ is the Mann-Whitney U statistic, $n_1$ is the number of diseased subjects, and $n_2$ is the number of non-diseased subjects. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Concept | Formula | Units / Range | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | Sensitivity (TPR) | $TPR = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Recall, Hit Rate | | Specificity (TNR) | $TNR = \dfrac{TN}{TN+FP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | True Negative Rate | | False Positive Rate | $FPR = 1 - TNR = \dfrac{FP}{TN+FP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | x-axis of ROC | | Youden's Index | $J = TPR + TNR - 1$ | $[-1, 1]$ | Optimal at **max J** | | AUC (Trapezoidal Rule) | $AUC = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \dfrac{(FPR_{i+1} - FPR_i)(TPR_{i+1} + TPR_i)}{2}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Non-parametric estimator | | AUC (Mann-Whitney) | $AUC = \dfrac{U}{n_1 n_2}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Where $U$ is Mann-Whitney U statistic | | Standard Error of AUC (Hanley-McNeil) | $SE(AUC) = \sqrt{\dfrac{AUC(1-AUC) + (n_1-1)(Q_1 - AUC^2) + (n_2-1)(Q_2 - AUC^2)}{n_1 n_2}}$ | — | $Q_1, Q_2$ are intermediate quantities | | Likelihood Ratio Positive | $LR^+ = \dfrac{TPR}{FPR}$ | $[1, \infty)$ | Higher = better rule-in test | | Likelihood Ratio Negative | $LR^- = \dfrac{FNR}{TNR}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Lower = better rule-out test | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Constraint Check:** For a valid diagnostic test, the curve MUST lie **above the diagonal** (i.e., AUC ≥ **0.5**). A curve dipping below the diagonal implies the test's interpretation is inverted. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering & Medical Utility ROC curves are indispensable in: - **Clinical Diagnostics:** Comparing two biomarkers (e.g., PSA vs. PCA3 for prostate cancer). - **Machine Learning:** Evaluating binary classifiers (Logistic Regression, SVM, Random Forest, Neural Networks). - **Radiology & Imaging:** Setting optimal cut-offs for CAD (Computer-Aided Detection) systems. - **Public Health Surveillance:** Threshold-setting for disease outbreak alerts. - **Pharmacovigilance:** Signal detection in adverse drug reaction databases. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Area Under the ROC Curve (Trapezoidal Rule) **Problem Setup:** Suppose we have $k$ distinct threshold values, generating $k$ points $(FPR_i, TPR_i)$ for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, k$. We want the area enclosed between the ROC curve, the x-axis, the line $FPR = 0$, and the line $FPR = 1$. **Step 1 — Visualize the Curve as a Series of Trapezoids:** Between any two consecutive points $(FPR_i, TPR_i)$ and $(FPR_{i+1}, TPR_{i+1})$, the ROC segment forms a **trapezoid** with: - Parallel sides of length $TPR_i$ and $TPR_{i+1}$ (vertical heights). - Width $(FPR_{i+1} - FPR_i)$ (horizontal distance). **Step 2 — Area of a Single Trapezoid:** $$A_i = \frac{1}{2} \times \left( TPR_i + TPR_{i+1} \right) \times \left( FPR_{i+1} - FPR_i \right)$$ **Step 3 — Sum Over All Trapezoids:** The total AUC is the sum of all trapezoidal areas: $$AUC = \sum_{i=1}^{k-1} A_i = \sum_{i=1}^{k-1} \frac{1}{2} \left( TPR_i + TPR_{i+1} \right) \left( FPR_{i+1} - FPR_i \right)$$ **Step 4 — Final Compact Form:** $$ \begin{aligned} AUC &= \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{k-1} \left( TPR_i + TPR_{i+1} \right) \left( FPR_{i+1} - FPR_i \right) \\[6pt] &= \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{k-1} \left[ TPR_i \cdot \Delta FPR_i + TPR_{i+1} \cdot \Delta FPR_i \right] \end{aligned} $$ where $\Delta FPR_i = FPR_{i+1} - FPR_i$. **Conversion Logic Explanation:** - **Why trapezoids?** The ROC curve between two operating points is approximated as a straight line — this is a piecewise-linear approximation. The exact area is the integral; the trapezoid gives a discrete approximation. - **Why divide by 2?** The arithmetic mean of the two parallel sides, multiplied by the distance between them, gives the trapezoid area. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Connection Between AUC and the Mann-Whitney U Statistic **Statement:** For empirical (non-parametric) ROC curves, $AUC = \dfrac{U}{n_1 n_2}$. **Step 1 — Define the Mann-Whitney U Statistic:** Given two independent samples: - Sample A (diseased): $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_{n_1}$ - Sample B (non-diseased): $Y_1, Y_2, \ldots, Y_{n_2}$ The Mann-Whitney U statistic counts the number of pairs $(X_i, Y_j)$ where the diseased person's score **exceeds** the non-diseased person's score: $$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n_1} \sum_{j=1}^{n_2} \mathbb{1}_{\{X_i > Y_j\}}$$ where $\mathbb{1}_{\{\cdot\}}$ is the indicator function (1 if condition holds, 0 otherwise). **Step 2 — Express the Indicator Function Using FPR and TPR:** For any candidate threshold $c$: $$\mathbb{1}_{\{X_i > c\}} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } X_i > c \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} \quad \text{(counts as a true positive)}$$ $$\mathbb{1}_{\{Y_j > c\}} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } Y_j > c \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} \quad \text{(counts as a false positive)}$$ **Step 3 — TPR as a Fraction of the Diseased Sample Exceeding Threshold:** $$TPR(c) = \frac{1}{n_1} \sum_{i=1}^{n_1} \mathbb{1}_{\{X_i > c\}}$$ **Step 4 — FPR as a Fraction of the Non-Diseased Sample Exceeding Threshold:** $$FPR(c) = \frac{1}{n_2} \sum_{j=1}^{n_2} \mathbb{1}_{\{Y_j > c\}}$$ **Step 5 — Recognize the Integral Identity:** The probability that a diseased score exceeds a non-diseased score can be written as an integral of TPR over the distribution of FPR: $$P(X > Y) = \int_0^1 TPR(c) \, dFPR(c)$$ **Step 6 — Discrete Approximation (Empirical AUC):** The integral is approximated by summing over all distinct thresholds: $$AUC = \sum_{i=1}^{k-1} \frac{1}{2} \left( TPR_i + TPR_{i+1} \right) \left( FPR_{i+1} - FPR_i \right)$$ **Step 7 — Conclude the Equivalence:** It is a classical result (Bamber 1975, Hanley-McNeil 1982) that this discrete sum equals exactly $U / (n_1 n_2)$: $$\boxed{AUC = \frac{U}{n_1 n_2}}$$ **Conversion Logic Explanation:** - The integral $\int TPR \, dFPR$ geometrically computes the area under the ROC curve. - The Mann-Whitney U statistic is a **rank-based** non-parametric measure of separation between the two distributions. - The equivalence arises because the empirical CDF of FPR is a step function jumping by $1/n_2$ at each observed non-diseased value. --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Constructing an ROC Curve from a Dataset **Dataset:** A new biomarker for heart disease is measured in 5 diseased and 5 non-diseased patients (scores in arbitrary units): | Patient | Status | Score | |---|---|---| | P1 | Diseased | 95 | | P2 | Diseased | 80 | | P3 | Non-diseased | 70 | | P4 | Diseased | 60 | | P5 | Non-diseased | 50 | | P6 | Non-diseased | 40 | | P7 | Diseased | 35 | | P8 | Non-diseased | 20 | | P9 | Diseased | 15 | | P10 | Non-diseased | 10 | **Step 1 — Sort all 10 scores in descending order:** | Rank | Score | Status | |---|---|---| | 1 | 95 | Diseased | | 2 | 80 | Diseased | | 3 | 70 | Non-diseased | | 4 | 60 | Diseased | | 5 | 50 | Non-diseased | | 6 | 40 | Non-diseased | | 7 | 35 | Diseased | | 8 | 20 | Non-diseased | | 9 | 15 | Diseased | | 10 | 10 | Non-diseased | **Step 2 — Initialize: Start at (FPR, TPR) = (0, 0).** **Step 3 — Apply thresholds one at a time (using each score as the cut-off, classifying scores ≥ threshold as "positive" for disease):** **Threshold = 95** (only P1 ≥ 95): - TP = 1, FN = 4, FP = 0, TN = 5 - TPR = 1/5 = 0.20, FPR = 0/5 = 0.00 - Point: (0.00, 0.20) **Threshold = 80** (P1, P2 ≥ 80): - TP = 2, FN = 3, FP = 0, TN = 5 - TPR = 2/5 = 0.40, FPR = 0/5 = 0.00 - Point: (0.00, 0.40) **Threshold = 70** (P1, P2, P3 ≥ 70): - TP = 2, FN = 3, FP = 1, TN = 4 - TPR = 2/5 = 0.40, FPR = 1/5 = 0.20 - Point: (0.20, 0.40) **Threshold = 60** (P1, P2, P3, P4 ≥ 60): - TP = 3, FN = 2, FP = 1, TN = 4 - TPR = 3/5 = 0.60, FPR = 1/5 = 0.20 - Point: (0.20, 0.60) **Threshold = 50** (P1, P2, P3, P4, P5 ≥ 50): - TP = 3, FN = 2, FP = 2, TN = 3 - TPR = 3/5 = 0.60, FPR = 2/5 = 0.40 - Point: (0.40, 0.60) **Threshold = 40** (P1–P6 ≥ 40): - TP = 3, FN = 2, FP = 3, TN = 2 - TPR = 3/5 = 0.60, FPR = 3/5 = 0.60 - Point: (0.60, 0.60) **Threshold = 35** (P1–P7 ≥ 35): - TP = 4, FN = 1, FP = 3, TN = 2 - TPR = 4/5 = 0.80, FPR = 3/5 = 0.60 - Point: (0.60, 0.80) **Threshold = 20** (P1–P8 ≥ 20): - TP = 4, FN = 1, FP = 4, TN = 1 - TPR = 4/5 = 0.80, FPR = 4/5 = 0.80 - Point: (0.80, 0.80) **Threshold = 15** (P1–P9 ≥ 15): - TP = 5, FN = 0, FP = 4, TN = 1 - TPR = 5/5 = 1.00, FPR = 4/5 = 0.80 - Point: (0.80, 1.00) **Threshold = 10** (P1–P10 ≥ 10): - TP = 5, FN = 0, FP = 5, TN = 0 - TPR = 5/5 = 1.00, FPR = 5/5 = 1.00 - Point: (1.00, 1.00) **Step 4 — Compute AUC using the Trapezoidal Rule:** $$ \begin{aligned} AUC &= \frac{1}{2} \Big[ (0.20)(0.00 - 0.00) + (0.40 + 0.20)(0.20 - 0.00) + (0.60 + 0.40)(0.20 - 0.20) \\ &\quad + (0.60 + 0.60)(0.40 - 0.20) + (0.60 + 0.60)(0.60 - 0.40) + (0.80 + 0.60)(0.60 - 0.60) \\ &\quad + (0.80 + 0.80)(0.80 - 0.60) + (1.00 + 0.80)(0.80 - 0.80) + (1.00 + 1.00)(1.00 - 0.80) \Big] \\[6pt] &= \frac{1}{2} \Big[ 0 + 0.12 + 0 + 0.24 + 0.24 + 0 + 0.32 + 0 + 0.40 \Big] \\[6pt] &= \frac{1}{2} \times 1.32 \\[6pt] &= 0.66 \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** $AUC = \mathbf{0.66}$, indicating **moderate discriminatory power** of the biomarker. **Step 5 — Find Optimal Threshold using Youden's Index:** Calculate $J = TPR + TNR - 1 = TPR - FPR$ for each operating point: | FPR | TPR | TNR | J = TPR + TNR − 1 | |---|---|---|---| | 0.00 | 0.40 | 1.00 | **0.40** ← Maximum | | 0.20 | 0.60 | 0.80 | 0.40 | | 0.40 | 0.60 | 0.60 | 0.20 | | 0.60 | 0.80 | 0.40 | 0.20 | | 0.80 | 1.00 | 0.20 | 0.20 | **Optimal threshold = 80** (Tied at 0.40, but 80 has zero false positives, so it's the clinically preferred choice). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: ROC Curve Construction Pipeline The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete data-flow architecture for generating an ROC curve from raw diagnostic data. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Diagnostic Dataset] --> B[Group A: Diseased Subjects n1] A --> C[Group B: Non-Diseased Subjects n2] B --> D[Merge and Sort Scores Descending] C --> D D --> E[Initialize: TP=0, FP=0, FN=n1, TN=n2] E --> F[Set Current Threshold = next unique score] F --> G[Classify all observations at this threshold] G --> H[Build 2x2 Confusion Matrix] H --> I[Compute TPR and FPR] I --> J[Plot Coordinate FPR, TPR] J --> K{More thresholds remain?} K -- Yes --> F K -- No --> L[Connect all points piecewise linearly] L --> M[Add 45 degree Reference Line] L --> N[Apply Trapezoidal Rule] N --> O[Compute AUC value] L --> P[Compute Youden J Statistic for each point] P --> Q[Identify Optimal Threshold at max J] O --> R[Final ROC Report] Q --> R M --> R ``` ## 4.2 Conceptual Schematic: ROC Curve Anatomy ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Origin 0,0] -->|Threshold Maximum| B[No positives classified] B --> C[Operating Points sweep curve] C --> D[Top Left Corner 0,1
Perfect Test] D --> E[Bottom Right Corner 1,0] E --> F[Diagonal Reference Line y=x
Random Classifier AUC=0.5] F --> G[End 1,1
All flagged positive] C -->|AUC integral| H[AUC shaded region
Diagnostic Power] H --> I[Higher AUC equals Better Test] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Decision Tree: Interpreting a Given AUC Value ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Computed AUC value] --> B{AUC equals 1.0} B -- Yes --> C[Perfect Discrimination] B -- No --> D{AUC between 0.9 and 1.0} D -- Yes --> E[Excellent Discrimination] D -- No --> F{AUC between 0.8 and 0.9} F -- Yes --> G[Good Discrimination] F -- No --> H{AUC between 0.7 and 0.8} H -- Yes --> I[Fair Discrimination] H -- No --> J{AUC between 0.6 and 0.7} J -- Yes --> K[Poor Discrimination] J -- No --> L{AUC between 0.5 and 0.6} L -- Yes --> M[Fail or Useless Test] L -- No --> N{AUC less than 0.5} N -- Yes --> O[Test performs worse than chance
Check label assignment] ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram: Two-ROC Comparison Use-Case ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Two Diagnostic Tests] --> B[Test 1: Existing Standard] A --> C[Test 2: New Biomarker] B --> D[Generate ROC Curve 1] C --> E[Generate ROC Curve 2] D --> F[Compute AUC 1 and 95 percent CI] E --> G[Compute AUC 2 and 95 percent CI] F --> H[DeLong Test for AUC Comparison] G --> H H --> I{AUC1 significantly greater than AUC2} I -- Yes --> J[Adopt Test 1] I -- No --> K{Adopt Test 2 if cost effective} K -- Yes --> L[Adopt Test 2] K -- No --> M[Retain Status Quo] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]** > **Define an ROC curve. What does the area under the ROC curve (AUC) represent in a diagnostic testing context?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** An **ROC (Receiver Operating Characteristic) curve** is a graphical plot that illustrates the diagnostic performance of a binary classifier across all possible threshold values. The y-axis represents the **True Positive Rate (Sensitivity)** and the x-axis represents the **False Positive Rate (1 − Specificity)**. The **Area Under the Curve (AUC)** is a single scalar value summarizing the overall discriminatory ability of the test. It ranges from **0.5** (no discrimination, equivalent to random guessing) to **1.0** (perfect discrimination). Probabilistically, the AUC equals the probability that the diagnostic test will assign a higher score to a randomly chosen diseased individual than to a randomly chosen non-diseased individual. --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]** > **Differentiate between Sensitivity and Specificity. Why is the ROC curve plotted with 1 − Specificity on the x-axis rather than Specificity itself?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | Sensitivity | Specificity | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | Probability that a truly diseased person tests positive | Probability that a truly healthy person tests negative | | **Formula** | $\dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | $\dfrac{TN}{TN+FP}$ | | **Also Called** | True Positive Rate (TPR), Recall | True Negative Rate (TNR) | | **Range** | 0 to 1 | 0 to 1 | The ROC curve is plotted with **1 − Specificity** (i.e., the False Positive Rate) on the x-axis, **not Specificity**, for the following reasons: 1. **Convention:** It is a universally accepted standard in clinical biostatistics; using Specificity directly would invert the curve and produce a confusing plot. 2. **Geometric Utility:** As the threshold decreases, the FPR increases monotonically from 0 to 1, allowing a clean, monotonically increasing x-axis — this is essential for the AUC to be a well-defined integral. 3. **Diagnostic Interpretation:** The FPR directly quantifies the **cost of false alarms**, which is the clinically relevant quantity in disease screening. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ### **Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** > **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** With a neat sketch, explain the construction of an ROC curve. Clearly label the axes, the diagonal reference line, and identify the regions corresponding to a perfect test, a useless test, and a good test. > **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** A new cardiac troponin assay was evaluated on 200 patients. Of these, 100 were confirmed to have myocardial infarction (MI) by gold-standard angiography. The test gave 85 true positives, 80 true negatives, 15 false positives, and 20 false negatives. Compute the Sensitivity, Specificity, AUC (using a single-threshold trapezoidal approximation), and Youden's Index. Identify the optimal operating point. --- #### **Model Solution for Question A:** **Part (a) — Construction of ROC Curve [7 Marks]:** **Step 1 [2 Marks — Axes & Definitions]:** - The **x-axis** represents the **False Positive Rate (FPR = 1 − Specificity)**, ranging from 0 to 1. - The **y-axis** represents the **True Positive Rate (TPR = Sensitivity)**, ranging from 0 to 1. - A **45° diagonal reference line** is drawn from (0,0) to (1,1). This line represents a **random classifier** (no discrimination); its AUC = **0.5**. **Step 2 [2 Marks — Curve Construction]:** - The curve is constructed by **plotting (FPR, TPR) pairs** computed at each candidate threshold. - Consecutive points are connected with line segments. - The curve is **monotonically non-decreasing** in the TPR direction (as the threshold is relaxed, sensitivity can only stay the same or rise). **Step 3 [3 Marks — Regions of Interest]:** | Region | Description | |---|---| | **Diagonal Line (y = x)** | Useless test (AUC = 0.5); same as a coin flip | | **Curve close to top-left corner** | Good test (high AUC, e.g., 0.85–0.95) | | **Curve passing through (0, 1)** | Perfect test (AUC = 1.0) | | **Curve below diagonal** | Pathological test (labels are inverted) | **Sketch:** ``` TPR 1.0 |• ← Perfect test (0, 1) | •_____ ← Good test | •____ 0.5 |____________•___ ← Diagonal (useless) | •__ 0.0 |________________•→ FPR 0.0 1.0 ``` **Part (b) — Numerical Computation [7 Marks]:** **Step 1 [1 Mark] — Tabulate the 2×2 Confusion Matrix:** | Actual \ Predicted | MI Positive | MI Negative | Total | |---|---|---|---| | **MI Present** | TP = 85 | FN = 20 | 100 | | **MI Absent** | FP = 15 | TN = 80 | 100 | | **Total** | 100 | 100 | 200 | **Step 2 [2 Marks] — Sensitivity and Specificity:** $$ \text{Sensitivity} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{85}{85 + 20} = \frac{85}{105} = 0.8095 \approx 0.81 $$ $$ \text{Specificity} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = \frac{80}{80 + 15} = \frac{80}{95} = 0.8421 \approx 0.84 $$ **Step 3 [2 Marks] — False Positive Rate and Operating Point:** $$ FPR = 1 - \text{Specificity} = 1 - 0.8421 = 0.1579 \approx 0.16 $$ Single operating point: **(FPR, TPR) = (0.16, 0.81)**. **Step 4 [1 Mark] — Youden's Index:** $$ J = \text{Sensitivity} + \text{Specificity} - 1 = 0.81 + 0.84 - 1 = 0.65 $$ **Step 5 [1 Mark] — AUC (Single-Threshold Trapezoidal Approximation):** With only one operating point, the "curve" is implicitly bounded by the diagonal at the endpoints: $$ \begin{aligned} AUC &\approx \frac{1}{2} \big[ (0 + 0.81)(0.16 - 0) + (0.81 + 1)(1 - 0.16) \big] \\ &= \frac{1}{2} \big[ 0.1296 + 1.5444 \big] \\ &= \frac{1}{2} \times 1.6740 \\ &= 0.837 \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answer [Part b]:** - **Sensitivity = 0.81** - **Specificity = 0.84** - **AUC ≈ 0.84** (good discrimination) - **Youden's J = 0.65** - **Optimal operating point: (FPR, TPR) = (0.16, 0.81)** — this single threshold is optimal. --- ### **Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – June 2023]** > **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Derive the trapezoidal rule expression for the Area Under the ROC Curve. Explain the geometric interpretation of each term. > **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Two screening tests for a rare disease were compared. Test A yielded an AUC of 0.91 (95% CI: 0.86–0.96), while Test B yielded an AUC of 0.84 (95% CI: 0.78–0.90). Using a significance level of α = 0.05, determine which test is statistically superior and explain the role of the DeLong test in this comparison. --- #### **Model Solution for Question B:** **Part (a) — Trapezoidal Rule Derivation [7 Marks]:** **Step 1 [1 Mark] — Setup:** Let the empirical ROC curve pass through $k$ points: $P_i = (FPR_i, TPR_i)$ for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, k$, where $FPR_1 = 0, TPR_1 = 0$ and $FPR_k = 1, TPR_k = 1$. **Step 2 [2 Marks] — Single Trapezoid Area:** Between $P_i$ and $P_{i+1}$, the area of the trapezoid is: $$A_i = \frac{1}{2}(TPR_i + TPR_{i+1})(FPR_{i+1} - FPR_i)$$ **Step 3 [2 Marks] — Summation:** $$ \begin{aligned} AUC &= \sum_{i=1}^{k-1} A_i \\ &= \sum_{i=1}^{k-1} \frac{1}{2}(TPR_i + TPR_{i+1})(FPR_{i+1} - FPR_i) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 [2 Marks] — Geometric Interpretation:** - The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ is the average-height of the two parallel sides of the trapezoid. - The term $(TPR_i + TPR_{i+1})$ represents the **sum of the two TPR values** bounding the trapezoid vertically. - The term $(FPR_{i+1} - FPR_i)$ represents the **horizontal width** (FPR interval) of the trapezoid. - The summation adds up these piecewise trapezoidal approximations, yielding a piecewise-linear estimate of the integral $\int_0^1 TPR \, dFPR$. --- **Part (b) — Comparing Two AUCs [7 Marks]:** **Step 1 [1 Mark] — Confidence Interval Inspection:** - Test A: AUC = 0.91, 95% CI = (0.86, 0.96) - Test B: AUC = 0.84, 95% CI = (0.78, 0.90) **Step 2 [2 Marks] — Overlap Analysis:** The two confidence intervals are: - Test A: [0.86, 0.96] - Test B: [0.78, 0.90] These intervals **overlap** in the range [0.86, 0.90]. The overlap means the observed difference **may not** be statistically significant based on CI inspection alone; a formal hypothesis test is required. **Step 3 [2 Marks] — The DeLong Test:** The **DeLong test** (DeLong, DeLong & Clarke-Pearson, 1988) is a non-parametric method specifically designed to compare two correlated ROC curves (i.e., when the same subjects are tested with both methods). It works by: 1. Computing the **variance-covariance matrix** of the two AUC estimates using placement values (akin to influence functions). 2. Using a **z-statistic** for the difference: $$ z = \frac{AUC_A - AUC_B}{\sqrt{Var(AUC_A) + Var(AUC_B) - 2 \cdot Cov(AUC_A, AUC_B)}} $$ 3. Comparing the z-value to a standard normal distribution to obtain a p-value. **Step 4 [2 Marks] — Conclusion (assumed scenario):** - **Null Hypothesis $H_0$:** $AUC_A = AUC_B$ - **Alternative Hypothesis $H_1$:** $AUC_A > AUC_B$ - Suppose the DeLong test yields $p = 0.012 < 0.05$. - **Decision:** Reject $H_0$. **Test A is statistically significantly superior** to Test B at $\alpha = 0.05$. **Final Answer [Part b]:** Test A (AUC = 0.91) is the **statistically superior diagnostic test** based on the DeLong test, despite the slight CI overlap. The DeLong test is essential because simple CI overlap is not a valid test of significance for correlated ROC curves. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:** > 1. **Plotting Specificity on the x-axis instead of 1 − Specificity (FPR).** This is the **#1 mistake** and costs 2–3 marks instantly. Always state FPR = 1 − Specificity explicitly. > 2. **Confusing AUC with accuracy.** AUC measures **discriminatory ability** (ranking), not classification accuracy at a single threshold. > 3. **Forgetting the diagonal reference line.** Any ROC sketch without the y = x reference line is incomplete and loses 1 mark. > 4. **Using the trapezoidal rule incorrectly** — students often add instead of average the two TPR values. > 5. **Youden's Index formula error:** The correct form is $J = \text{Sensitivity} + \text{Specificity} - 1$, **not** $J = \text{Sensitivity} - \text{Specificity}$. > 6. **Forgetting to state the probabilistic interpretation of AUC** — the answer "AUC is the area under the curve" is incomplete. Always add: "AUC equals the probability that the test will rank a randomly chosen diseased person higher than a randomly chosen non-diseased person." --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **ROC = Receiver Operating Characteristic curve**, a fundamental tool for evaluating **binary diagnostic tests** and classifiers. - **Y-axis:** Sensitivity (TPR) = $\dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$. **X-axis:** 1 − Specificity (FPR) = $\dfrac{FP}{TN+FP}$. - **Diagonal line** ($y = x$): represents a **random classifier**; AUC = 0.5. - **Top-left corner** (0, 1): **perfect classifier**; AUC = 1.0. - **AUC range:** 0.5 (useless) to 1.0 (perfect). Below 0.5 implies an **inverted test**. - **Probabilistic meaning of AUC:** $P(\text{diseased score} > \text{non-diseased score})$. - **Trapezoidal rule for AUC:** $AUC = \dfrac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{k-1}(TPR_i + TPR_{i+1})(FPR_{i+1} - FPR_i)$. - **AUC ↔ Mann-Whitney U statistic:** $AUC = \dfrac{U}{n_1 n_2}$. - **Youden's Index:** $J = \text{Sensitivity} + \text{Specificity} - 1$; **optimal threshold = threshold that maximizes J**. - **DeLong test:** the standard non-parametric method for **comparing two correlated ROC curves** derived from the same subjects. - **Likelihood Ratios** $LR^+ = \dfrac{TPR}{FPR}$ and $LR^- = \dfrac{FNR}{TNR}$ are derived from the ROC curve and quantify **rule-in** and **rule-out** clinical utility. - **Curve monotonicity:** TPR never decreases as FPR increases (the curve is **monotonically non-decreasing**). - **Thresholding rule:** Lower threshold ⇒ higher Sensitivity, lower Specificity. Higher threshold ⇒ lower Sensitivity, higher Specificity. - **Confidence intervals for AUC** are essential — a high AUC with a wide CI may not be statistically distinguishable from a lower AUC.

topics.title: Random Variables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT414 - BIOSTATISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Random Variables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Random Variable (RV)** is a real-valued function defined on the sample space $\Omega$ of a random experiment. Formally, it is a function $X : \Omega \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ that assigns a unique real number $X(\omega)$ to every outcome $\omega \in \Omega$.

In the context of **Biostatistics**, random variables are used to quantify biological measurements such as:
- Heart rate (beats per minute) of a patient
- Hemoglobin concentration (g/dL) in blood
- Number of patients recovering from a disease
- Survival time (in months) after a clinical intervention

Mathematically, the random variable $X$ partitions the sample space into measurable sets:

$$X(\omega) = x \quad \text{where} \quad \omega \in \Omega$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A random variable is NOT a variable in the algebraic sense. It is a *function* whose value depends on the outcome of a random experiment. This conceptual distinction is frequently tested in KTU examinations.

## 1.2 Types of Random Variables

### A. Discrete Random Variable (DRV)
A random variable $X$ is **discrete** if it can assume only a finite or countably infinite set of values.

**Examples in Biostatistics:**
- Number of patients cured in a clinical trial
- Number of bacteria colonies on a petri dish
- Number of adverse drug reactions in a sample

### B. Continuous Random Variable (CRV)
A random variable $X$ is **continuous** if it can assume any value within one or more intervals on the real line.

**Examples in Biostatistics:**
- Blood pressure (mmHg) of a patient
- Cholesterol level (mg/dL) in serum
- Body temperature ($^\circ$C)
- Height and weight measurements

### C. Mixed Random Variable
A random variable that has both discrete and continuous components.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Discrete variables are usually *counted*, while continuous variables are usually *measured*. This is one of the highest-weightage conceptual questions in KTU Module 2.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are a **public health researcher** conducting a study on blood pressure among adults in Kerala. You randomly select 100 individuals and measure their systolic blood pressure (SBP).

- The **experiment**: Measuring SBP of 100 adults
- The **sample space** $\Omega$: All possible sets of 100 SBP values
- The **random variable** $X$: The SBP of the *first* selected adult
- The **value** $x$: A specific number like $128$ mmHg

Think of $X$ as a **"rule"** or **"machine"** that takes an outcome (a person) and outputs a number (their SBP). The randomness is in the *person chosen*, not in the rule itself. This is analogous to a function in mathematics — the input is random, but the mapping is deterministic.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for KTU Exams:** "Random Variable = Random Input + Variable Output". The *input* is random (the outcome), and the *output* is the numerical value we observe.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Probability Mass Function (PMF) — For Discrete RV

The **PMF** $P_X(x)$ gives the probability that a discrete random variable $X$ takes the exact value $x$:

$$P_X(x) = P(X = x) = P(\{\omega \in \Omega : X(\omega) = x\})$$

### Properties of PMF

For any discrete RV $X$ with PMF $P_X(x)$:

1. **Non-negativity:** $P_X(x) \geq 0$ for all $x$
2. **Normalization:** $\sum_{x} P_X(x) = 1$
3. **Probability of an event:** $P(X \in A) = \sum_{x \in A} P_X(x)$

## 2.2 Probability Density Function (PDF) — For Continuous RV

The **PDF** $f_X(x)$ is a non-negative integrable function such that for any interval $[a, b]$:

$$P(a \leq X \leq b) = \int_{a}^{b} f_X(x) \, dx$$

### Properties of PDF

1. **Non-negativity:** $f_X(x) \geq 0$ for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$
2. **Total area equals unity:** $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_X(x) \, dx = 1$
3. **Point probability is zero:** $P(X = x) = 0$ for any specific $x$
4. **CDF as integral:** $F_X(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f_X(u) \, du$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Conceptual Trap:** For a continuous RV, $P(X = x) = 0$ does NOT mean the event is impossible. It means the probability is concentrated in a measure-zero set. This is a common 3-mark question.

## 2.3 Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) — Universal

The **CDF** $F_X(x)$ is defined for both discrete and continuous RVs as:

$$F_X(x) = P(X \leq x)$$

### Properties of CDF

1. **Monotonically non-decreasing:** $F_X(x_1) \leq F_X(x_2)$ for $x_1 \leq x_2$
2. **Boundary values:** $F_X(-\infty) = 0$ and $F_X(+\infty) = 1$
3. **Right-continuous:** $\lim_{h \to 0^+} F_X(x + h) = F_X(x)$
4. **Range:** $0 \leq F_X(x) \leq 1$ for all $x$

### Relationship Between CDF and PMF/PDF

For **discrete** RV:
$$F_X(x) = \sum_{x_i \leq x} P_X(x_i)$$

For **continuous** RV:
$$f_X(x) = \frac{d}{dx} F_X(x) \quad \text{wherever the derivative exists}$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Discrete RV | Continuous RV |
|---|---|---|
| Defining Function | PMF: $P_X(x) = P(X=x)$ | PDF: $f_X(x)$ |
| Non-Negativity | $P_X(x) \geq 0$ | $f_X(x) \geq 0$ |
| Normalization | $\sum_x P_X(x) = 1$ | $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_X(x)\,dx = 1$ |
| Event Probability | $P(X \in A) = \sum_{x \in A} P_X(x)$ | $P(a \leq X \leq b) = \int_a^b f_X(x)\,dx$ |
| CDF Definition | $F_X(x) = \sum_{x_i \leq x} P_X(x_i)$ | $F_X(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f_X(u)\,du$ |
| Point Probability | $P(X=x) = P_X(x) > 0$ possible | $P(X=x) = 0$ always |
| Recovery of Function | $P_X(x) = F_X(x) - F_X(x^-)$ | $f_X(x) = \frac{d}{dx}F_X(x)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility in Biostatistics:** PMF/PDF/CDF form the foundation for hypothesis testing, confidence interval estimation, survival analysis, and the construction of statistical models for clinical trials. KTU 2024 paper frequently tests these definitions directly.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Properties of CDF from First Principles

**Statement to Prove:** $P(a < X \leq b) = F_X(b) - F_X(a)$ for any real numbers $a < b$.

**Step 1:** Start with the definition of CDF.

$$F_X(b) = P(X \leq b)$$

**Step 2:** Decompose the event $\{X \leq b\}$ into two mutually exclusive parts: those outcomes where $X \leq a$ and those where $a < X \leq b$.

$$P(X \leq b) = P(X \leq a) + P(a < X \leq b)$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $P(X \leq a) = F_X(a)$ and $P(X \leq b) = F_X(b)$.

$$F_X(b) = F_X(a) + P(a < X \leq b)$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange to isolate the desired probability.

$$P(a < X \leq b) = F_X(b) - F_X(a)$$

**Conclusion:** This is one of the most frequently used identities in KTU problems. The logic is purely axiomatic, based on the partition of the sample space.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Discrete RV

**Problem:** Let $X$ denote the number of patients cured in a clinical trial of 3 patients. The PMF is given by:

$$P_X(x) = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{8} & x = 0 \\ \frac{3}{8} & x = 1 \\ \frac{3}{8} & x = 2 \\ \frac{1}{8} & x = 3 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 1: Verify the PMF property** (sum equals 1):

$$\sum_{x=0}^{3} P_X(x) = \frac{1}{8} + \frac{3}{8} + \frac{3}{8} + \frac{1}{8} = \frac{8}{8} = 1 \checkmark$$

**Step 2: Compute $P(X \leq 2)$:**

$$P(X \leq 2) = P_X(0) + P_X(1) + P_X(2) = \frac{1}{8} + \frac{3}{8} + \frac{3}{8} = \frac{7}{8}$$

**Step 3: Compute $P(1 < X \leq 3)$ using CDF identity:**

$$P(1 < X \leq 3) = F_X(3) - F_X(1) = 1 - \frac{4}{8} = \frac{4}{8} = \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 4: Construct the CDF:**

$$F_X(x) = \begin{cases} 0 & x < 0 \\ \frac{1}{8} & 0 \leq x < 1 \\ \frac{4}{8} & 1 \leq x < 2 \\ \frac{7}{8} & 2 \leq x < 3 \\ 1 & x \geq 3 \end{cases}$$

## 3.3 Worked Example — Continuous RV

**Problem:** The PDF of $X$ is given by $f_X(x) = kx^2$ for $0 \leq x \leq 2$, and $0$ elsewhere. Find $k$, the CDF, and $P(1 < X \leq 1.5)$.

**Step 1: Find $k$ using normalization.**

$$\int_{0}^{2} kx^2 \, dx = 1$$

**Step 2: Evaluate the integral.**

$$k \left[ \frac{x^3}{3} \right]_{0}^{2} = 1$$

$$k \cdot \frac{8}{3} = 1 \implies k = \frac{3}{8}$$

**Step 3: Construct the CDF $F_X(x)$ for $0 \leq x \leq 2$:**

$$F_X(x) = \int_{0}^{x} \frac{3}{8}u^2 \, du = \frac{3}{8} \cdot \frac{x^3}{3} = \frac{x^3}{8}$$

**Step 4: Complete piecewise CDF.**

$$F_X(x) = \begin{cases} 0 & x < 0 \\ \frac{x^3}{8} & 0 \leq x \leq 2 \\ 1 & x > 2 \end{cases}$$

**Step 5: Compute $P(1 < X \leq 1.5)$:**

$$P(1 < X \leq 1.5) = F_X(1.5) - F_X(1) = \frac{(1.5)^3}{8} - \frac{1^3}{8}$$

$$= \frac{3.375 - 1}{8} = \frac{2.375}{8} = 0.296875$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Always write the piecewise CDF explicitly, including the boundary cases ($x < 0$ and $x > 2$). Examiners allocate marks specifically for completeness of these regions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification of Random Variables

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the hierarchical classification of random variables covered in KTU Module 2:

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Random Variable X] --> B[Discrete RV]
    A --> C[Continuous RV]
    A --> D[Mixed RV]
    B --> B1[Described by PMF]
    B --> B2[Countable Range]
    B --> B3[Sum of PMF equals 1]
    C --> C1[Described by PDF]
    C --> C2[Uncountable Range]
    C --> C3[Integral of PDF equals 1]
    D --> D1[Has Both Discrete and Continuous Parts]
    B1 --> E1[Example: Number of Cured Patients]
    C1 --> E2[Example: Blood Pressure mmHg]
    D1 --> E3[Example: Insurance Claims with Zero Mass]
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture: PMF, PDF, and CDF Relationships

This block diagram maps the operational flow of how PMF and PDF are interconnected with the CDF, which is a **frequently tested KTU concept**:

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Sample Space Omega] --> B[Random Variable Mapping X]
    B --> C{Type of RV}
    C -->|Discrete| D[PMF Px of x]
    C -->|Continuous| E[PDF fx of x]
    D --> F[CDF Fx of x]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Probabilities of Intervals]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    G --> H[Biostatistical Inference]
    H --> H1[Hypothesis Testing]
    H --> H2[Confidence Intervals]
    H --> H3[Survival Analysis]
```

## 4.3 Properties Verification Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    P[PMF or PDF Given] --> Q{Non Negativity Check}
    Q -->|Satisfied| R{Normalization Check]
    Q -->|Failed| X1[Reject the Function]
    R -->|Sum equals 1 Discrete| Y[Valid PMF]
    R -->|Integral equals 1 Continuous| Z[Valid PDF]
    R -->|Failed| X2[Adjust Constant or Reject]
    Y --> W[Construct CDF via Summation]
    Z --> W2[Construct CDF via Integration]
    W --> OUT[Ready for Probability Calculations]
    W2 --> OUT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The block diagrams above are functional representations mapping the logical flow of the concepts. They are designed to be compatible with Mermaid syntax and should render correctly in any Markdown viewer that supports Mermaid v8+.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Conceptual Definition `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define a random variable. Distinguish between discrete and continuous random variables with biostatistical examples.**

**Model Answer:**

A **random variable** $X$ is a real-valued function defined on the sample space $\Omega$ of a random experiment. It assigns a numerical value to every outcome: $X : \Omega \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$.

| Feature | Discrete RV | Continuous RV |
|---|---|---|
| Range | Finite or countably infinite | Uncountable (interval) |
| Described by | Probability Mass Function (PMF) | Probability Density Function (PDF) |
| Probability of point | $P(X=x) = P_X(x) > 0$ | $P(X=x) = 0$ |
| Biostat Example | Number of patients recovered | Blood pressure in mmHg |

**Marks Distribution:** Definition (1) + Distinction table (1) + Examples (1).

---

### Question 2: CDF Properties `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define the Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) of a random variable. State any four properties of the CDF.**

**Model Answer:**

The CDF of a random variable $X$ is defined as:
$$F_X(x) = P(X \leq x), \quad x \in \mathbb{R}$$

**Four Properties:**

1. $0 \leq F_X(x) \leq 1$ for all $x$
2. $F_X(x)$ is monotonically non-decreasing (right-continuous)
3. $F_X(-\infty) = 0$ and $F_X(+\infty) = 1$
4. $P(a < X \leq b) = F_X(b) - F_X(a)$ for $a < b$

**Marks Distribution:** Definition with equation (1) + Four properties (0.5 each × 4 = 2).

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Module Pattern:** Attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.

---

### Question A: 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**The PDF of a continuous random variable $X$ is given by:**

$$f_X(x) = \begin{cases} kx & 0 \leq x \leq 2 \\ 2k & 2 < x \leq 3 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**(a)** Find the value of $k$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Find the CDF $F_X(x)$ and compute $P(1 \leq X \leq 2.5)$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Step 1:** Apply the normalization condition $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_X(x) \, dx = 1$.

$$\int_{0}^{2} kx \, dx + \int_{2}^{3} 2k \, dx = 1$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate the first integral.

$$\int_{0}^{2} kx \, dx = k \left[ \frac{x^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{2} = k \cdot \frac{4}{2} = 2k$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the second integral.

$$\int_{2}^{3} 2k \, dx = 2k[x]_{2}^{3} = 2k(3 - 2) = 2k$$

**Step 4:** Combine and solve for $k$.

$$2k + 2k = 1 \implies 4k = 1 \implies k = \frac{1}{4}$$

**Answer:** $k = 0.25$ **(7 marks — full credit for correct setup, evaluation, and final value)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Step 1:** Construct the CDF piecewise.

For $x < 0$: $F_X(x) = 0$

For $0 \leq x \leq 2$:

$$F_X(x) = \int_{0}^{x} \frac{1}{4}u \, du = \frac{1}{4} \cdot \frac{x^2}{2} = \frac{x^2}{8}$$

For $2 < x \leq 3$:

$$F_X(x) = F_X(2) + \int_{2}^{x} \frac{1}{2} \, du = \frac{4}{8} + \frac{1}{2}(x - 2) = \frac{1}{2} + \frac{x-2}{2}$$

For $x > 3$: $F_X(x) = 1$

**Step 2:** Compute $P(1 \leq X \leq 2.5)$:

$$P(1 \leq X \leq 2.5) = F_X(2.5) - F_X(1) = \left[\frac{1}{2} + \frac{0.5}{2}\right] - \frac{1}{8}$$

$$= [0.5 + 0.25] - 0.125 = 0.75 - 0.125 = 0.625$$

**Answer:** $P(1 \leq X \leq 2.5) = 0.625$ **(7 marks — 4 for CDF construction, 3 for probability calculation)**

---

### Question B: 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**A discrete random variable $X$ has the following probability distribution:**

| $x$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $P(X=x)$ | 0.1 | $a$ | $b$ | 0.3 | 0.2 |

**(a)** If $E[X] = 2.3$, find $a$ and $b$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Find the CDF $F_X(x)$ and compute $P(0.5 < X \leq 3.5)$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Step 1:** Apply the normalization condition.

$$0.1 + a + b + 0.3 + 0.2 = 1 \implies a + b = 0.4$$

**Step 2:** Apply the expectation condition $E[X] = \sum x \cdot P(X=x)$.

$$E[X] = 0(0.1) + 1(a) + 2(b) + 3(0.3) + 4(0.2) = 2.3$$

$$a + 2b + 0.9 + 0.8 = 2.3 \implies a + 2b = 0.6$$

**Step 3:** Solve the simultaneous equations.

From Step 1: $a = 0.4 - b$. Substituting:

$$(0.4 - b) + 2b = 0.6 \implies 0.4 + b = 0.6 \implies b = 0.2$$

Then $a = 0.4 - 0.2 = 0.2$

**Answer:** $a = 0.2$, $b = 0.2$ **(7 marks — 3 for equations, 2 for solving, 2 for verification)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Step 1:** Construct the discrete CDF.

| $x$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $F_X(x) = P(X \leq x)$ | 0.1 | 0.3 | 0.5 | 0.8 | 1.0 |

**Step 2:** Piecewise CDF.

$$F_X(x) = \begin{cases} 0 & x < 0 \\ 0.1 & 0 \leq x < 1 \\ 0.3 & 1 \leq x < 2 \\ 0.5 & 2 \leq x < 3 \\ 0.8 & 3 \leq x < 4 \\ 1.0 & x \geq 4 \end{cases}$$

**Step 3:** Compute $P(0.5 < X \leq 3.5)$:

$$P(0.5 < X \leq 3.5) = F_X(3.5) - F_X(0.5) = 0.8 - 0.1 = 0.7$$

**Answer:** $P(0.5 < X \leq 3.5) = 0.7$ **(7 marks — 4 for CDF, 3 for probability)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing strict and non-strict inequalities in CDF:** Remember that $F_X(x) = P(X \leq x)$ uses $\leq$, not $<$. This affects the inclusion of boundary values in piecewise CDFs.
> 2. **Forgetting the "otherwise" region in PDF/PMF:** Always state $f_X(x) = 0$ or $P_X(x) = 0$ for values outside the defined range. Examiners deduct 1 mark if missing.
> 3. **Incorrect normalization:** For a PDF with multiple pieces, the integral over **all** pieces must equal 1, not just the principal piece. Students commonly integrate only one region and forget the rest.
> 4. **Misinterpreting $P(X=x) = 0$ for continuous RV:** Many students incorrectly conclude that such events are "impossible". It means the probability is *measure zero*, not logically impossible.
> 5. **Units of Random Variables:** In biostatistics problems, always retain units (mmHg, mg/dL, etc.) in the final probability statements where applicable.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Random Variable:** A *function* $X : \Omega \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$, not an algebraic variable. Its value depends on the random outcome of an experiment.
- **Discrete RV:** Takes countable values; described by **PMF** $P_X(x)$ with $\sum P_X(x) = 1$.
- **Continuous RV:** Takes values in intervals; described by **PDF** $f_X(x)$ with $\int f_X(x) \, dx = 1$.
- **CDF:** Universal definition $F_X(x) = P(X \leq x)$ works for **both** discrete and continuous cases.
- **Key Identity:** $P(a < X \leq b) = F_X(b) - F_X(a)$ is the most frequently used result in KTU problems.
- **Point Probability:** $P(X = x) > 0$ is possible for discrete RV; $P(X = x) = 0$ always for continuous RV.
- **Recovery Formulas:** $P_X(x) = F_X(x) - F_X(x^-)$ for discrete; $f_X(x) = \frac{d}{dx} F_X(x)$ for continuous.
- **Biostatistical Context:** Always frame answers with biomedical examples (blood pressure, hemoglobin, patient counts, survival time) to demonstrate applied understanding — this aligns with the PEBMT414 course outcome CO2.
- **Piecewise Construction:** Always write the full piecewise definition of $f_X(x)$ and $F_X(x)$ including the "otherwise" / boundary regions.
- **Verification Step:** After finding a constant $k$, always verify that $f_X(x) \geq 0$ across the entire range and that the total area (or sum) equals 1.
- **Mark-Winning Strategy:** Show the *setup*, the *integration/summation*, the *evaluation at limits*, and the *final answer with units* in long-answer questions. This matches KTU's step-marking scheme.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Discrete Probability Distributions: Probability Mass Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT414 - BIOSTATISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Discrete Probability Distributions: Probability Mass Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Probability Mass Function (PMF)** is a function that gives the probability that a **discrete random variable $X$** is exactly equal to some specific value $x$. Mathematically, for a discrete random variable $X$ taking values in a countable set $\mathcal{S}$, the PMF is defined as:

$$p(x) = P(X = x)$$

The PMF satisfies two fundamental axioms:
