The Cumulative Distribution Function $F_X(x)$ satisfies **four** fundamental properties. These are board-favorite 2-mark and 3-mark questions:

1. **Non-negativity and boundedness:** $0 \le F_X(x) \le 1$ for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$.
2. **Monotonically non-decreasing:** If $x_1 < x_2$, then $F_X(x_1) \le F_X(x_2)$. (As $x$ increases, you can only accumulate more probability, never less.)
3. **Right-continuity:** $\lim_{x \to a^+} F_X(x) = F_X(a)$ (a technical property from measure theory, but it ensures the CDF is "well-behaved" from the right).
4. **Limiting behavior:** $F_X(-\infty) = 0$ and $F_X(+\infty) = 1$.

---

### 2.2 Why These Properties Hold — The Logic

- **Non-decreasing:** Because $\{X \le x_1\} \subseteq \{X \le x_2\}$ whenever $x_1 \le x_2$, monotonicity follows directly from the subset property of probability.
- **Right-continuity:** Guarantees no "jumps" occur from the right; jumps (in discrete cases) are absorbed **at** the point $x$ itself.
- **Limits at infinity:** Encodes the fact that $X$ must take *some* finite real value with certainty.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Applies To | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|------------|-------|
| 1 | Definition | $F_X(x) = P(X \le x)$ | All RVs | Universal |
| 2 | Interval Probability | $P(a < X \le b) = F_X(b) - F_X(a)$ | All RVs | Core identity |
| 3 | PMF to CDF (discrete) | $F_X(x) = \sum_{x_i \le x} P(X = x_i)$ | Discrete RV | Step function |
| 4 | PDF to CDF (continuous) | $F_X(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f(t)\, dt$ | Continuous RV | By FTC |
| 5 | CDF to PDF (continuous) | $f(x) = \dfrac{d}{dx} F_X(x)$ | Continuous RV | Differentiation |
| 6 | Survival Function | $S(x) = 1 - F_X(x) = P(X > x)$ | All RVs | Clinical trials |
| 7 | Lower Tail | $P(X < a) = \lim_{x \to a^-} F_X(x)$ | All RVs | Open interval |
| 8 | Upper Tail | $P(X \ge a) = 1 - \lim_{x \to a^-} F_X(x)$ | All RVs | For survival |
| 9 | Quantile (Median) | $F_X(m) = 0.5$ | All RVs | $m$ is median |
| 10 | Expectation (continuous) | $E[X] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x\, f(x)\, dx$ | Continuous RV | From PDF |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** A student must never write $P(a < X < b) = F(b) - F(a)$ for **discrete** RVs without the appropriate corrections. The correct closed form is $F(b) - \lim_{x \to a^-} F(x)$, since discrete CDFs have jumps that must be excluded.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Biostatistics

- **Clinical trials:** The survival function $S(t) = 1 - F(t)$ models time-to-event data (e.g., time until a patient recovers or relapses).
- **Dose-response studies:** CDF describes the cumulative proportion of patients responding at or below a given drug dose.
- **Diagnostic thresholds:** $F(\text{threshold})$ gives the probability of a *false negative* under a continuous test outcome.
- **Growth charts (pediatrics):** Pediatricians use empirical CDFs to map a child's measurement to a *percentile*.
- **Epidemic modeling:** Time-to-infection distributions are expressed as CDFs.

---

### 2.5 Graphical Behavior — The "Shape" of a CDF

- **Discrete CDF:** A staircase function with jumps of size $P(X = x_i)$ at each outcome.
- **Continuous CDF:** A smooth, monotonically non-decreasing $S$-shaped curve.
- **Mixed CDF:** Has both smooth segments and discrete jumps.

> [!NOTE]
> A CDF is **never** a bell curve, never a straight diagonal line (except for the uniform distribution), and never decreasing. If you see a graph like that in a multiple-choice question, it is **not** a valid CDF.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — From PMF to CDF (Discrete Case)

**Statement:** Given a discrete random variable $X$ with PMF $P(X = x_i) = p_i$, prove that

$$F_X(x) = \sum_{x_i \le x} p_i$$

**Step 1.** Start with the definition.

$$F_X(x) = P(X \le x)$$

**Step 2.** For a discrete random variable, the event $\{X \le x\}$ is the union of all elementary outcomes that are less than or equal to $x$.

$$\{X \le x\} = \bigcup_{i \,:\, x_i \le x} \{X = x_i\}$$

**Step 3.** These elementary events are mutually exclusive, so we apply the **additivity axiom** of probability for countable disjoint unions.

$$P(X \le x) = \sum_{i \,:\, x_i \le x} P(X = x_i) = \sum_{x_i \le x} p_i$$

**Step 4.** Conclude with the result.

$$\boxed{F_X(x) = \sum_{x_i \le x} p_i}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — From PDF to CDF (Continuous Case)

**Statement:** Given a continuous random variable $X$ with PDF $f(x)$, derive

$$F_X(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f(t)\, dt$$

**Step 1.** Begin with the CDF definition.

$$F_X(x) = P(X \le x)$$

**Step 2.** The probability that $X$ lies in $(-\infty, x]$ is, by the fundamental theorem relating PDFs to probabilities, the integral of the density over that interval.

$$P(X \le x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f(t)\, dt$$

**Step 3.** Since $f(t) \ge 0$ everywhere and $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(t)\, dt = 1$, this integral yields a value in $[0, 1]$, consistent with the CDF range.

$$\boxed{F_X(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f(t)\, dt}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — From CDF to PDF (Continuous Case)

**Statement:** Show that $f(x) = \dfrac{d}{dx} F_X(x)$.

**Step 1.** Take the derivative of the CDF with respect to $x$.

$$\frac{d}{dx} F_X(x) = \frac{d}{dx} \int_{-\infty}^{x} f(t)\, dt$$

**Step 2.** Apply the **Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, Part 1** (Leibniz rule with constant lower limit).

$$= f(x)$$

**Step 3.** Hence the relationship is established.

$$\boxed{f(x) = F_X^{\,\prime}(x) = \frac{d}{dx} F_X(x)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> At points where $F_X(x)$ has a jump (discrete mass), the derivative does not exist in the classical sense, but the relationship still holds in the *distributional* sense via the Dirac delta.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Probability in an Interval

**Statement:** Show that $P(a < X \le b) = F_X(b) - F_X(a)$ for any real numbers $a < b$.

**Step 1.** Use the decomposition of the event $\{X \le b\}$ into two disjoint events.

$$\{X \le b\} = \{X \le a\} \cup \{a < X \le b\}$$

**Step 2.** The two events on the right are mutually exclusive, so probability is additive.

$$P(X \le b) = P(X \le a) + P(a < X \le b)$$

**Step 3.** Substitute the definition of the CDF on both sides.

$$F_X(b) = F_X(a) + P(a < X \le b)$$

**Step 4.** Rearrange to isolate the interval probability.

$$\boxed{P(a < X \le b) = F_X(b) - F_X(a)}$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Worked Example with Both Discrete and Continuous

**Example Problem (KTU-pattern):** Let $X$ denote the number of patients responding to a drug in a clinical trial. Suppose $X \sim \text{Binomial}(n = 3, p = 0.4)$. Construct the CDF $F_X(x)$ and compute $P(1 < X \le 3)$.

**Step 1.** List the PMF values.

$P(X=0) = (0.6)^3 = 0.216$

$P(X=1) = \binom{3}{1}(0.4)(0.6)^2 = 0.432$

$P(X=2) = \binom{3}{2}(0.4)^2(0.6) = 0.288$

$P(X=3) = (0.4)^3 = 0.064$

**Step 2.** Compute the cumulative sum at each point.

| $x$ | $F_X(x) = P(X \le x)$ |
|-----|------------------------|
| $x < 0$ | $0$ |
| $0 \le x < 1$ | $0.216$ |
| $1 \le x < 2$ | $0.216 + 0.432 = 0.648$ |
| $2 \le x < 3$ | $0.648 + 0.288 = 0.936$ |
| $x \ge 3$ | $0.936 + 0.064 = 1.000$ |

**Step 3.** Apply the interval formula.

$$P(1 < X \le 3) = F_X(3) - F_X(1) = 1.000 - 0.216 = 0.784$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note the strict inequality** on the left: $X = 1$ is excluded, so we use $F_X(1)$, not $\lim_{x \to 1^-} F_X(x)$. The value $F_X(1) = 0.216$ already *includes* $P(X=1)$, but since the upper bound is $\le$, we are fine. The exclusion of 1 from the interval does not affect the right side, only the interpretation.

---

### 3.6 Worked Example — Continuous CDF Recovery

**Problem:** Suppose the CDF of a continuous random variable $T$ (representing time to recovery in days) is

$$F_T(t) = \begin{cases} 0, & t < 0 \\ 1 - e^{-0.5t}, & t \ge 0 \end{cases}$$

Find the PDF and $P(2 < T \le 6)$.

**Step 1.** Differentiate to obtain the PDF.

$$f_T(t) = \frac{d}{dt} F_T(t) = \frac{d}{dt}\left(1 - e^{-0.5t}\right) = 0.5\, e^{-0.5t}, \quad t \ge 0$$

This is the **exponential distribution** with rate $\lambda = 0.5$.

**Step 2.** Use the interval formula.

$$P(2 < T \le 6) = F_T(6) - F_T(2)$$

$$= \left(1 - e^{-3}\right) - \left(1 - e^{-1}\right) = e^{-1} - e^{-3}$$

**Step 3.** Numerical value.

$$= 0.3679 - 0.0498 = 0.3181$$

$$\boxed{P(2 < T \le 6) \approx 0.3181}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the CDF Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Random Variable X] --> B{Type?}
    B -- Discrete --> C[PMF: P X equals xi equals pi]
    B -- Continuous --> D[PDF: f of x]
    C --> E[Sum all pi for xi less than or equal x]
    D --> F[Integrate f of t from neg infinity to x]
    E --> G[CDF F sub X of x]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Monotonic non decreasing]
    G --> I[Right continuous]
    G --> J[Limits zero at neg infinity and one at pos infinity]
    G --> K[Interval Probability: F of b minus F of a]
    G --> L[Survival Function: S of x equals 1 minus F of x]
    G --> M[Quantiles: solve F of x equals p]
    L --> N[Clinical Time to Event Analysis]
    K --> O[Diagnostic Threshold Studies]
    M --> P[Pediatric Growth Percentiles]
```

### 4.2 Concept Map: CDF vs PDF vs PMF

```mermaid
graph LR
    PMF[PMF P of X equals xi] -- sum over xi leq x --> CDF[CDF F of x]
    PDF[PDF f of x] -- integrate minus infinity to x --> CDF
    CDF -- differentiate --> PDF
    CDF -- difference of masses --> PMF
    CDF --> S[Survival S of x equals 1 minus F of x]
    CDF --> H[Hazard Function h of x]
    S --> H
    H --> N[Clinical Survival Modeling]
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree — Solving a CDF Problem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Problem Given] --> Q1{What is given?}
    Q1 -- PMF table --> S1[Sort values of X ascending]
    S1 --> S2[Cumulate probabilities]
    S2 --> S3[Construct step function F of x]
    Q1 -- PDF formula --> T1[Integrate from minus infinity to x]
    T1 --> T2[Apply limits of integration]
    T2 --> T3[Simplify to obtain F of x]
    Q1 -- CDF formula --> U1[Identify continuous or discrete]
    U1 -- Continuous --> U2[Differentiate to get PDF]
    U1 -- Discrete --> U3[Take differences to get PMF]
    S3 --> V[Apply interval formula for probabilities]
    T3 --> V
    U2 --> V
    U3 --> V
    V --> End[Final Answer]
```

### 4.4 Schematic: Shape of CDF for Common Distributions

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Distribution] --> B[Discrete: Binomial]
    A --> C[Continuous: Normal]
    A --> D[Continuous: Exponential]
    B --> B1[Staircase CDF with jumps at 0,1,...,n]
    C --> C1[Smooth S shaped CDF symmetric about mean]
    D --> D1[Concave CDF rising from zero to one]
    B1 --> E[KTU Exam Favorite]
    C1 --> E
    D1 --> E
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** Define the Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) of a random variable $X$. State any **three** of its properties.

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) of a random variable $X$ is defined as

$$F_X(x) = P(X \le x), \quad x \in \mathbb{R}$$

**Three Properties:**

1. **Boundedness:** $0 \le F_X(x) \le 1$ for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$.
2. **Monotonically non-decreasing:** If $x_1 \le x_2$, then $F_X(x_1) \le F_X(x_2)$.
3. **Limiting values:** $\lim_{x \to -\infty} F_X(x) = 0$ and $\lim_{x \to +\infty} F_X(x) = 1$.

---

**Q2.** The CDF of a random variable $X$ is given by

$$F_X(x) = \begin{cases} 0, & x < 0 \\ x^2, & 0 \le x \le 1 \\ 1, & x > 1 \end{cases}$$

Verify that $F_X(x)$ is a valid CDF and find $P(0.3 < X \le 0.8)$.

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Verification of CDF properties:**

1. $F_X(x) \ge 0$ for all $x$: True since $x^2 \ge 0$.
2. $F_X(x) \le 1$: For $0 \le x \le 1$, $x^2 \le 1$, and for $x > 1$, $F_X(x) = 1$.
3. Monotonically non-decreasing: $x^2$ is increasing on $[0, 1]$, and the constant value 1 beyond 1 does not decrease.
4. $\lim_{x \to -\infty} F_X(x) = 0$ and $\lim_{x \to +\infty} F_X(x) = 1$: Both satisfied.

**Hence $F_X(x)$ is a valid CDF.**

**Computing the probability:**

$$P(0.3 < X \le 0.8) = F_X(0.8) - F_X(0.3)$$

$$= (0.8)^2 - (0.3)^2 = 0.64 - 0.09 = 0.55$$

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Note:** In the KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2 questions in Biostatistics (PEBMT414) carry 14 marks and offer internal choice between two question sets. We provide both options below for full board-exam preparation.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the expression for the Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) of a **continuous** random variable from its Probability Density Function (PDF). State clearly all assumptions used. (7 marks)

**(b)** Let $X$ be a continuous random variable with CDF

$$F_X(x) = \begin{cases} 0, & x \le 0 \\ 1 - e^{-2x}, & x > 0 \end{cases}$$

Find (i) the PDF of $X$, (ii) $P(X > 1)$, and (iii) the median of $X$. (7 marks)

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Assumptions:**

- $X$ is a continuous random variable.
- $X$ has a well-defined PDF $f(x) \ge 0$ with $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(t)\, dt = 1$.

**Derivation:**

By definition,

$$F_X(x) = P(X \le x)$$

For a continuous random variable, the probability of $X$ lying in the interval $(-\infty, x]$ is the integral of the density over that interval:

$$F_X(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f(t)\, dt$$

The function $F_X(x)$ is non-decreasing (since $f(t) \ge 0$), continuous, and satisfies $F_X(-\infty) = 0$, $F_X(+\infty) = 1$.

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**(i) PDF of $X$:**

$$f_X(x) = \frac{d}{dx} F_X(x) = \frac{d}{dx}\left(1 - e^{-2x}\right) = 2e^{-2x}, \quad x > 0$$

This is the **exponential distribution** with rate $\lambda = 2$.

**(ii) $P(X > 1)$:**

$$P(X > 1) = 1 - F_X(1) = 1 - \left(1 - e^{-2}\right) = e^{-2} \approx 0.1353$$

**(iii) Median $m$:**

The median is the value $m$ such that $F_X(m) = 0.5$.

$$1 - e^{-2m} = 0.5 \implies e^{-2m} = 0.5 \implies -2m = \ln(0.5) = -\ln 2$$

$$m = \frac{\ln 2}{2} \approx 0.3466$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** Define the Cumulative Distribution Function of a random variable. Prove that it is a monotonically non-decreasing function and right-continuous. (7 marks)

**(b)** A discrete random variable $X$ has the following PMF:

| $x$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 |
|-----|---|---|---|---|
| $P(X = x)$ | 0.1 | 0.2 | 0.4 | 0.3 |

Construct the CDF $F_X(x)$ and compute $P(1 \le X \le 2)$ and $P(X > 1)$. (7 marks)

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Definition:** The CDF of a random variable $X$ is $F_X(x) = P(X \le x)$ for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$.

**Monotonicity proof:**

Let $x_1 < x_2$. Then $\{X \le x_1\} \subseteq \{X \le x_2\}$.

By the monotonicity property of probability,

$$P(\{X \le x_1\}) \le P(\{X \le x_2\})$$

$$\implies F_X(x_1) \le F_X(x_2)$$

Hence $F_X(x)$ is non-decreasing.

**Right-continuity proof:**

Consider the sequence $x_n \to a^+$ with $x_n \downarrow a$. Then

$$F_X(a) = P(X \le a) = P\left(X \le \lim_{n \to \infty} x_n\right) = P\left(\bigcap_{n=1}^{\infty} \{X \le x_n\}\right)$$

By the **continuity of probability measure** (countable intersection of decreasing events),

$$= \lim_{n \to \infty} P(X \le x_n) = \lim_{n \to \infty} F_X(x_n) = \lim_{x \to a^+} F_X(x)$$

Hence $F_X$ is right-continuous.

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Construction of CDF:**

| $x$ | $F_X(x) = P(X \le x)$ |
|-----|------------------------|
| $x < 0$ | $0$ |
| $0 \le x < 1$ | $0.1$ |
| $1 \le x < 2$ | $0.1 + 0.2 = 0.3$ |
| $2 \le x < 3$ | $0.3 + 0.4 = 0.7$ |
| $x \ge 3$ | $0.7 + 0.3 = 1.0$ |

**Computation of $P(1 \le X \le 2)$:**

$$P(1 \le X \le 2) = P(X = 1) + P(X = 2) = 0.2 + 0.4 = 0.6$$

Alternatively, using the CDF directly:

$$P(1 \le X \le 2) = F_X(2) - \lim_{x \to 1^-} F_X(x) = 0.7 - 0.1 = 0.6$$

**Computation of $P(X > 1)$:**

$$P(X > 1) = 1 - F_X(1) = 1 - 0.3 = 0.7$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the closed interval convention:** A student must remember that $P(a < X \le b) = F(b) - F(a)$ is *only* valid when the CDF is continuous at $a$. For discrete RVs, the correct formula is $F(b) - \lim_{x \to a^-} F(x)$. Losing this nuance costs **2 marks** consistently.
> 2. **Confusing $F_X(1)$ with $\lim_{x \to 1^-} F_X(x)$:** The value *at* a jump is the *upper* value (right-continuity), not the lower value. Always be careful when reading CDFs at jump points.
> 3. **Skipping the limits verification:** When asked to verify a CDF, students often forget to check $F_X(-\infty) = 0$ and $F_X(+\infty) = 1$. The KTU examiner deducts **1 mark** for this oversight.
> 4. **Mixing up PDF/CDF conversion direction:** Differentiating a CDF gives a PDF, not the other way around. State this clearly in derivations.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Definition:** $F_X(x) = P(X \le x)$ — works for **all** random variables, discrete or continuous.
- ✅ **Universal property set:** bounded, non-decreasing, right-continuous, limits 0 and 1 at $\pm\infty$.
- ✅ **For discrete RVs:** $F_X(x)$ is a **staircase (step) function** with jumps of size $P(X = x_i)$.
- ✅ **For continuous RVs:** $F_X(x)$ is a **smooth, differentiable** function and $f(x) = F_X^{\,\prime}(x)$.
- ✅ **Interval probability identity:** $P(a < X \le b) = F_X(b) - F_X(a)$ — the workhorse of KTU problems.
- ✅ **Survival function:** $S(x) = 1 - F_X(x)$ — directly used in clinical biostatistics and reliability theory.
- ✅ **Median:** The value $m$ such that $F_X(m) = 0.5$.
- ✅ **Quantiles:** The $p$-th quantile $q_p$ satisfies $F_X(q_p) = p$.
- ✅ **Graphical test:** If a proposed $F(x)$ ever decreases or goes below 0 or above 1, it is invalid.
- ✅ **Conversion facts to memorize:**
  - PMF $\xrightarrow{\text{cumulate}}$ CDF
  - PDF $\xrightarrow{\text{integrate}}$ CDF
  - CDF $\xrightarrow{\text{differentiate}}$ PDF
  - CDF $\xrightarrow{\text{difference of jumps}}$ PMF
- ✅ **Common exam trap:** Discrete CDFs require careful handling of strict vs non-strict inequality bounds.
- ✅ **Biostatistics application hot-spots:** survival analysis, dose-response curves, percentile growth charts, ROC curves.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Binomial Distribution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT414 - BIOSTATISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Binomial Distribution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Binomial Distribution** is a discrete probability distribution that models the number of successes in a fixed number of **n** independent Bernoulli trials, where each trial has exactly two mutually exclusive outcomes (success or failure) and the probability of success **p** remains constant from trial to trial.

If a random variable **X** represents the number of successes in **n** independent trials, with probability of success **p** and probability of failure **q = 1 − p**, then **X** is said to follow a Binomial Distribution, written as:

$$X \sim B(n, p)$$

The **Probability Mass Function (PMF)** is given by:

$$P(X = x) = \binom{n}{x} p^x q^{n-x}, \quad x = 0, 1, 2, \dots, n$$

where:
- **n** = number of independent trials (a positive integer)
- **p** = probability of success on a single trial
- **q = 1 − p** = probability of failure on a single trial
- **x** = number of successes observed

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Quality Control in a Pharmaceutical Plant:**
> Imagine a tablet-manufacturing unit where each tablet is independently inspected. A tablet is either *defective* (success in the context of finding defects) with probability **p = 0.02**, or *non-defective* with probability **q = 0.98**. If you randomly pick **n = 10** tablets from the conveyor belt, the number of defective tablets **X** follows a **Binomial Distribution B(10, 0.02)**. The PMF answers the question: *"What is the probability that exactly 2 out of these 10 tablets are defective?"*

The Binomial Distribution is essentially a **sum of independent Bernoulli trials**. Each individual trial is a "coin-flip-like" experiment, and the Binomial Distribution counts how many "heads" (successes) appear after **n** such flips.

### 1.3 Key Assumptions of a Binomial Experiment (Bertrand's Postulates)

For **X** to truly follow **B(n, p)**, the following four conditions **MUST** be satisfied:

1. **Fixed Number of Trials (n):** The experiment is repeated a definite, predetermined number of times.
2. **Two Mutually Exclusive Outcomes:** Each trial must result in either a *success* or a *failure* only — no other outcome is possible.
3. **Constant Probability (p):** The probability of success must remain the same for every trial.
4. **Independence of Trials:** The outcome of one trial must not influence the outcome of any other trial.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics Used in Binomial Problems:**
> - **Mean (Expected Value):** $\mu = np$
> - **Variance:** $\sigma^2 = npq$
> - **Standard Deviation:** $\sigma = \sqrt{npq}$
> - **Distribution Type:** Discrete, Unimodal (for moderate p), Skewed (when p is near 0 or 1)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of the Binomial PMF

The PMF $P(X = x) = \binom{n}{x} p^x q^{n-x}$ is built from three intuitive components:

1. **$\binom{n}{x}$ — The Combination Term:**
   Counts the *number of ways* to choose which **x** trials (out of **n**) result in success. Order does not matter — only the position of successes.

2. **$p^x$ — Success Probability Term:**
   The probability of getting **x** successes, assuming they occur in specific pre-chosen positions.

3. **$q^{n-x}$ — Failure Probability Term:**
   The probability of getting **(n − x)** failures in the remaining positions.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why":** The joint probability of any *one specific arrangement* (e.g., SSSFF…F) is $p^x \cdot q^{n-x}$. Since there are $\binom{n}{x}$ equally-likely arrangements of **x** successes, the total probability is obtained by multiplication.

### 2.2 Important Properties of the Binomial Distribution

- **Mean (Expected Value):** $\mu = E(X) = np$
- **Variance:** $\sigma^2 = V(X) = npq = np(1-p)$
- **Skewness:** $\gamma_1 = \dfrac{q-p}{\sqrt{npq}}$
- **Kurtosis:** $\gamma_2 = \dfrac{1 - 6pq}{npq}$
- **Moment Generating Function (MGF):** $M_X(t) = (q + pe^t)^n$
- **Probability Generating Function (PGF):** $G_X(z) = (q + pz)^n$
- **Recurrence Relation:** $P(X = x+1) = \left(\dfrac{n-x}{x+1}\right)\left(\dfrac{p}{q}\right) P(X = x)$
- **Additive Property:** If $X_1 \sim B(n_1, p)$ and $X_2 \sim B(n_2, p)$ are independent, then $X_1 + X_2 \sim B(n_1 + n_2, p)$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following table — these formulas appear in nearly every KTU Biostatistics problem.**

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|
| PMF | $P(X=x) = \binom{n}{x} p^x q^{n-x}$ | Probability of exactly **x** successes |
| CDF | $P(X \le x) = \sum_{k=0}^{x} \binom{n}{k} p^k q^{n-k}$ | Cumulative probability up to **x** |
| Mean | $\mu = np$ | Expected number of successes |
| Variance | $\sigma^2 = npq$ | Measure of dispersion |
| Std. Deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{npq}$ | Square root of variance |
| Mode | $\lfloor (n+1)p \rfloor$ | Most likely value of **X** |
| Skewness | $\gamma_1 = \dfrac{q-p}{\sqrt{npq}}$ | Asymmetry measure |
| MGF | $M_X(t) = (q+pe^t)^n$ | Moment generating function |
| PGF | $G_X(z) = (q+pz)^n$ | Probability generating function |
| Recurrence | $P(x+1) = \dfrac{n-x}{x+1} \cdot \dfrac{p}{q} \cdot P(x)$ | For computational ease |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility in Engineering:** The Binomial Distribution is heavily used in **reliability engineering** (probability a system with n components fails), **signal processing** (bit error rate analysis), **clinical trials** (number of patients responding to a drug), and **acceptance sampling** in quality control.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Mean $E(X) = np$

**Step 1:** Start with the definition of expected value for a discrete random variable.

$$E(X) = \sum_{x=0}^{n} x \cdot P(X = x)$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the binomial PMF $P(X = x) = \binom{n}{x} p^x q^{n-x}$.

$$E(X) = \sum_{x=0}^{n} x \cdot \binom{n}{x} p^x q^{n-x}$$

**Step 3:** The **x = 0** term is zero, so it can be dropped. Pull out the factor **x** and rewrite the binomial coefficient as $\binom{n}{x} = \dfrac{n!}{x! \cdot (n-x)!}$, so $x \cdot \binom{n}{x} = n \cdot \binom{n-1}{x-1}$.

$$E(X) = \sum_{x=1}^{n} n \cdot \binom{n-1}{x-1} p^x q^{n-x}$$

**Step 4:** Factor out **n** and **p**, and substitute $k = x - 1$ (so $x = k + 1$, and as **x** runs from 1 to n, **k** runs from 0 to n − 1).

$$E(X) = n \cdot p \cdot \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \binom{n-1}{k} p^{k} q^{(n-1)-k}$$

**Step 5:** Apply the **Binomial Theorem**: $\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \binom{n-1}{k} p^{k} q^{(n-1)-k} = (p + q)^{n-1} = 1^{n-1} = 1$.

$$\boxed{E(X) = np}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Variance $V(X) = npq$

**Step 1:** Use the identity $V(X) = E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2$. We need $E(X^2)$.

**Step 2:** Express $E(X^2) = E[X(X-1)] + E(X)$, because $X(X-1) = X^2 - X$.

**Step 3:** Compute $E[X(X-1)]$ by direct summation.

$$E[X(X-1)] = \sum_{x=0}^{n} x(x-1) \binom{n}{x} p^x q^{n-x}$$

**Step 4:** Again, the terms **x = 0** and **x = 1** vanish, so we begin from **x = 2**. Using $x(x-1)\binom{n}{x} = n(n-1)\binom{n-2}{x-2}$:

$$E[X(X-1)] = \sum_{x=2}^{n} n(n-1) \binom{n-2}{x-2} p^x q^{n-x}$$

**Step 5:** Factor out $n(n-1)p^2$ and substitute $k = x - 2$.

$$E[X(X-1)] = n(n-1)p^2 \cdot \sum_{k=0}^{n-2} \binom{n-2}{k} p^{k} q^{(n-2)-k}$$

**Step 6:** The sum equals $(p + q)^{n-2} = 1$.

$$E[X(X-1)] = n(n-1)p^2$$

**Step 7:** Therefore:

$$E(X^2) = n(n-1)p^2 + np$$

**Step 8:** Substituting into the variance identity:

$$V(X) = n(n-1)p^2 + np - (np)^2$$

$$V(X) = n^2 p^2 - np^2 + np - n^2 p^2$$

$$\boxed{V(X) = np - np^2 = np(1-p) = npq}$$

### 3.3 Derivation of the Moment Generating Function (MGF)

**Step 1:** MGF is defined as $M_X(t) = E(e^{tX}) = \sum_{x=0}^{n} e^{tx} \cdot P(X=x)$.

**Step 2:** Substitute the PMF.

$$M_X(t) = \sum_{x=0}^{n} e^{tx} \binom{n}{x} p^x q^{n-x}$$

**Step 3:** Rewrite as $M_X(t) = \sum_{x=0}^{n} \binom{n}{x} (pe^t)^x q^{n-x}$.

**Step 4:** Apply the Binomial Theorem $(A + B)^n = \sum_{x=0}^{n}\binom{n}{x} A^x B^{n-x}$ with $A = pe^t$ and $B = q$.

$$\boxed{M_X(t) = (q + pe^t)^n}$$

### 3.4 Worked Example — KTU Standard Problem

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** In a clinical trial, the probability that a patient responds to a new drug is **p = 0.6**. If **n = 5** patients are treated, find the probability that exactly **3** patients respond. Also compute the mean and variance.

**Solution:**

Given: $n = 5$, $p = 0.6$, $q = 0.4$, $x = 3$.

**Step 1:** Compute the binomial coefficient.

$$\binom{5}{3} = \frac{5!}{3! \cdot 2!} = \frac{5 \cdot 4}{2 \cdot 1} = 10$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the PMF.

$$P(X=3) = \binom{5}{3} (0.6)^3 (0.4)^2 = 10 \times 0.216 \times 0.16$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate.

$$P(X=3) = 10 \times 0.03456 = 0.3456$$

**Step 4:** Compute the mean.

$$\mu = np = 5 \times 0.6 = 3.0$$

**Step 5:** Compute the variance.

$$\sigma^2 = npq = 5 \times 0.6 \times 0.4 = 1.2$$

$$\boxed{P(X=3) = 0.3456, \quad \mu = 3.0, \quad \sigma^2 = 1.2}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Derivation Flow of Binomial Distribution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Bernoulli Trial Single Experiment] --> B[Two Outcomes Success with p, Failure with q = 1 - p]
    B --> C[Repeat n Times Independently]
    C --> D[Count Number of Successes X]
    D --> E[X follows Binomial Distribution B n comma p]
    E --> F1[PMF P of X equals x equals nCx px qn minus x]
    E --> F2[Mean equals np]
    E --> F3[Variance equals npq]
    E --> F4[Skewness equals q minus p over sqrt npq]
    F1 --> G[Engineering Applications]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> G1[Quality Control and Acceptance Sampling]
    G --> G2[Reliability Engineering]
    G --> G3[Clinical Trials and Biostatistics]
    G --> G4[Signal Processing and Bit Error Analysis]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Recurrence Relation Computational Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start: Compute P of X equals 0 equals q to the n] --> B[Use Recurrence to Find P of X equals 1]
    B --> C[Use Recurrence to Find P of X equals 2]
    C --> D[Continue Iteratively Up to P of X equals n]
    D --> E[Sum Probabilities to Verify Equals 1]
    E --> F[Final Probability Distribution Table]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Relationship with Normal & Poisson Distributions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Binomial Distribution B n comma p] --> B1[If n is large and p is moderate: Approximated by Normal]
    A --> B2[If n is large and p is small with np constant: Approximated by Poisson]
    A --> B3[Special Case n equals 1: Reduces to Bernoulli]
    B1 --> C[Engineering Approximations]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**State and explain the properties of a Binomial Distribution. A random variable X follows B(8, 0.25). Find the mean and variance of X.**

**Model Answer:**

The **four essential properties** (or assumptions) of a Binomial Distribution are:

1. The number of trials **n** is **finite and fixed**.
2. Each trial has only **two possible outcomes** — success or failure.
3. The probability of success **p** is **constant** for every trial.
4. All trials are **statistically independent**.

**Given:** $n = 8$, $p = 0.25$, $q = 0.75$.

**Mean:**
$$\mu = np = 8 \times 0.25 = 2$$

**Variance:**
$$\sigma^2 = npq = 8 \times 0.25 \times 0.75 = 1.5$$

$$\boxed{\mu = 2, \quad \sigma^2 = 1.5}$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define the Moment Generating Function of a Binomial Distribution and derive it.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Moment Generating Function (MGF)** of a random variable **X** is defined as:

$$M_X(t) = E(e^{tX})$$

For $X \sim B(n, p)$:

$$M_X(t) = \sum_{x=0}^{n} e^{tx} \binom{n}{x} p^x q^{n-x} = \sum_{x=0}^{n} \binom{n}{x} (pe^t)^x q^{n-x}$$

Applying the **Binomial Theorem**:

$$\boxed{M_X(t) = (q + pe^t)^n}$$

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - May 2024]*

**(a)** Derive the mean and variance of a Binomial Distribution $B(n, p)$. State the four assumptions of a Binomial experiment. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** In a batch of 10 cardiac patients undergoing bypass surgery, the probability of successful recovery is 0.7. Find:
- (i) The probability that exactly 6 patients recover successfully.
- (ii) The probability that at most 2 patients fail to recover.
- (iii) The probability that at least 8 patients recover successfully. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of Mean and Variance:**

Refer to Sections 3.1 and 3.2 of these notes for the complete derivation.

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{E(X) = np, \quad V(X) = npq}$$

**Four Assumptions of a Binomial Experiment:** *(As stated in Section 1.3)*

1. Fixed number of trials **n**.
2. Each trial has exactly two outcomes (success/failure).
3. Constant probability **p** across trials.
4. Independence of trials.

**(b) Solution:**

**Given:** $n = 10$, $p = 0.7$, $q = 0.3$.

**(i) P(X = 6):**

$$P(X=6) = \binom{10}{6}(0.7)^6(0.3)^4 = 210 \times 0.117649 \times 0.0081$$

$$P(X=6) = 210 \times 0.0009529 = 0.2001$$

**(ii) P(at most 2 fail) = P(X ≥ 8) (since success is recovery):**

$$P(X \ge 8) = P(X=8) + P(X=9) + P(X=10)$$

$$P(X=8) = \binom{10}{8}(0.7)^8(0.3)^2 = 45 \times 0.05765 \times 0.09 = 0.2335$$

$$P(X=9) = \binom{10}{9}(0.7)^9(0.3)^1 = 10 \times 0.04035 \times 0.3 = 0.1211$$

$$P(X=10) = \binom{10}{10}(0.7)^{10} = 0.0282$$

$$P(X \ge 8) = 0.2335 + 0.1211 + 0.0282 = 0.3828$$

**(iii) P(X ≥ 8) = 0.3828 (Same as part ii)**

$$\boxed{P(X=6) = 0.2001, \quad P(\text{at most 2 fail}) = P(X \ge 8) = 0.3828}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]*

**(a)** Define the Binomial Distribution. Derive its Moment Generating Function and use it to find the mean. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The probability that a certain electronic component survives an overload test is 0.8. If 6 such components are tested independently, find:
- (i) The probability that exactly 4 components survive.
- (ii) The probability that fewer than 2 components survive.
- (iii) The mean and standard deviation of the number of survivors. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Definition and MGF Derivation:**

A **Binomial Distribution** $B(n, p)$ gives the probability of obtaining **x** successes in **n** independent Bernoulli trials, each with constant success probability **p**.

**MGF Derivation** *(see Section 3.3 for full derivation)*:

$$M_X(t) = (q + pe^t)^n$$

**Finding Mean from MGF:**

The mean is the first derivative of MGF evaluated at $t = 0$:

$$E(X) = M_X'(0)$$

Differentiating $M_X(t) = (q + pe^t)^n$ with respect to **t**:

$$M_X'(t) = n(q + pe^t)^{n-1} \cdot pe^t$$

Evaluating at $t = 0$:

$$E(X) = M_X'(0) = n(q + p)^{n-1} \cdot p = n(1)^{n-1} \cdot p = np$$

**(b) Solution:**

**Given:** $n = 6$, $p = 0.8$, $q = 0.2$.

**(i) P(X = 4):**

$$P(X=4) = \binom{6}{4}(0.8)^4(0.2)^2 = 15 \times 0.4096 \times 0.04 = 0.2458$$

**(ii) P(X < 2) = P(X = 0) + P(X = 1):**

$$P(X=0) = \binom{6}{0}(0.8)^0(0.2)^6 = 1 \times 1 \times 0.000064 = 0.000064$$

$$P(X=1) = \binom{6}{1}(0.8)^1(0.2)^5 = 6 \times 0.8 \times 0.00032 = 0.001536$$

$$P(X < 2) = 0.000064 + 0.001536 = 0.0016$$

**(iii) Mean and Standard Deviation:**

$$\mu = np = 6 \times 0.8 = 4.8$$

$$\sigma = \sqrt{npq} = \sqrt{6 \times 0.8 \times 0.2} = \sqrt{0.96} = 0.9798$$

$$\boxed{P(X=4) = 0.2458, \quad P(X<2) = 0.0016, \quad \mu = 4.8, \quad \sigma = 0.9798}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to AVOID:**
> 1. **Forgetting to compute $q$:** Many students use $p$ twice or forget that $q = 1 - p$. Always compute **q explicitly** in the first step.
> 2. **Confusing "at least" with "at most":** Read the question carefully. "At least **k**" means $X \ge k$; "at most **k**" means $X \le k$.
> 3. **Miscalculating $\binom{n}{x}$:** A frequent mistake is using $\dfrac{n!}{x!(n-x)!}$ incorrectly. Verify with a known identity like $\binom{n}{0} = \binom{n}{n} = 1$.
> 4. **Skipping intermediate steps in MGF/Mean derivation:** KTU examiners award **partial marks** for each correct intermediate step. Always show the differentiation and substitution explicitly.
> 5. **Mixing up p and q in skewness formula:** Skewness is $\frac{q-p}{\sqrt{npq}}$, NOT $\frac{p-q}{\sqrt{npq}}$.

---

### 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick Revision Checklist — Binomial Distribution**

- **Definition:** $X \sim B(n, p)$ — number of successes in **n** independent trials with constant success probability **p**.
- **PMF:** $P(X = x) = \binom{n}{x} p^x q^{n-x}$, for $x = 0, 1, 2, \dots, n$.
- **Mean:** $E(X) = np$
- **Variance:** $V(X) = npq = np(1-p)$
- **Standard Deviation:** $\sigma = \sqrt{npq}$
- **Skewness:** $\gamma_1 = \dfrac{q - p}{\sqrt{npq}}$ (right-skewed if $p < 0.5$, left-skewed if $p > 0.5$)
- **MGF:** $M_X(t) = (q + pe^t)^n$
- **PGF:** $G_X(z) = (q + pz)^n$
- **Mode:** $\lfloor (n+1)p \rfloor$
- **Recurrence:** $P(x+1) = \dfrac{n-x}{x+1} \cdot \dfrac{p}{q} \cdot P(x)$ — **use this for efficient computation**.
- **Additive Property:** $B(n_1, p) + B(n_2, p) \sim B(n_1 + n_2, p)$ for independent variables with the same **p**.
- **Special Cases:** $B(1, p)$ is **Bernoulli**; when **n** is large and **p** is small with **np** constant, approximate by **Poisson** ($\lambda = np$); when **n** is large, approximate by **Normal** ($\mu = np$, $\sigma^2 = npq$).
- **Validity Check:** $\sum_{x=0}^{n} P(X = x) = (p + q)^n = 1$ — always verify in problems.
- **Engineering Use:** Quality control, reliability testing, clinical trials, signal processing, bit error analysis.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Poisson Distribution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT414 - BIOSTATISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Poisson Distribution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A random variable $X$ is said to follow a **Poisson Distribution** with parameter $\lambda > 0$ if it assumes only non-negative integer values $x = 0, 1, 2, 3, \ldots, \infty$ with the probability mass function (PMF) given by:

$$
P(X = x) = \frac{e^{-\lambda} \cdot \lambda^{x}}{x!}, \quad x = 0, 1, 2, \ldots
$$

where:
- $\lambda$ is the **rate parameter** (also called the intensity or mean parameter), strictly positive: $\lambda > 0$.
- $e$ is Euler's number, $e \approx 2.71828$.
- The constant $e^{-\lambda}$ is the **normalizing constant** ensuring the total probability sums to $1$.

We write this compactly as $X \sim P(\lambda)$ or $X \sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Poisson Distribution is a discrete probability distribution used to model the **number of independent events occurring in a fixed interval of time or space**, when these events occur with a known constant mean rate $\lambda$ and independently of the time since the last event.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Call Center" Story

Imagine you are a quality-control inspector watching a conveyor belt for **defects in a fabric roll**. On average, you see $\lambda = 3$ defects per 10 square meters. The Poisson Distribution answers the question: *"What is the probability that I will see exactly $x$ defects in the next 10 m²?"*

Three key real-world scenarios where Poisson applies:

| Domain | Event Counted | Time/Space Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Telecommunications | Phone calls received at a switchboard | per minute |
| Biology / Biostatistics | Bacteria colonies in a petri dish | per cm² |
| Road Safety | Accidents at a traffic junction | per day |
| Manufacturing | Defects on a printed circuit board | per square meter |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Poisson"?** Named after the French mathematician **Siméon Denis Poisson** (1781–1840), who introduced it in 1837 in his work on the probability of judicial verdicts. It is sometimes called the **"law of rare events"** because it models situations where $p$ is very small and $n$ is very large.

### 1.3 Conditions for a Poisson Process (KTU Board-Favorite Question)

For a random variable to be genuinely Poisson-distributed, the underlying process must satisfy:

1. **Independence:** The number of events in non-overlapping intervals are independent.
2. **Stationarity (Homogeneity):** The probability of an event occurring is the same for all intervals of equal length.
3. **Orderliness:** Events cannot occur simultaneously (no two events at the exact same instant).
4. **Non-negativity & Integrality:** Outcomes must be discrete counts ($0, 1, 2, \ldots$).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Poisson? The Conceptual Foundation

The Poisson distribution arises naturally in three mathematically equivalent ways, each of which the KTU board loves to test:

- **As a limit of the Binomial:** When $n \to \infty$ and $p \to 0$ such that $np = \lambda$ remains constant. Useful for "rare events in large trials."
- **As a pure counting process:** Models count data over continuous time/space.
- **As exponential inter-arrival times:** If the time between successive events follows an Exponential distribution with rate $\lambda$, the count in a fixed interval is Poisson with the same $\lambda$.

### 2.2 Properties of the Poisson Distribution

1. **Mean = Variance = $\lambda$** — this is the single most important property. Poisson is the canonical example of a *mean-equals-variance* distribution.
2. **Skewness** = $\frac{1}{\sqrt{\lambda}}$ — distribution is right-skewed for small $\lambda$, becomes symmetric as $\lambda \to \infty$.
3. **Excess Kurtosis** = $\frac{1}{\lambda}$ — heavier tails than the Normal distribution for small $\lambda$.
4. **Additivity (Reproductive Property):** If $X_1 \sim P(\lambda_1)$ and $X_2 \sim P(\lambda_2)$ are independent, then $X_1 + X_2 \sim P(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2)$. This extends to any finite sum of independent Poissons.
5. **MGF Property:** $M_X(t) = e^{\lambda(e^t - 1)}$
6. **Probability Generating Function (PGF):** $G_X(z) = E(z^X) = e^{\lambda(z-1)}$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **S.No.** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Remarks** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | PMF | $P(X=x) = \dfrac{e^{-\lambda}\lambda^x}{x!}$ | $x \in \{0,1,2,\ldots\}$ |
| 2 | CDF | $F(x) = \displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{\lfloor x \rfloor} \dfrac{e^{-\lambda}\lambda^k}{k!}$ | Step function |
| 3 | Mean | $E(X) = \lambda$ | First raw moment |
| 4 | Variance | $\mathrm{Var}(X) = \lambda$ | Second central moment |
| 5 | Std. Deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\lambda}$ | Square root of mean |
| 6 | $E(X^2)$ | $\lambda + \lambda^2$ | Used in variance derivation |
| 7 | Skewness | $\gamma_1 = \lambda^{-1/2}$ | Right-skewed for small $\lambda$ |
| 8 | Excess Kurtosis | $\gamma_2 = \lambda^{-1}$ | Heavier tails than Normal |
| 9 | MGF | $M_X(t) = e^{\lambda(e^t - 1)}$ | $t \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| 10 | CGF | $K_X(t) = \lambda(e^t - 1)$ | $\ln M_X(t)$ |
| 11 | Char. Function | $\phi_X(t) = e^{\lambda(e^{it} - 1)}$ | $t \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| 12 | PGF | $G_X(z) = e^{\lambda(z-1)}$ | $\vert z \vert \le 1$ |
| 13 | Mode | $\lfloor \lambda \rfloor$ | Largest integer $\le \lambda$ |
| 14 | Additivity | $X_1+X_2 \sim P(\lambda_1+\lambda_2)$ | For independent $X_1, X_2$ |
| 15 | Binomial Limit | $P(X=x) = \displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty} \binom{n}{x} p^x (1-p)^{n-x}$ | When $np = \lambda$ fixed |

### 2.4 Engineering / Biostatistics Utility

In **biostatistics and biomedical engineering**, the Poisson distribution is indispensable for:

- Modeling the **incidence of rare diseases** (e.g., leukemia cases per district per year).
- Counting **chromosome aberrations** in radiation biology.
- **Radioactive decay events** detected by a Geiger counter over a fixed interval.
- Modeling **hospital admissions** or **emergency room arrivals**.
- In **reliability engineering**, as the limiting distribution of rare equipment failures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world utility:** When your data is a **count of independent random events in a fixed time/space window**, and the events are rare relative to the opportunities, Poisson is the natural choice. The KTU board often poses word problems where the student must identify $\lambda$ from the given average.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Poisson as Limiting Case of Binomial (KTU Board Favorite — 7 marks)

We start with a Binomial random variable $Y \sim B(n, p)$:

$$
P(Y = x) = \binom{n}{x} p^{x} (1-p)^{n-x}
$$

We want to take the limit as $n \to \infty$ and $p \to 0$ such that the product $np = \lambda$ remains a finite positive constant. This is the "rare events in many trials" scenario.

**Step 1:** Express $p$ in terms of $n$ and $\lambda$: $p = \dfrac{\lambda}{n}$, so $1 - p = 1 - \dfrac{\lambda}{n}$.

**Step 2:** Substitute these into the Binomial PMF:

$$
P(Y = x) = \binom{n}{x} \left(\frac{\lambda}{n}\right)^{x} \left(1 - \frac{\lambda}{n}\right)^{n-x}
$$

**Step 3:** Expand the binomial coefficient: $\binom{n}{x} = \dfrac{n!}{x!\,(n-x)!} = \dfrac{n(n-1)(n-2)\cdots(n-x+1)}{x!}$.

**Step 4:** Substitute and group terms:

$$
P(Y = x) = \frac{n(n-1)(n-2)\cdots(n-x+1)}{x!} \cdot \frac{\lambda^{x}}{n^{x}} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{\lambda}{n}\right)^{n-x}
$$

**Step 5:** Split into three interpretable pieces:

$$
P(Y = x) = \underbrace{\left[\frac{n(n-1)(n-2)\cdots(n-x+1)}{n^{x}}\right]}_{\text{Term A}} \cdot \underbrace{\frac{\lambda^{x}}{x!}}_{\text{Term B}} \cdot \underbrace{\left(1 - \frac{\lambda}{n}\right)^{n-x}}_{\text{Term C}}
$$

**Step 6:** Take the limit of each term as $n \to \infty$ (with $x$ fixed).

**Term A (limit):**

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{n(n-1)(n-2)\cdots(n-x+1)}{n^{x}} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \prod_{k=0}^{x-1} \left(1 - \frac{k}{n}\right) = 1
$$

since each factor $\left(1 - \dfrac{k}{n}\right) \to 1$ as $n \to \infty$.

**Term B (limit):** Independent of $n$, so the limit is simply $\dfrac{\lambda^{x}}{x!}$.

**Term C (limit):** Split the exponent:

$$
\left(1 - \frac{\lambda}{n}\right)^{n-x} = \left(1 - \frac{\lambda}{n}\right)^{n} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{\lambda}{n}\right)^{-x}
$$

Taking the limit:

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} \left(1 - \frac{\lambda}{n}\right)^{n} = e^{-\lambda} \quad \text{(standard limit)}
$$

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} \left(1 - \frac{\lambda}{n}\right)^{-x} = 1
$$

**Step 7:** Multiply the three limits together:

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} P(Y = x) = 1 \cdot \frac{\lambda^{x}}{x!} \cdot e^{-\lambda} = \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^{x}}{x!}
$$

This is precisely the Poisson PMF. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Mean $E(X) = \lambda$

Starting from the definition of expected value:

$$
E(X) = \sum_{x=0}^{\infty} x \cdot P(X=x) = \sum_{x=0}^{\infty} x \cdot \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^{x}}{x!}
$$

The $x = 0$ term is zero, so we may start from $x = 1$:

$$
E(X) = \sum_{x=1}^{\infty} x \cdot \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^{x}}{x!} = \sum_{x=1}^{\infty} \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^{x}}{(x-1)!}
$$

Factor out one $\lambda$:

$$
E(X) = e^{-\lambda} \lambda \sum_{x=1}^{\infty} \frac{\lambda^{x-1}}{(x-1)!}
$$

Substitute $k = x - 1$ (so $k$ runs from $0$ to $\infty$):

$$
E(X) = e^{-\lambda} \lambda \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{\lambda^{k}}{k!}
$$

The summation is the Taylor expansion of $e^{\lambda}$:

$$
\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{\lambda^{k}}{k!} = e^{\lambda}
$$

Therefore:

$$
E(X) = e^{-\lambda} \lambda \cdot e^{\lambda} = \lambda \qquad \blacksquare
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Variance $\mathrm{Var}(X) = \lambda$

We use the identity $\mathrm{Var}(X) = E(X^{2}) - [E(X)]^{2}$ and first compute $E(X^{2})$.

**Step 1:** Compute $E[X(X-1)]$ (a standard trick to avoid the factorial mess):

$$
E[X(X-1)] = \sum_{x=0}^{\infty} x(x-1) \cdot \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^{x}}{x!}
$$

The $x = 0$ and $x = 1$ terms vanish, so we start at $x = 2$:

$$
E[X(X-1)] = \sum_{x=2}^{\infty} \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^{x}}{(x-2)!}
$$

Factor out $\lambda^{2}$ and substitute $k = x - 2$:

$$
E[X(X-1)] = e^{-\lambda} \lambda^{2} \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{\lambda^{k}}{k!} = e^{-\lambda} \lambda^{2} \cdot e^{\lambda} = \lambda^{2}
$$

**Step 2:** Now expand $X^{2} = X(X-1) + X$:

$$
E(X^{2}) = E[X(X-1)] + E(X) = \lambda^{2} + \lambda
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the variance:

$$
\mathrm{Var}(X) = E(X^{2}) - [E(X)]^{2} = (\lambda^{2} + \lambda) - \lambda^{2} = \lambda \qquad \blacksquare
$$

### 3.4 Worked Example 1 (KTU Pattern — Part B)

**Problem:** A call center receives on average 3 calls per minute. Find the probability that in a given minute: (i) exactly 5 calls are received, (ii) at most 2 calls are received, (iii) at least 4 calls are received. (Given $e^{-3} \approx 0.0498$.)

**Solution:** Here $X \sim P(\lambda = 3)$.

**(i) $P(X = 5)$:**

$$
P(X = 5) = \frac{e^{-3} \cdot 3^{5}}{5!} = \frac{0.0498 \times 243}{120} = \frac{12.1014}{120} \approx 0.1008
$$

**(ii) $P(X \le 2) = P(0) + P(1) + P(2)$:**

$$
P(0) = \frac{e^{-3} \cdot 3^{0}}{0!} = 0.0498
$$

$$
P(1) = \frac{e^{-3} \cdot 3^{1}}{1!} = 0.1494
$$

$$
P(2) = \frac{e^{-3} \cdot 3^{2}}{2!} = \frac{0.0498 \times 9}{2} = 0.2241
$$

$$
P(X \le 2) = 0.0498 + 0.1494 + 0.2241 = 0.4233
$$

**(iii) $P(X \ge 4) = 1 - P(X \le 3)$:**

$$
P(3) = \frac{e^{-3} \cdot 3^{3}}{3!} = \frac{0.0498 \times 27}{6} = 0.2241
$$

$$
P(X \le 3) = 0.4233 + 0.2241 = 0.6474
$$

$$
P(X \ge 4) = 1 - 0.6474 = 0.3526
$$

### 3.5 Worked Example 2 — Additivity Property

**Problem:** In a hospital ward, the number of patients arriving with Condition A per day follows $P(2)$ and Condition B follows $P(3)$, independently. Find the probability that the total arrivals in a day is exactly 4.

**Solution:** By the additive property, total $T = X + Y \sim P(2 + 3) = P(5)$.

$$
P(T = 4) = \frac{e^{-5} \cdot 5^{4}}{4!} = \frac{e^{-5} \cdot 625}{24} \approx \frac{0.00674 \times 625}{24} \approx 0.1755
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Map: The Poisson Distribution Ecosystem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Counting Random Events] --> B{Fixed interval of time or space?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Are events independent?}
    C -- Yes --> D{Is the average rate constant?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Apply Poisson Distribution]
    E --> F[PMF: P of X equal to x = e sup negative lambda times lambda sup x divided by x factorial]
    E --> G[Mean equal to lambda]
    E --> H[Variance equal to lambda]
    E --> I[Additivity: Sum of independent Poissons is Poisson]
    E --> J[Limiting case of Binomial when n large p small np equal lambda]
    J --> K[Validates the model for rare events]
    I --> L[Useful in biostatistics for combined counts]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: From Binomial to Poisson Limit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Binomial Distribution: Y tilde B of n and p] --> nodeB[Condition: n to infinity, p to zero]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Constraint: n times p equals lambda constant]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Step 1: Term A limit equals 1]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Step 2: Term B gives lambda sup x divided by x factorial]
    nodeC --> nodeF[Step 3: Term C gives e sup negative lambda]
    nodeD --> nodeG[Multiply all three limits]
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH[Result: Poisson PMF]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Parameter lambda equals n times p]
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture: Properties & Applications Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    propA[Property: Mean equal to Variance equal to lambda] --> appA[Application: Quality control and defect monitoring]
    propB[Property: Additivity for independent variables] --> appB[Application: Merging hospital admission data]
    propC[Property: Skewness equals one over square root lambda] --> appC[Application: Approximation of Normal for large lambda]
    propD[Property: Limiting case of Binomial] --> appD[Application: Modeling rare diseases and events]
    propE[Property: MGF equals e sup lambda times e sup t minus one] --> appE[Application: Distribution of sums via MGF]
    style propA fill:#e1f5ff
    style propB fill:#e1f5ff
    style propC fill:#e1f5ff
    style propD fill:#e1f5ff
    style propE fill:#e1f5ff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Define the Poisson distribution. State any two of its important properties.**

**Model Answer:**
A discrete random variable $X$ taking values $0, 1, 2, \ldots$ is said to follow a Poisson distribution with parameter $\lambda > 0$ if its PMF is:

$$
P(X = x) = \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^{x}}{x!}, \quad x = 0, 1, 2, \ldots
$$

**Two important properties:**
1. **Mean = Variance = $\lambda$**: Both the expected value and the variance of a Poisson random variable equal the parameter $\lambda$.
2. **Additivity (Reproductive Property):** If $X_1 \sim P(\lambda_1)$ and $X_2 \sim P(\lambda_2)$ are independent, then $X_1 + X_2 \sim P(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2)$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] The number of accidents in a city follows a Poisson distribution with mean 2 per day. Find the probability of (i) no accidents in a day, (ii) exactly 3 accidents in a day. (Given $e^{-2} = 0.1353$.)**

**Model Answer:**
Here $X \sim P(\lambda = 2)$.

**(i) Probability of no accidents:**

$$
P(X = 0) = \frac{e^{-2} \cdot 2^{0}}{0!} = e^{-2} \times 1 = 0.1353
$$

**(ii) Probability of exactly 3 accidents:**

$$
P(X = 3) = \frac{e^{-2} \cdot 2^{3}}{3!} = \frac{0.1353 \times 8}{6} = \frac{1.0824}{6} \approx 0.1804
$$

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) Derive the mean and variance of the Poisson distribution with parameter $\lambda$.** *(7 marks)*

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Derivation of Mean:**

$$
E(X) = \sum_{x=0}^{\infty} x \cdot P(X = x) = \sum_{x=0}^{\infty} x \cdot \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^{x}}{x!}
$$

The $x=0$ term is $0$, so we start at $x=1$. Also $x/x! = 1/(x-1)!$:

$$
E(X) = \sum_{x=1}^{\infty} \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^{x}}{(x-1)!} = e^{-\lambda} \lambda \sum_{x=1}^{\infty} \frac{\lambda^{x-1}}{(x-1)!}
$$

Let $k = x - 1$:

$$
E(X) = e^{-\lambda} \lambda \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{\lambda^{k}}{k!} = e^{-\lambda} \lambda \cdot e^{\lambda} = \lambda
$$

**Derivation of Variance:** First compute $E[X(X-1)]$:

$$
E[X(X-1)] = \sum_{x=0}^{\infty} x(x-1) \cdot \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^{x}}{x!} = \sum_{x=2}^{\infty} \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^{x}}{(x-2)!}
$$

Let $k = x - 2$:

$$
E[X(X-1)] = e^{-\lambda} \lambda^{2} \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{\lambda^{k}}{k!} = e^{-\lambda} \lambda^{2} \cdot e^{\lambda} = \lambda^{2}
$$

Now $X^{2} = X(X-1) + X$, so:

$$
E(X^{2}) = E[X(X-1)] + E(X) = \lambda^{2} + \lambda
$$

$$
\mathrm{Var}(X) = E(X^{2}) - [E(X)]^{2} = (\lambda^{2} + \lambda) - \lambda^{2} = \lambda
$$

Hence, $\boxed{E(X) = \mathrm{Var}(X) = \lambda}$. $\blacksquare$

---

**(b) A factory produces electronic chips. On average, 1.5 chips are defective per hour. Using the Poisson model, find:** *(7 marks)*
- **(i) The probability that no chip is defective in one hour.**
- **(ii) The probability that at most 2 chips are defective in 4 hours.**
- **(iii) The standard deviation of the number of defective chips in a 4-hour shift.**

*(Given $e^{-1.5} = 0.2231$, $e^{-6} = 0.00248$.)*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Probability of no defect in 1 hour:**

$\lambda = 1.5$ per hour.

$$
P(X = 0) = \frac{e^{-1.5} \cdot 1.5^{0}}{0!} = e^{-1.5} = 0.2231
$$

**(ii) Probability of at most 2 defects in 4 hours:**

For 4 hours, $\lambda = 1.5 \times 4 = 6$. So $Y \sim P(6)$.

$$
P(Y \le 2) = P(0) + P(1) + P(2)
$$

$$
P(0) = \frac{e^{-6} \cdot 6^{0}}{0!} = 0.00248
$$

$$
P(1) = \frac{e^{-6} \cdot 6^{1}}{1!} = 0.00248 \times 6 = 0.01488
$$

$$
P(2) = \frac{e^{-6} \cdot 6^{2}}{2!} = \frac{0.00248 \times 36}{2} = 0.04464
$$

$$
P(Y \le 2) = 0.00248 + 0.01488 + 0.04464 = 0.0620
$$

**(iii) Standard deviation in 4 hours:**

For $\lambda = 6$:

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{\mathrm{Var}(Y)} = \sqrt{\lambda} = \sqrt{6} \approx 2.449
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a) Show that the Poisson distribution is a limiting case of the Binomial distribution as $n \to \infty$, $p \to 0$ with $np = \lambda$ constant.** *(7 marks)*

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

Let $Y \sim B(n, p)$. The PMF is:

$$
P(Y = x) = \binom{n}{x} p^{x} (1-p)^{n-x}
$$

We let $n \to \infty$, $p \to 0$ with $np = \lambda$ (fixed), so $p = \lambda/n$.

Substituting $p = \lambda/n$:

$$
P(Y = x) = \binom{n}{x} \left(\frac{\lambda}{n}\right)^{x} \left(1 - \frac{\lambda}{n}\right)^{n-x}
$$

Expand the binomial coefficient:

$$
P(Y = x) = \frac{n!}{x!(n-x)!} \cdot \frac{\lambda^{x}}{n^{x}} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{\lambda}{n}\right)^{n-x}
$$

Regroup as:

$$
P(Y = x) = \underbrace{\frac{n(n-1)(n-2)\cdots(n-x+1)}{n^{x}}}_{\text{Term A}} \cdot \frac{\lambda^{x}}{x!} \cdot \underbrace{\left(1 - \frac{\lambda}{n}\right)^{n-x}}_{\text{Term C}}
$$

**Term A:** As $n \to \infty$ with $x$ fixed, each factor $(1 - k/n) \to 1$ for $k = 0, 1, \ldots, x-1$. So Term A $\to 1$.

**Term C:** Rewrite as $\left(1 - \frac{\lambda}{n}\right)^{n} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{\lambda}{n}\right)^{-x}$. As $n \to \infty$, the first factor $\to e^{-\lambda}$ (standard limit) and the second $\to 1$. So Term C $\to e^{-\lambda}$.

Multiplying:

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} P(Y = x) = 1 \cdot \frac{\lambda^{x}}{x!} \cdot e^{-\lambda} = \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^{x}}{x!}
$$

This is the Poisson PMF. Hence, Poisson is the limiting case of Binomial. $\blacksquare$

---

**(b) The number of typing errors on a page of a book follows a Poisson distribution with mean 0.5. Find the probability that:** *(7 marks)*
- **(i) A randomly selected page contains no errors.**
- **(ii) A randomly selected page contains at least one error.**
- **(iii) Out of 5 randomly selected pages, exactly 2 pages are error-free. Use the answer from part (i).**

**Model Solution:**

Here $X \sim P(\lambda = 0.5)$. Note $e^{-0.5} \approx 0.6065$.

**(i) Probability a page has no errors:**

$$
P(X = 0) = \frac{e^{-0.5} \cdot 0.5^{0}}{0!} = e^{-0.5} = 0.6065
$$

**(ii) Probability a page has at least one error:**

$$
P(X \ge 1) = 1 - P(X = 0) = 1 - 0.6065 = 0.3935
$$

**(iii) Probability exactly 2 out of 5 pages are error-free:**

Each page is error-free with probability $p = 0.6065$, independently. Let $W$ be the number of error-free pages out of 5. Then $W \sim B(5, 0.6065)$.

$$
P(W = 2) = \binom{5}{2} (0.6065)^{2} (1 - 0.6065)^{3} = 10 \times 0.3678 \times (0.3935)^{3}
$$

$$
(0.3935)^{3} = 0.0609
$$

$$
P(W = 2) = 10 \times 0.3678 \times 0.0609 \approx 0.2240
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the rate $\lambda$ scaling:** When asked about a different time window (e.g., 4 hours instead of 1), students often forget to multiply $\lambda$ by the new time. This loses **3 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Confusing "at most" vs "at least":** $P(X \le k) = \sum_{i=0}^{k} P(i)$, while $P(X \ge k) = 1 - P(X \le k-1)$. Mixing these up is a **2-mark deduction** typical error.
> 3. **Dropping the $e^{-\lambda}$ normalization:** Students sometimes write $P(X = x) = \lambda^{x}/x!$ without $e^{-\lambda}$, giving probabilities that don't sum to 1. The full KTU mark requires all three factors: $e^{-\lambda}$, $\lambda^{x}$, and $1/x!$.
> 4. **Sign error in variance derivation:** Writing $\mathrm{Var}(X) = E(X^{2}) + [E(X)]^{2}$ instead of $E(X^{2}) - [E(X)]^{2}$ — loses **1 mark** for the conceptual gap.
> 5. **In the Binomial limit derivation, skipping Term A:** Many students only show the $(1 - \lambda/n)^{n} \to e^{-\lambda}$ limit and forget the factorial cancellation in $\binom{n}{x}/n^{x}$. Full marks require **all three** limits.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **PMF Master Formula:** $P(X = x) = \dfrac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^{x}}{x!}$, for $x = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$
- **Mean = Variance = $\lambda$** — the signature property of Poisson.
- **Standard deviation = $\sqrt{\lambda}$** — use this for quick dispersion checks.
- **Three equivalent origins of Poisson:** (i) Limiting case of Binomial ($n \to \infty$, $p \to 0$, $np = \lambda$), (ii) Counting process in a Poisson process, (iii) Exponential inter-arrival times.
- **MGF:** $M_X(t) = e^{\lambda(e^{t} - 1)}$ — use it to prove the additivity property.
- **Additivity:** Sum of independent Poissons is Poisson with summed parameters.
- **Scaling rule:** If $X \sim P(\lambda)$ per unit, then in $k$ units, $X \sim P(k\lambda)$.
- **Skewness = $1/\sqrt{\lambda}$**: Right-skewed for small $\lambda$, near-symmetric for large $\lambda$ (Normal approximation works when $\lambda \ge 10$).
- **Mode** = $\lfloor \lambda \rfloor$ (largest integer not exceeding $\lambda$).
- **Trick for variance derivation:** Compute $E[X(X-1)]$ first, then use $X^{2} = X(X-1) + X$.
- **Complement rule:** $P(X \ge 1) = 1 - e^{-\lambda}$ is a very common KTU shortcut.
- **Practical identification:** If a problem gives an *average rate per unit time/space* and asks for a *count probability*, it is Poisson.
- **Watch out for the words:** "On average", "per minute", "per square meter", "independent events" — these are the trigger words for Poisson.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Continuous Probability Distributions: Normal Distribution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT414 - BIOSTATISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Continuous Probability Distributions: Normal Distribution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Normal Distribution** (also called the **Gaussian Distribution**) is the most important continuous probability distribution in statistics and biostatistics. It is a **bell-shaped, symmetric distribution** defined by two parameters: the **mean (μ)** and the **variance (σ²)**. A continuous random variable $X$ is said to follow a Normal distribution if its Probability Density Function (PDF) is given by the **de Moivre–Laplace equation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> A continuous random variable $X$ is said to follow a Normal Distribution with parameters $\mu$ (mean) and $\sigma^2$ (variance) if its probability density function is:
> $$f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{x-\mu}{\sigma}\right)^{2}}$$
> where $\pi \approx 3.14159$ and $e \approx 2.71828$. It is denoted as $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the Normal Distribution as a **"bell-curve ruler"** used by nature. Imagine you are measuring the **heights of 10,000 college students**:

- Most students will have a height **near the average** (say, 165 cm) — this is the **tall middle part of the bell**.
- A few students will be **slightly taller or shorter** than average — these form the **sloping sides**.
- **Very few** students will be **extremely tall (200 cm) or extremely short (140 cm)** — these are the **thin tails** of the bell.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** The Normal Distribution describes phenomena where small random variations accumulate around an average value. That is why it appears everywhere in biology — blood pressure, birth weight, IQ, enzyme activity, drug response times, and measurement errors all follow a roughly Normal shape.

### 1.3 Why Normal Distribution is the "Backbone" of Biostatistics

- The **Central Limit Theorem (CLT)** states that the sampling distribution of the mean of *any* population (regardless of its shape) tends toward Normal as the sample size grows. This makes the Normal distribution the foundation of **hypothesis testing** and **confidence intervals**.
- Many biological measurements are approximately Normal due to the **additive effect of many small genetic + environmental factors**.

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Notation Used in KTU Boards:**
> - $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ → General (raw) Normal variable
> - $Z \sim N(0, 1)$ → **Standard Normal Variable** (μ = 0, σ = 1)
> - $z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ → **Z-transformation (Standardization)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Anatomy of the Normal Curve

The Normal PDF $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{x-\mu}{\sigma}\right)^{2}}$ is composed of **three critical components**:

1. **The Constant $\dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}$** — This is the **normalizing factor**. Its sole job is to ensure the total area under the curve equals **1** (since probabilities must sum to 1). A larger $\sigma$ makes this constant smaller, which **flattens and widens** the curve.

2. **The Exponent $-\dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{x-\mu}{\sigma}\right)^{2}$** — This controls the **shape**. The squared term ensures the function is always non-negative (since $e$ raised to a negative power is positive). The square also makes the curve **symmetric** about $\mu$.

3. **The Squared Distance $\left(\dfrac{x-\mu}{\sigma}\right)^{2}$** — Measures how many **standard deviations** $x$ is from the mean $\mu$. Values close to $\mu$ yield exponents near 0, giving $e^0 = 1$ (the peak). Values far from $\mu$ yield very negative exponents, making $f(x) \to 0$ (the tails).

### 2.2 The Seven Classical Properties of the Normal Distribution

These properties are **favourite KTU theory questions** (often asked for 3 to 7 marks):

1. **Bell-shaped and Symmetric** about the vertical line $x = \mu$.
2. **Mean = Median = Mode = $\mu$** (all three measures of central tendency coincide).
3. **Total Area = 1** (it is a valid probability distribution).
4. **Asymptotic to the x-axis** — the curve never touches the axis, but approaches it as $x \to \pm\infty$.
5. **Unimodal** — it has exactly one peak (at $x = \mu$).
6. **Inflection Points** occur at $x = \mu \pm \sigma$ (where the curve changes from concave-down to concave-up).
7. **Empirical Rule (68-95-99.7 Rule):**
   - **68.27%** of data lies within **1σ** of the mean.
   - **95.45%** of data lies within **2σ** of the mean.
   - **99.73%** of data lies within **3σ** of the mean.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — every KTU board exam draws from these formulas.**

| **Concept** | **Formula / Expression** | **Notes / Conditions** |
|---|---|---|
| General Normal PDF | $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{x-\mu}{\sigma}\right)^{2}}$ | $-\infty < x < \infty$ |
| Notation | $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ | Mean $\mu$, Variance $\sigma^2$ |
| Mean | $E(X) = \mu$ | First moment about origin |
| Variance | $Var(X) = \sigma^2$ | Second central moment |
| Standard Deviation | $SD(X) = \sigma$ | Square root of variance |
| Median | $Median = \mu$ | Equal to mean |
| Mode | $Mode = \mu$ | Peak of the curve |
| Skewness | $\gamma_1 = 0$ | Symmetric (no skew) |
| Kurtosis | $\gamma_2 = 3$ | Mesokurtic (standard peakedness) |
| Standard Normal PDF | $\phi(z) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{-\frac{z^2}{2}}$ | $Z \sim N(0,1)$ |
| Z-Transformation | $z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Converts $X$ to $Z$ |
| Linear Transformation | If $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$, then $aX + b \sim N(a\mu + b, a^2 \sigma^2)$ | Property of linear combinations |
| Sum of Normals | If $X_1 \sim N(\mu_1, \sigma_1^2)$ and $X_2 \sim N(\mu_2, \sigma_2^2)$ independent, then $X_1 + X_2 \sim N(\mu_1 + \mu_2, \sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2)$ | Used in CLT problems |
| Empirical Rule 1σ | $P(\mu - \sigma < X < \mu + \sigma) \approx 0.6827$ | 68.27% area |
| Empirical Rule 2σ | $P(\mu - 2\sigma < X < \mu + 2\sigma) \approx 0.9545$ | 95.45% area |
| Empirical Rule 3σ | $P(\mu - 3\sigma < X < \mu + 3\sigma) \approx 0.9973$ | 99.73% area |
| Cumulative Probability | $P(X \leq x) = \Phi\left(\dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}\right)$ | Read from Standard Normal table |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse the **variance** ($\sigma^2$) parameter with the **standard deviation** ($\sigma$) when writing $N(\mu, \sigma^2)$. Always remember — **the second parameter of the Normal distribution is the VARIANCE, not the standard deviation.**

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Biostatistics & Engineering

- **Clinical Trials:** Modelling patient response times to a drug, baseline blood pressure readings, and cholesterol levels.
- **Quality Control (Engineering):** Monitoring the diameter of manufactured pills, the tensile strength of biomedical implants.
- **Genetic Studies:** Phenotypic traits (height, weight) often follow a Normal distribution due to polygenic inheritance.
- **Measurement Error Analysis:** Any physical or chemical measurement is modelled as *True Value + Random Normal Error*.
- **Hypothesis Testing Foundation:** z-tests, t-tests, ANOVA, and regression all **assume** or **rely on** Normality.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Mean of the Normal Distribution

**Statement to Prove:** $E(X) = \mu$ for $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$.

**Proof:**

By definition, the expected value of a continuous random variable is:
$$E(X) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x \cdot f(x) \, dx$$

Substituting the Normal PDF:
$$E(X) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x \cdot \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{x-\mu}{\sigma}\right)^{2}} dx$$

Substitute $z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$, which gives $x = \mu + \sigma z$ and $dx = \sigma \, dz$.

When $x \to -\infty$, $z \to -\infty$. When $x \to +\infty$, $z \to +\infty$. The Jacobian term is $\sigma$.

$$E(X) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (\mu + \sigma z) \cdot \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{-\frac{z^2}{2}} \cdot \sigma \, dz$$

Simplify $\sigma$ in numerator and denominator (they cancel):
$$E(X) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (\mu + \sigma z) \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{-\frac{z^2}{2}} dz$$

Split into two integrals:
$$E(X) = \mu \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{-\frac{z^2}{2}} dz \; + \; \sigma \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} z \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{-\frac{z^2}{2}} dz$$

**Evaluate the first integral:** The integrand is the Standard Normal PDF, whose total area is 1.
$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{-\frac{z^2}{2}} dz = 1$$

**Evaluate the second integral:** The function $z \cdot e^{-z^2/2}$ is an **odd function** (because $z$ is odd and $e^{-z^2/2}$ is even). The integral of an odd function over the symmetric interval $(-\infty, \infty)$ is **zero**.
$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} z \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{-\frac{z^2}{2}} dz = 0$$

Therefore:
$$E(X) = \mu(1) + \sigma(0) = \mu$$

$$\boxed{E(X) = \mu} \qquad \blacksquare$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Variance of the Normal Distribution

**Statement to Prove:** $Var(X) = \sigma^2$ for $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$.

**Proof:**

By definition:
$$Var(X) = E\left[(X - \mu)^2\right] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (x - \mu)^2 \cdot f(x) \, dx$$

Substituting the PDF:
$$Var(X) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (x - \mu)^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{x-\mu}{\sigma}\right)^{2}} dx$$

Apply the same substitution $z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$, giving $x - \mu = \sigma z$ and $dx = \sigma \, dz$.

$$Var(X) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (\sigma z)^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{-\frac{z^2}{2}} \cdot \sigma \, dz$$

Simplify — $(\sigma z)^2 = \sigma^2 z^2$, and the two $\sigma$ factors in the denominator and Jacobian cancel to give one $\sigma$:

$$Var(X) = \sigma^2 \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} z^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{-\frac{z^2}{2}} dz$$

The remaining integral is $E(Z^2)$ where $Z \sim N(0,1)$. We split $z^2$ using the identity $z^2 = z \cdot z$:
$$E(Z^2) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} z \cdot \left( z \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{-\frac{z^2}{2}} \right) dz$$

Recognize that the bracketed term is related to the derivative of the standard normal PDF. Specifically:
$$\frac{d}{dz}\left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{-\frac{z^2}{2}}\right] = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \cdot (-z) \, e^{-\frac{z^2}{2}} = -z \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{-\frac{z^2}{2}}$$

Therefore: $z \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{-z^2/2} = -\dfrac{d}{dz}\left[\phi(z)\right]$

Substituting back:
$$E(Z^2) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} z \cdot \left(-\frac{d\phi}{dz}\right) dz = -\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} z \, \phi'(z) \, dz$$

Use integration by parts: $\int u \, dv = uv - \int v \, du$, with $u = z$ and $dv = \phi'(z) \, dz$ (so $v = \phi(z)$ and $du = dz$):

$$E(Z^2) = -\left[ z \cdot \phi(z) \Big|_{-\infty}^{\infty} - \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \phi(z) \, dz \right]$$

**Boundary term:** As $z \to \pm\infty$, $\phi(z) \to 0$ exponentially fast, so $z \cdot \phi(z) \to 0$ at both limits.
$$z \cdot \phi(z) \Big|_{-\infty}^{\infty} = 0 - 0 = 0$$

**Second term:** $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \phi(z) \, dz = 1$ (total area of standard normal).

$$E(Z^2) = -\left[ 0 - 1 \right] = 1$$

Substituting back:
$$Var(X) = \sigma^2 \cdot 1 = \sigma^2$$

$$\boxed{Var(X) = \sigma^2} \qquad \blacksquare$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Examples

#### **Example 1: Finding Probability using Standardization** `[KTU Model: 7-mark standard problem]`

**Problem:** The systolic blood pressure (SBP) of adult males in a city follows a Normal distribution with mean $\mu = 120$ mmHg and standard deviation $\sigma = 10$ mmHg. Find the probability that a randomly selected male has SBP between 110 and 130 mmHg.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify the parameters.**
$\mu = 120$, $\sigma = 10$. We need $P(110 < X < 130)$.

**Step 2: Standardize the bounds using $z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$.**

$$z_1 = \frac{110 - 120}{10} = \frac{-10}{10} = -1.00$$

$$z_2 = \frac{130 - 120}{10} = \frac{10}{10} = +1.00$$

**Step 3: Rewrite the probability in terms of $Z$.**
$$P(110 < X < 130) = P(-1.00 < Z < +1.00)$$

**Step 4: Use the symmetry of the standard Normal distribution.**

By symmetry: $P(-1 < Z < 1) = P(0 < Z < 1) + P(-1 < Z < 0) = 2 \cdot P(0 < Z < 1)$

**Step 5: Read from the standard Normal table (Z-table).**
$\Phi(1.00) = 0.8413$, therefore $P(0 < Z < 1) = 0.8413 - 0.5000 = 0.3413$

$$P(-1 < Z < 1) = 2 \times 0.3413 = 0.6826$$

$$\boxed{P(110 < X < 130) \approx 0.6827 \text{ (or 68.27\%)}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Note:** This result matches the **Empirical Rule** — exactly 68.27% of observations lie within $1\sigma$ of the mean. This is a classic KTU verification question.

---

#### **Example 2: Finding a Cutoff Value (Inverse Problem)** `[KTU Model: 7-mark inverse problem]`

**Problem:** The scores in a medical entrance exam follow $N(500, 100^2)$. The top 5% of candidates are called for an interview. Find the minimum score required to be called.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify what we need.**
We need the value $x_0$ such that $P(X > x_0) = 0.05$ (top 5% in the right tail).

**Step 2: Convert to Z.**
$P(X > x_0) = P\left(Z > \dfrac{x_0 - 500}{100}\right) = 0.05$

**Step 3: Find the critical z-value from the Z-table.**
We need $P(Z > z_0) = 0.05$, which means $\Phi(z_0) = 1 - 0.05 = 0.9500$.

From the standard Normal table, $\Phi(1.645) = 0.9500$, so $z_0 = 1.645$.

**Step 4: Convert back to $x_0$.**
$$z_0 = \frac{x_0 - \mu}{\sigma} \implies 1.645 = \frac{x_0 - 500}{100}$$

$$x_0 - 500 = 1.645 \times 100 = 164.5$$

$$x_0 = 500 + 164.5 = 664.5$$

$$\boxed{\text{Minimum score required} = 664.5}$$

---

#### **Example 3: Linear Combination of Independent Normals** `[KTU Model: Application of property]`

**Problem:** Two independent machines fill medicine bottles. Machine A dispenses $X_1 \sim N(100, 4)$ ml. Machine B dispenses $X_2 \sim N(150, 9)$ ml. Find the distribution of the total volume $Y = X_1 + X_2$ and $P(Y > 248)$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Apply the sum property.**
For independent Normals, the sum is also Normal with:
$$\mu_Y = \mu_1 + \mu_2 = 100 + 150 = 250$$
$$\sigma_Y^2 = \sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 = 4 + 9 = 13$$
$$\sigma_Y = \sqrt{13} \approx 3.606$$

So $Y \sim N(250, 13)$.

**Step 2: Find $P(Y > 248)$.**
$$z = \frac{248 - 250}{\sqrt{13}} = \frac{-2}{3.606} \approx -0.555$$

$$P(Y > 248) = P(Z > -0.555) = \Phi(0.555) \approx 0.7108$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** Always square the standard deviations when adding variances. A common student error is to add $\sigma$ values directly, e.g., $\sqrt{4} + \sqrt{9} = 5$, instead of $\sqrt{4 + 9} = \sqrt{13}$.

$$\boxed{P(Y > 248) \approx 0.7108 \text{ (or 71.08\%)}}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of the Normal Distribution

The following Mermaid **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** maps the conceptual structure of the Normal Distribution — from raw measurement $X$ to standardized variable $Z$, and onward to probability lookup. This is the **process model** that every KTU probability problem follows.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Measurement X] --> B[Identify Parameters: mu and sigma squared]
    B --> C{Is the Distribution Standard Normal?}
    C -- Yes, mu=0 and sigma=1 --> D[Use Z directly: Z ~ N 0,1]
    C -- No, general Normal --> E[Apply Z Transformation]
    E --> F[Compute z equals x minus mu divided by sigma]
    F --> G[Standardized Variable Z]
    G --> H[Read Probability from Z table]
    D --> H
    H --> I{Type of Probability?}
    I -- Between a and b --> J[Compute Phi of b minus Phi of a]
    I -- Greater than c --> K[Compute 1 minus Phi of c]
    I -- Less than d --> L[Compute Phi of d]
    J --> M[Final Probability Output]
    K --> M
    L --> M
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** Every KTU Normal Distribution problem flows through this pipeline. First identify if you are dealing with a **standard** Normal (μ = 0, σ = 1) or a **general** Normal. If general, **standardize** first, then look up probabilities from the Z-table.

### 4.2 The Empirical Rule Visual Schema

The following diagram represents the **68-95-99.7 Rule** in block format, showing how probability mass is distributed around the mean in the Normal curve.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TAIL3L[Left Tail 3 sigma: 0.135%]
        A1[Beyond 3 sigma]
    end
    subgraph BAND3[Band 2 sigma to 3 sigma: 2.275%]
        A2[Between 2 and 3 sigma]
    end
    subgraph BAND2[Band 1 sigma to 2 sigma: 13.59%]
        A3[Between 1 and 2 sigma]
    end
    subgraph BAND1[Central Band 1 sigma: 34.13% each side]
        A4[Between 0 and 1 sigma]
    end
    subgraph CORE[Peak at mu: 0.3989 density]
        A5[Mean equals Median equals Mode]
    end
    CORE --- BAND1
    BAND1 --- BAND2
    BAND2 --- BAND3
    BAND3 --- TAIL3L
    BAND1 --- BAND1R[Right side 34.13%]
    BAND1R --- BAND2R[Band 13.59%]
    BAND2R --- BAND3R[Band 2.275%]
    BAND3R --- TAIL3R[Right Tail 0.135%]
```

### 4.3 Z-Transformation Pipeline (Process Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1: Identify X] --> P2[Step 2: Note mu and sigma]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Compute z equals x minus mu over sigma]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Look up Phi of z in Standard Normal Table]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Apply Complement Rule if needed]
    P5 --> P6[Final Probability Answer]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The Z-table typically provides the area from $0$ to $z$ (i.e., $P(0 \leq Z \leq z)$). For negative $z$, use the **symmetry property**: $P(-z < Z < 0) = P(0 < Z < z)$. For $P(Z > z)$, use $1 - \Phi(z)$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the Normal Distribution. State any four of its important properties.**

**Model Answer:**

The Normal Distribution is a continuous probability distribution defined by the probability density function:
$$f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{x-\mu}{\sigma}\right)^{2}}, \quad -\infty < x < \infty$$

It is denoted as $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$, where $\mu$ is the mean and $\sigma^2$ is the variance.

**Four Important Properties:**

1. The curve is **bell-shaped and perfectly symmetric** about the mean $\mu$.
2. **Mean = Median = Mode = $\mu$** (all three coincide).
3. The total area under the curve is **equal to 1**.
4. The curve is **asymptotic** to the x-axis — it approaches but never touches the axis.
5. The **empirical rule** holds: ~68% of data lies within 1σ, ~95% within 2σ, and ~99.7% within 3σ of the mean.

*(Any four properties accepted for full 3 marks.)*

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is a Standard Normal Distribution? Write down its probability density function.**
