## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define Analysis of Variance (ANOVA). State the null and alternative hypotheses tested in a one-way ANOVA.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** ANOVA is a statistical technique developed by **R.A. Fisher** used to test whether the means of **three or more independent groups** are significantly different by partitioning the total variability in the data into *between-group* and *within-group* components.
- **Hypotheses (1 mark):**
  - $H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2 = \mu_3 = \dots = \mu_k$ (all population means are equal)
  - $H_1$: At least one population mean $\mu_i$ differs from the rest.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*State the fundamental identity of ANOVA. What is the test statistic used and what is its distribution under $H_0$?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Identity (1.5 marks):** $SST = SSB + SSW$, where SST, SSB, and SSW are the Total, Between-group, and Within-group sums of squares respectively.
- **Test Statistic (1.5 marks):** The **F-statistic** is used, defined as $F = \dfrac{MSB}{MSW} = \dfrac{SSB/(k-1)}{SSW/(N-k)}$. Under $H_0$, $F$ follows the **Snedecor's F-distribution** with $(k-1, N-k)$ degrees of freedom.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. You are required to answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**A medical researcher wants to compare the effectiveness of 3 different diets (Low-Carb, Mediterranean, Intermittent Fasting) on weight loss. Each diet is prescribed to 6 patients. The weight loss (in kg) after 8 weeks is recorded:**

| Patient | Low-Carb (A) | Mediterranean (B) | Intermittent Fasting (C) |
|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|
| 1 | 8.2 | 6.5 | 5.1 |
| 2 | 7.8 | 7.0 | 4.8 |
| 3 | 9.0 | 6.8 | 5.5 |
| 4 | 8.5 | 7.2 | 4.5 |
| 5 | 7.5 | 6.3 | 5.0 |
| 6 | 8.0 | 6.9 | 4.6 |

**Test at the 5% level of significance whether the three diets produce significantly different mean weight losses. Use ANOVA.**

#### **(a) Formulate the hypotheses, compute the grand mean, and find SSB and SSW. (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Hypotheses.**
$$H_0: \mu_A = \mu_B = \mu_C \quad \text{vs.} \quad H_1: \text{At least one mean differs}$$

**Step 2 — Group Totals and Group Means.**

$T_A = 8.2 + 7.8 + 9.0 + 8.5 + 7.5 + 8.0 = 49.0 \implies \bar{x}_A = 49.0/6 = 8.1667$

$T_B = 6.5 + 7.0 + 6.8 + 7.2 + 6.3 + 6.9 = 40.7 \implies \bar{x}_B = 40.7/6 = 6.7833$

$T_C = 5.1 + 4.8 + 5.5 + 4.5 + 5.0 + 4.6 = 29.5 \implies \bar{x}_C = 29.5/6 = 4.9167$

**Step 3 — Grand Mean and Grand Total.**

$T = 49.0 + 40.7 + 29.5 = 119.2$

$N = 18 \implies \bar{\bar{x}} = 119.2 / 18 = 6.6222$

**Step 4 — Correction Factor (CF).**

$$CF = \frac{T^2}{N} = \frac{(119.2)^2}{18} = \frac{14208.64}{18} = 789.3689$$

**Step 5 — Raw Sum of Squares (Total).**

$$
\sum\sum x_{ij}^2 = (8.2^2+7.8^2+9.0^2+8.5^2+7.5^2+8.0^2) + (6.5^2+7.0^2+6.8^2+7.2^2+6.3^2+6.9^2) + (5.1^2+4.8^2+5.5^2+4.5^2+5.0^2+4.6^2)
$$

Diet A: $67.24 + 60.84 + 81.00 + 72.25 + 56.25 + 64.00 = 401.58$

Diet B: $42.25 + 49.00 + 46.24 + 51.84 + 39.69 + 47.61 = 276.63$

Diet C: $26.01 + 23.04 + 30.25 + 20.25 + 25.00 + 21.16 = 145.71$

$\sum\sum x_{ij}^2 = 401.58 + 276.63 + 145.71 = 823.92$

**Step 6 — SSB Computation.**

$$
SSB = \frac{T_A^2}{6} + \frac{T_B^2}{6} + \frac{T_C^2}{6} - CF
$$

$$
SSB = \frac{2401.00}{6} + \frac{1656.49}{6} + \frac{870.25}{6} - 789.3689
$$

$$
SSB = 400.1667 + 276.0817 + 145.0417 - 789.3689 = 821.2901 - 789.3689 = 31.9212
$$

**Step 7 — SSW Computation.**

$$
SSW = \sum\sum x_{ij}^2 - \left(\frac{T_A^2}{6} + \frac{T_B^2}{6} + \frac{T_C^2}{6}\right) = 823.92 - 821.2901 = 2.6299
$$

**Step 8 — SST Verification.**

$$SST = \sum\sum x_{ij}^2 - CF = 823.92 - 789.3689 = 34.5511$$

$$SST \stackrel{?}{=} SSB + SSW \implies 34.5511 = 31.9212 + 2.6299 = 34.5511 \quad \checkmark$$

#### **(b) Construct the ANOVA table, compute the F-statistic, and draw your conclusion at $\alpha = 0.05$. (7 marks)**

**Step 9 — Degrees of Freedom.**

$df_B = k - 1 = 3 - 1 = 2$

$df_W = N - k = 18 - 3 = 15$

$df_T = N - 1 = 18 - 1 = 17$

**Step 10 — Mean Squares.**

$MSB = \dfrac{SSB}{df_B} = \dfrac{31.9212}{2} = 15.9606$

$MSW = \dfrac{SSW}{df_W} = \dfrac{2.6299}{15} = 0.1753$

**Step 11 — F-Statistic.**

$$F_{calc} = \frac{MSB}{MSW} = \frac{15.9606}{0.1753} = 91.05$$

**Step 12 — Critical Value.**
From F-tables: $F_{0.05,\,2,\,15} = 3.68$.

**Step 13 — ANOVA Summary Table.**

| Source | SS | df | MS | F-ratio |
|:--|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|
| Between Diets | 31.9212 | 2 | 15.9606 | **91.05** |
| Within Diets (Error) | 2.6299 | 15 | 0.1753 | — |
| **Total** | 34.5511 | 17 | — | — |

**Step 14 — Decision and Conclusion.**

Since $F_{calc} = 91.05 \gg F_{crit} = 3.68$, we **reject $H_0$** at the 5% significance level.

> **Conclusion:** There is *very strong* statistical evidence that the mean weight loss differs significantly between at least two of the three diets. The diets are not equally effective.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**A botanist applies 4 different growth hormones to plants. Each hormone is applied to 5 plants. The plant heights (in cm) after one month are:**

| Plant | H1 | H2 | H3 | H4 |
|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|
| 1 | 12 | 15 | 10 | 18 |
| 2 | 14 | 16 | 11 | 19 |
| 3 | 13 | 14 | 9 | 20 |
| 4 | 15 | 17 | 12 | 17 |
| 5 | 11 | 13 | 10 | 21 |

**Perform a one-way ANOVA and test at $\alpha = 0.01$ whether the mean plant heights differ significantly across the four hormones.**

#### **(a) Compute the corrections factor, SSB, and SSW. State the hypotheses clearly. (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Hypotheses.**

$H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2 = \mu_3 = \mu_4$ (all hormones have equal mean effect)

$H_1$: At least one $\mu_i$ differs.

**Step 2 — Group Totals and Means.**

$T_1 = 12+14+13+15+11 = 65 \implies \bar{x}_1 = 13.0$

$T_2 = 15+16+14+17+13 = 75 \implies \bar{x}_2 = 15.0$

$T_3 = 10+11+9+12+10 = 52 \implies \bar{x}_3 = 10.4$

$T_4 = 18+19+20+17+21 = 95 \implies \bar{x}_4 = 19.0$

**Step 3 — Grand Total and Grand Mean.**

$T = 65 + 75 + 52 + 95 = 287, \quad N = 20$

$\bar{\bar{x}} = 287 / 20 = 14.35$

**Step 4 — Correction Factor.**

$$CF = \frac{T^2}{N} = \frac{287^2}{20} = \frac{82369}{20} = 4118.45$$

**Step 5 — Raw Sum of Squares.**

H1: $144 + 196 + 169 + 225 + 121 = 855$

H2: $225 + 256 + 196 + 289 + 169 = 1135$

H3: $100 + 121 + 81 + 144 + 100 = 546$

H4: $324 + 361 + 400 + 289 + 441 = 1815$

$\sum\sum x_{ij}^2 = 855 + 1135 + 546 + 1815 = 4351$

**Step 6 — SSB Computation.**

$$
SSB = \frac{65^2}{5} + \frac{75^2}{5} + \frac{52^2}{5} + \frac{95^2}{5} - CF
$$

$$
SSB = \frac{4225}{5} + \frac{5625}{5} + \frac{2704}{5} + \frac{9025}{5} - 4118.45
$$

$$
SSB = 845.0 + 1125.0 + 540.8 + 1805.0 - 4118.45 = 4315.8 - 4118.45 = 197.35
$$

**Step 7 — SSW Computation.**

$$
SSW = \sum\sum x_{ij}^2 - 4315.8 = 4351 - 4315.8 = 35.20
$$

**Step 8 — SST Verification.**

$SST = 4351 - 4118.45 = 232.55$

$SSB + SSW = 197.35 + 35.20 = 232.55 \quad \checkmark$

#### **(b) Construct the ANOVA table, compute F, and conclude at $\alpha = 0.01$. (7 marks)**

**Step 9 — Degrees of Freedom.**

$df_B = k - 1 = 3, \quad df_W = N - k = 16, \quad df_T = 19$

**Step 10 — Mean Squares.**

$MSB = \dfrac{197.35}{3} = 65.7833$

$MSW = \dfrac{35.20}{16} = 2.2000$

**Step 11 — F-Statistic.**

$$F_{calc} = \frac{65.7833}{2.2000} = 29.90$$

**Step 12 — Critical Value.**
At $\alpha = 0.01$, from F-tables: $F_{0.01,\,3,\,16} = 5.29$.

**Step 13 — ANOVA Summary Table.**

| Source | SS | df | MS | F-ratio |
|:--|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|
| Between Hormones | 197.35 | 3 | 65.7833 | **29.90** |
| Within Hormones (Error) | 35.20 | 16 | 2.2000 | — |
| **Total** | 232.55 | 19 | — | — |

**Step 14 — Decision and Conclusion.**

Since $F_{calc} = 29.90 \gg F_{crit} = 5.29$, we **reject $H_0$** at the 1% significance level.

> **Conclusion:** There is *overwhelming* statistical evidence that the four growth hormones produce significantly different mean plant heights. At least one hormone is genuinely more (or less) effective than the others.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the CF subtraction:** Students often compute $\sum(T_i^2/n_i)$ and report it as SSB. The CF must always be subtracted to get the *deviation* sum of squares.
> 2. **Arithmetic errors in $\bar{\bar{x}}$:** Using the *average of group means* instead of the *total/N*. These are equal only when all $n_i$ are equal.
> 3. **Wrong df for F-critical:** The numerator df is $k-1$ (between), denominator is $N-k$ (within). Reversing these gives an *incorrect* critical value.
> 4. **Failing to verify the SS identity:** Always check $SST = SSB + SSW$ to catch arithmetic errors early.
> 5. **Confusing SSW formula variants:** The direct (deviation) form $SSW = \sum\sum(x_{ij}-\bar{x}_i)^2$ and the shortcut (raw SS) form $SSW = \sum\sum x_{ij}^2 - \sum(T_i^2/n_i)$ give identical results; use whichever is faster.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4: ANOVA**

- **ANOVA** = partitioning total variance into **between-group (SSB)** and **within-group (SSW)** components.
- **Why not multiple t-tests?** Each additional t-test inflates the family-wise error rate; ANOVA controls it at a single $\alpha$.
- **F-statistic** = $MSB / MSW$ follows the **F-distribution** with $(k-1, N-k)$ degrees of freedom under $H_0$.
- **Decision rule:** Reject $H_0$ if $F_{calc} > F_{\alpha,\,k-1,\,N-k}$.
- **Master identity:** $SST = SSB + SSW$ with corresponding $df_T = df_B + df_W$.
- **Shortcut formulas** use the *Correction Factor* $CF = T^2/N$ to avoid computing deviations from means.
- **Assumptions:** Normality, Homoscedasticity (equal variances), and Independence.
- **Assumption Testing (advanced):** Use **Bartlett's test** or **Levene's test** for homogeneity of variance; **Shapiro-Wilk** or **Q-Q plot** for normality.
- **Post-Hoc Tests** (if $H_0$ is rejected): Use **Tukey's HSD**, **Bonferroni**, or **Scheffé's test** to identify *which* specific groups differ.
- **ANOVA is robust:** Mild violations of normality are tolerable, especially for balanced designs (equal $n_i$).
- **Connection to t-test:** When $k = 2$, $F = t^2$ exactly. This is the *mathematical bridge* between two-sample and multisample inference.
- **Pooled variance estimator:** $\hat{\sigma}^2 = MSW$ is the best (minimum variance unbiased) estimator of the common population variance $\sigma^2$ under $H_0$.
- **Always report:** Sample sizes, group means, F-statistic, df, and p-value in any ANOVA result.
- **Cautions:** Outliers heavily distort $MSW$ (since it's in the denominator); consider **robust ANOVA** (e.g., Welch's ANOVA) when variances are unequal.
- **One-way vs Two-way:** Module 4 covers **one-way ANOVA** (single factor). Two-way ANOVA with interaction is a higher-order extension in advanced biostatistics.
